- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com>`

```
> Avionics computers were not programmed in Cobol. There
> was no danger on that front, nor from elevators nor electric
> utilities.

I think that this illustrates your shortfall in knowledge on Y2K. You
seem to think, rather naievely, that all Y2K problems were related to
Cobol.  In fact you have claimed that PC and Unix did not have the same
problem.

It may well be that you were only given work that related to Cobol for
the Y2k projects.  As you are a Cobol programmer then they probably
wouldn't give you other work, or even inform you about what else was
happening.

Y2K problems were spread across most systems, I gave the example where
Windows had some problems generating correct dates.

> There was no danger on that front

Your opinion is of no consequence.  The managers of airports, traffic
control and aviation software did not want _any_ risk of planes falling
out of the sky.  They considered it best to spend the money to test the
systems fully rather than wait and see if any problems occured and face
the consequences.

The cost of this testing of non-Cobol systems is included in the
billions applied to Y2K.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Aircraft safety

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T04:41:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> 
> Your opinion is of no consequence.  The managers of airports, traffic
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Completely off the topic you were discussing with your erstwhile friend, 
but your above comment triggered what I was watching on TV last night.

PBS 'Frontline' excellent low-key factual reporting, no histrionics, 
with a comforting voice-over from a narrator, covered the Swiss Air 111 
(McDonnell Douglas MD-11) disaster where the plane crashed into the sea 
off Canada's East Coast at Peggy's Cove in 1998. Crew and passengers 
killed - 250.

It was chilling to watch. Both Canadian and US Aviation authorities, the 
latter also being responsible for setting international standards have 
been investigating for years. And it took *years* for the Canadians to 
retrieve the wreckage dispersed in mini-pieces, some no more than a 
bracket or about three to four inches of wire. As best they could they 
reassembled the frame of the aircraft, but still left with a warehouse 
full of boxes of different parts.

First, fairly common with most large commercial aircraft - some 150 
miles of electrical wiring. Like it or not the wiring deteriorates, 
movement, vibration, age etc. So you get bared wires. Any condensation 
kicking about and it will arc. Now all this wiring just isn't bare, it's 
covered with insulation - don't know what it is - but think of something 
like a lining on a bedspread which in turn is covered with a silvery 
surface - you techies get what I'm trying to describe. Of course the 
wiring and insulation is set by aviation standards.

To summarize - they the Canadians/Americans have reasonably concluded 
this is what happened. Somewhere in the plane, and in overhead areas not 
so particular - who tries to crawl between the outer skin and the cabin 
internal roof - an arc occurred in some wiring, and that approved 
insulation material burned like a paper tissue used to wipe your nose !

The flight recorder box picked up messages from the aircrew when they 
indicated an emergency wanting to land at Halifax, subsequently turned 
to "Immediately". It would appear the cockpit was the prime area 
enveloped in smoke and soot. Instruments went and literally the pilot 
and co-pilot just had no idea of which way was up. Possibly with a tip 
of a wing, the plane nose-dived into the sea.

Back to that insulation. Teams tested and proved that it just wasn't 
safe, (with scenes of their test burns on TV), and made recommendations 
it should be replaced. Think of the cost - all the commercial fleets 
using that specific material. In an aviation hearing, the 'commercials' 
  managed to get an extension not to actually have to implement the 
change until June 2005.

So my wife says, 'You stand more chance of being killed crossing the 
road, rather than dying in an air crash'. Hmmm. Perhaps when I visit my 
daughter back East I should consider a pony and trap - would take a bit 
longer though.

I confirmed the aircraft make from googling - so anybody out there 
interested in aviation - seems to be quite a bit on Swiss Air 111.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aircraft safety

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T06:25:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q5yVd.140005$JF2.1156@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no...
> Richard wrote:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Reminds me of a great movie scene:

Narrator: A new car built by my company leaves somewhere traveling at 60 
mph. The rear differential locks up. The car crashes and burns with everyone 
trapped inside. Now, should we initiate a recall? Take the number of 
vehicles in the field, A, multiply by the probable rate of failure, B, 
multiply by the average out-of-court settlement, C. A times B times C equals 
X. If X is less than the cost of a recall, we don't do one.

Business Lady on Plane: Are there a lot of these kinds of accidents?

Narrator: You wouldn't believe.

Business woman on plane: Which car company do you work for?

Narrator: A major one.


There are many twists to this:

First there is the new Dodge model (that I approve of) which sends out a 
letter - by the way, we do have a minor problem with suspension in the rear 
of your truck that affects high mileage vehicles. Rather than do a recall 
we're extending the warranty.

Second there is the VW model (and this is MY personal story that I share so 
you can do the same)

Me: They say the heater core on my VW needs replacing.  The car has less 
than 35,000 miles.  Can you why I have to pay $800 of labor for something so 
soon in the cars life ?
Customer Service: Hmmm....let me see. [wait] So you say the heater core is 
the problem. What model car?
Me: It's the Jetta that you put glue together in Mexico.  This is really 
pathetic, I'm going to sell it and never buy anything from you again.
Customer Service: Have your dealer call us, we'll take care of the labor 
charge.

Two things I learnt -
1) They only recall using the equations in Fight Club.  They have a status 
that is "watch list" which means it's not on recall, but they are aware of 
problems.  This is why they paid the labor. ALWAYS call your dealer before 
major work :-D
2) Dealers will hate you because the manufacturers do not pay the same labor 
rate you and I would :-)


JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aircraft safety

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T12:14:40-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<42275410.A08ACFD0@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no> <Q5yVd.140005$JF2.1156@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
jce wrote:
> 

> 
> Narrator: A new car built by my company leaves somewhere traveling at 60
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


There is good reason to believe that exactly this scenario happens all
over the place.  You may recall a train crash in England - near London -
a few years ago where one train t-boned another - apparently because the
controlling signal light did not turn red when it was set to go red.  It
came out afterwards that a calculation had been done comparing the cost
of keeping all the signals and control systems fully repaired and up to
snuff to the cost of compensating victims & families.  On an annual
basis, it was cheaper to compensate, given the anticpated number of
accidents.  So: maintenance got neglected.  People died.  The train
company came out ahead.

"Don't be nervous, Arthur Dent: be very, very afraid ... Arthur Dent!"

PL
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<42275410.A08ACFD0@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no> <Q5yVd.140005$JF2.1156@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <42275410.A08ACFD0@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey wrote:
> jce wrote:
> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

To my mind, where there is a calamity involving the death of just ONE 
human being - then the cost to remedy that is immaterial. Just think of 
the two examples so far. Assume one of the aviation experts involved had 
lost his young daughter as a passenger on Swiss Air 111. Or the train 
accident (approaching Paddington wasn't it ?). A British Rail official 
lost his daughter in that crash.

We accept there is a loss of life in war. Just like bombers were 
vulnerable during WW2, no matter what fighter coverage was provided. But 
then think of that young reservist who confronted Donald Rumsfeld, "Our 
vehicles (Humvee ?) are only soft-skinned". Rummy waffled on about cost 
and implementation of an upgrade. Meanwhile back there in Baghdad - 
there are still young Americans getting blown up in some vehicles, which 
are soft targets.

To relieve auto congestion as Calgary has grown, and just one of many 
locations, we have a major route McLeod Trail, (named after the mountie 
colonel who back in 1873, first arrived at Fort McLeod south of us near 
the border). McLeod Trail is a north to south road. I approach it on 
Canyon Meadows on a east west route. So Canyon Meadows is now 'bridged' 
over McLeod with 'off' ramps. So I turn right to go north. My wife made 
the observation that there was no railing/fence to protect anybody 
sliding off, primarily during ice conditions, and sliding down the 
grassy bank on to McLeod, roughly 30 feet below - and of course smack 
bang into traffic heading north on McLeod.

I agree with her observation. Ironically if we are traveling south on 
McLeod, and turn off right to pick up the left-angle turn to go east on 
Canyon Meadows there *IS* a fence. Bearing in mind you are slowing down 
when exiting, there is absolutely no danger of you hitting the fence - 
you have to wait at traffic lights before you can turn left and east 
anyway.

First she phoned the city Engineering department. No reaction. Then a 
year later an alderwoman, who pursued it with the city. Still no 
reaction. That is UNTIL the first time there is a fatality. Then watch 
my wife start hollering - and if we get the name of the family involved 
we will prompt them to sue the pants off the city !

Costs and humanity - what about those poor buggers in Darfur - we've 
seen shots of them suffering. Now it is no longer newsworthy - so they 
suffer in silence.

Jimmy
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:OjJVd.564541$8l.533130@pd7tw1no...
> Peter Lacey wrote:
>> jce wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> lost his daughter in that crash.
Unfortunately you cannot personalize the issue.

For interesting discussion:
<start link=========
http://www.crookedtimber.org/archives/000414.html

  Basic Trolley Case: There is a run away trolley car careening down some 
railway tracks towards two tunnels. If it stays on the track it is on, it 
will kill five people working in the eastern tunnel. If you pull a switch it 
will move onto a side track, go through the western tunnel and kill the 
person working in it. Assume you can't do anything to stop the car or change 
its path except pull this switch. You pull the switch, saving the five and 
leading to the one's death.

  Fatman: Same runaway car, but without the switch. This time, you're 
standing on a cliff looking over the track the car is about to careen down. 
If unchecked it will kill the five people in the tunnel. Fortunately, there 
is a very fat man standing beside you - fat enough that if somehow he were 
to fall onto the track his sheer mass would stop the trolley. You give him a 
little shove, he falls off the cliff onto the train tracks is killed by the 
trolley (if not the fall) and the five are saved.

A significant number of people think your action in the first case is 
permissible, perhaps even mandatory, but the action in the second case is 
impermissible.

===========end link>

The fact is that there are estimated to be 6,422,192,930 people in the world 
and if we were to ask the US or Europe to spend just ONE dollar per person 
per day helping all of them there would be outcry.  Maybe you're unusual but 
your statement that is "calamity involving the death of just ONE human 
being - then the cost to remedy that is immaterial".  The value of life is 
infinite at its source and dimishes exponentially as you move 
outwards....that's why we get headlines like:  Tsunami hits- 200 000 die, 
supermodel hurt.

If I said cut off your finger and your family will remain safe, you'd do it. 
If I said cut off your finger and I will prevent the massacre of millions in 
Rwanda, you might, but I know you'd think about it even though if the value 
of ONE life is as important as you say it should be millions times easier to 
make the second decision.

I don't refuse you your right to be self righteous in the matter :-) It's 
easier to say "I value all life" than appear cold and say "yeah, whatever, I 
feel sorry for the families but means nothing to me".

JCE
```

```
jce wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
> news:OjJVd.564541$8l.533130@pd7tw1no...
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Unfortunately you cannot personalize the issue.

I tend to disagree with you on that one. In the real world I think the 
aviation or railway official is going to take it very personally - and 
have grievous doubts about his previous position, based on a dollar and 
cents evaluation. Be honest - ask yourself the same question. But before 
you answer it, your family unit and love of same have to have significance.
> 
> For interesting discussion:
> <start link=========
> http://www.crookedtimber.org/archives/000414.html
<snip>

Yes understand what you are illustrating. It's a trick the UK military 
use, and probably also your own, when evaluating candidates as potential 
officer cadets. The test is a 'What if....".

Scenario - eight people in a lifeboat, famous actress, brilliant 
scientific professor, Mom and little kid, an internationally renowned 
entrepreneur etc..... You get the idea.

Only enough food to feed seven out of the eight. Which one do you toss 
overboard. Having got you all to blab on your choice, then they zing 
you. Now chuck # 2 overboard. (Perhaps says one, "If I'd know you wanted 
us to throw two overboard, I would have picked a pair. The mother and 
the kid " :-) ). Seriously, depending on how they evaluate these tests, 
they might acknowledge that candidate's flip remark as a 'positive'.

How effective that particular technique is I don't know - but the intent 
  is valid in a military environment where you are looking for 'war 
leaders/warriors', who can dispassionately make cool judgments. I read 
just last week, "Having made decisions like destroying the French Fleet 
etc., Churchill agonized over some of his decisions"

> 
> ===========end link>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> per day helping all of them there would be outcry. 

Funnily enough, last night on current series, or perhaps re-runs on 
Mondays and Tuesday, the fictional 'West Wing' made that point. 
President Bartlett :-

Nobody gives a toss about foreign policy, just how taxes affect them in 
their pocket book, relating to expense here at home. But we should be 
concerned.

Perhaps I relate to that because Prez Martin Sheen and I are both liberals.


  Maybe you're unusual but

I do know that I'm neither brilliant nor a rocket scientist. But I don't 
think I'm 'unusual' - got to be some that think along the same lines.

> your statement that is "calamity involving the death of just ONE human 
> being - then the cost to remedy that is immaterial".  The value of life is 
> infinite at its source and dimishes exponentially as you move 
> outwards....that's why we get headlines like:  Tsunami hits- 200 000 die, 
> supermodel hurt.

Plus of course the TV networks having swamped us in their own version of 
a Tsunami - it isn't newsworthy anymore. Even with the backing of George 
Bush Senr. and Bill Clinton, I'd bet the relief effort will just fade away.
> 
> If I said cut off your finger and your family will remain safe, you'd do it. 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> feel sorry for the families but means nothing to me".

"Self righteous' - kind of a zinger. When you break it down the word 
'righteous' on its own doesn't indicate a stiff-neck. Howz about 'Just 
somebody who cares ?"

There's a tremendous amount of self-interest in our Western culture. We 
nod awkwardly, acknowledging the Third World is a problem, but don't do 
anything about it. History has its own way of shaping itself, and some 
things come back to bite you. Out of self-interest the coastal European 
nations became empire-builders. Real big problem now - Africans, Arabs 
and Asians in droves becoming major groups in some countries. UK for 
example, since the post WW2 emigration, is now going through a second 
phase of "Let's get out of here and go to Australia and New Zealand". 
Others head for N. America. That's the same situation in the Netherlands.

Coming over here - long term, that's like jumping out of the frying pan 
into the fire. It's not just one guy called Osama, (fairly common name), 
who looks at N. America with a jaundiced eye.

.... Now back to my Treeview - and comment on your observations about 
components......

Jimmy
```

Old joke:


The pilot says the plane will crash unless they can reduce its weight some more.
  All the seats have been thrown over, and all that's left is people.   Not
enough.    An Englishman says "God save the Queen" and jumps out.   Not enough.
 A Frenchman doesn't want the Englishman to show him up and jumps, saying "Vive
la France".   Still need 200 pounds removed.    A big Texan steps up to the
door, shouts "Remember the Alamo", and pushes out two Mexicans.
```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:viNVd.566239$Xk.440055@pd7tw3no...
---and I snipped some---
> jce wrote:
>> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> significance.

This is one of the issues that is faced by any national health service. 
There is a save at all cost mentality when in reality you need a brave 
person at the top who says, "this is my budget, and this is what I'm going 
to do with it".  Is it worth spending $1 million dollars to save an 
extremely premature baby? To the parents it is, but to the thousands of 
people waiting in line for crowns, hip replacements, eye surgery it isn't. 
It's also why I don't apply for that job - I wouldn't want to tell anyone 
that it was too bad, we have a funding crisis.
From a logical standpoint you cannot personalize the issue - because you're 
human we tend to - all of us to different degrees.  I think I'm more on the 
uncaring side, you on the caring.  Neither of us is wrong, and should we 
have a real personal attachment to someone then we really are affected.  My 
gran dying means nothing to you, and something more to me.

>> For interesting discussion:
>> <start link=========
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>> ===========end link>
The particular example was more interesting due in part that it took two 
very similar incidents and the responses are different based on the "active" 
role.

>> The fact is that there are estimated to be 6,422,192,930 people in the 
>> world and if we were to ask the US or Europe to spend just ONE dollar per 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> liberals.
Prez Martin Sheen is no doubt upset by his son's divorce.

>  Maybe you're unusual but
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> away.
Will, or has? In Florida we're already talking about hurricanes and the 
relief we will need.

>> If I said cut off your finger and your family will remain safe, you'd do 
>> it. If I said cut off your finger and I will prevent the massacre of 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> somebody who cares ?"
I can go with that.  It wasn't meant in a nasty way, but  sometimes I wonder 
if people have the brass to say, you know what, people die :-)  Maybe that's 
why the US has HMO's..takes _all_ the caring out of health care...come to 
that, much of the 'health' to.  These days you go to the doctor once 
something is really bad.

> There's a tremendous amount of self-interest in our Western culture. We 
> nod awkwardly, acknowledging the Third World is a problem, but don't do 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Nice reply though.  Self righteous is not the right word (I hedged on that 
when I wrote it) - I think sincere to a fault might work - and I don't think 
many would claim that was too terrible a fault :-)

JCE
```

- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no> <Q5yVd.140005$JF2.1156@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <42275410.A08ACFD0@mb.sympatico.ca> <OjJVd.564541$8l.533130@pd7tw1no> <_BKVd.140064$JF2.71798@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <viNVd.566239$Xk.440055@pd7tw3no> <6VOVd.141369$JF2.84834@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
jce wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 

I hate the messages which keep rolling on through previous answers. So 
just taking your point about health care. Subtle, but slightly different 
case.

The one you addressed was the cost of having health care. Has been for a 
long time and probably will remain a contentious issue. If a health 
administrator puts priorities on how he spends his budget, yes, without 
direct intervention, he is aware somebody is going to go short - and 
possibly die. He gets a fixed amount of money for the pie, so he and 
advisers, have to decide how the pie is going to be served as slices.

That's a little bit different to the plane/train examples. I'm 
reasonably certain the latter is now 'privatized' in UK and doesn't go 
by the old name I used - British Rail.

They are in the profit making business, else why exist. New topic for 
industry - ethics. Some have taken a jump at it with sincerity; probably 
majority are still giving it the look over, perhaps not to be followed 
by an serious intent. As the saying goes, "Power corrupts...." but howz 
about "Money corrupts much more quickly ". Even in 'uncomfortable mode' 
and making a decision to avoid spending on safety, having made that 
shaky step froward, is it really that far removed, gradually stepping, 
to arrive at the ultimate temptation which is big time - Bernie Ebbers 
at Worldcom and Kenneth Lay at Enron. (BTW Bernie is an Albertan from 
Edmonton ).

The other issue with health care, that you made passing reference to, 
how you handle the case of a premature birth. This one probably 
generates more heat than the funding of health care. Your own stance, 
religious beliefs, or other beliefs, that construct your own moral code. 
In brief, my thoughts are, only prolong life where you can also give 
somebody 'quality of life'.

There are lots of old folk shuffled off to hospices/homes - to rot, lose 
their marbles and eventually just fade away. Not my choice - as they 
wheel me in on a gurney, tip the bloody thing over so that I croak !

Better still, if still healthy or semi-healthy leave me at home to do my 
own thing, wrapped in the comfort of the memories associated with my own 
home and garden. Then someday my son phones to check on the old guy. No 
reply. Only half-concerned but comes around to check. The old guy has 
gone. Oh how I hate the obits that are worded, "Our beloved...... PASSED 
AWAY......".

The last scenario is absolutely false - from time to time I have to 
remind my wife that we have an unwritten agreement. I worded it, of 
course. "I'm going FIRST. There is no goddamn way I am going to spend 6 
months clearing all her junk out of our basement !"

Jimmy
```

The particular flavor of socialized medicine in the U.S. is that we spend more
money in keeping someone 80 years old alive, than getting health care to
infants.   Places where the state makes such decisions, the 80 year old usually
is on the bottom of the priority list.
```

Truly that's how they put it across. Bearing in mind I'm thinking 
1950-ish, it's quite possible they saw the black and white movie you are 
alluding to. I think I watched about five minutes of it about a year 
back - not my cup of tea/coffee.

Was Hitch the director on that one. I don't think it was Marlene 
Dirttrack - but wasn't there another oldie siren in the movie ?

BTW - one of the other tests. "Here's a few pieces of string, a plank 
and a couple of bits of whatever. See this tree, this is on one bank and 
that tree over there is on the other bank. And this bit in between is a 
raging African river teeming with crocodiles. Right young man, get your 
team of six across without any drownings or deaths".

Methinks to self. "What a load of cobblers. I don't like heights anyway 
(tree climbing). Piss on it. Let some other clown show he can be a team 
leader !"

Not the true competitive spirit, old boy.

Jimmy

```

On  3-Mar-2005, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

> "Self righteous' - kind of a zinger. When you break it down the word
> 'righteous' on its own doesn't indicate a stiff-neck. Howz about 'Just
> somebody who cares ?"

I suppose "righteous" is when God tells you what to be do, and "self righteous"
is when you figure it out for yourself.    Except I would think it's a good
thing to figure it out for yourself, and "self righteous" is always an insult.
 I'm moving towards the direction that "righteous" is the worst insult though,
as we find more righteous people strapping explosives to their bodies and
heading out to the market to punish the unrighteous.

When you're righteous, then you can commit any atrocity, and the victims deserve
it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Aircraft safety

- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no> <Q5yVd.140005$JF2.1156@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <42275410.A08ACFD0@mb.sympatico.ca> <OjJVd.564541$8l.533130@pd7tw1no> <_BKVd.140064$JF2.71798@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <viNVd.566239$Xk.440055@pd7tw3no> <d09t8i$nje$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

> "self righteous" is always an insult.

In organised religions you are not supposed to 'figure it out for
yourself', because that is the way of independent thought and action,
and then they no longer 'own' you.

Science teaches you _how_ to think, religion teaches you _what_ to
think.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no> <Q5yVd.140005$JF2.1156@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```

On  2-Mar-2005, "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

> Narrator: A new car built by my company leaves somewhere traveling at 60
> mph. The rear differential locks up. The car crashes and burns with everyone
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> X. If X is less than the cost of a recall, we don't do one.

To some extent, we have to accept this type of valuation.   How much is a life
worth?   Is it worth enough to have a world-wide speed limit of 20mph, and as
soon as a kid gets run over, lower it to 5mph?    Vehicles and laws can *always*
be made safer.    And programming design and testing procedures can always be
made more thorough.

The variable is when we have done enough.   The consensus (which is always a
compromise) varies over time.    It also varies depending on what we can afford.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Aircraft safety

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** Jussi Jumppanen <jussij@zeusedit.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T12:03:20+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4227B3D8.1A3B@zeusedit.com>`
- **References:** `<tcra11dep0bl1lkb9el57jreju1utc14u0@4ax.com> <0rhv11l119nv0h7qkv4jtsc6sldkebkkd2@4ax.com> <cvpr9p$4jt$1@panix5.panix.com> <cjb421loslu5s3t9r5psik0749oppc44ki@4ax.com> <cvv9kv$p2d$1@panix5.panix.com> <3dv9219cr52rd6s32psbctluihf1kio839@4ax.com> <fYoVd.124191$qB6.58353@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <c9kc21d2t4me4qbuvpsliic44dah6kv5k8@4ax.com> <1109820747.633971.9120@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <6AwVd.559064$Xk.100857@pd7tw3no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> The flight recorder box picked up messages from the aircrew when 
> they indicated an emergency wanting to land at Halifax, subsequently 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> with a tip of a wing, the plane nose-dived into the sea.

I have seen a similar documentary on this crash but they told a 
slightly different story.

The stories match as to the reason for the crash (ie electrical
fire made worse by the fact that flammable insulation was also 
used in the fuselage), but it was not the smoke and the lack of 
visibility that brought the plane down.

The reason for the crash was the fire started as you describe, the 
crew detect the smoke and the captain, who liked to fly by the book
immediately went into the aircrew emergency checklist procedures. 

Some 19 minutes later, the fire that was being fed by the outside 
air rushing over the fuselage, was now so intense it destroyed all
remaining controls lines, hence forcing the plane to crash.

The tradgedy is that had the aircrew reacted a little faster they may 
well have been able to land the plane before the controls failed. In 
fact recall that the documentary said one of the recommendations from 
the crash investigation was the emergency checklist procedures should 
be changed to effectively read "if there is any hint of fire land the 
plane ASAP".

Here are a couple of links about the crash:

  http://www.ldc.upenn.edu/Projects/TDT3/topic.research/topic3016.html
  http://members.aol.com/papcecst/Flight111.html

Jussi Jumppanen
Author of: Zeus for Windows (New version 3.94 out now)
"The C/C++, Cobol, Java, HTML, Python, PHP, Perl programmer's editor"
Home Page: http://www.zeusedit.com
```

"Forgive, but tie up your camel."   There's also a saying from this
continent that runs something like "Insanity is doing the same thing over
and over again, expecting different results."

On Mon, 28 Feb 2005 05:17:42 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Robert,
>   One more time (you do seem unable to understand why what you say is "held 
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>"essence" has nothing to do with what you stated.

Because you insist:

The '02 Cobol Standard specifies syntax different from IBM and Micro
Focus extensions. If IBM or Micro Focus drop support for the extension
syntax and insist on conformance with the '02 Standard, existing
programs would have to be changed by adding a BASED clause to
structure headers. No procedural changes would be required.

IBM users *might* want to switch to the newly available ALLOCATE/FREE
verbs

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cvibef$127f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <2dbq1198emt1p5r3827lfgskmhl90m1bn3@4ax.com> <cvl8bl$30qv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <s1ru11ppgmu71f7hbdooebl8kdekc0f6dk@4ax.com> <IoLTd.3384325$f47.608417@news.easynews.com> <5qb121ps51qunkgqvadpjl1s2m0p7u9pdf@4ax.com> <kn3Ud.3468980$f47.622899@news.easynews.com> <5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com>`

```
So you agree that by YOUR DEFINITION, the code that you provided does NOT 
qualify as "general", right?

Wouldn't it have helped your credibility (as at least I see it) if you admitted 
this when it was first pointed out to you?

FYI-
  My personal definition of a "general" solution is one that:

A) conforms to a current (or past) generally implemented Standard (i.e that 
currently supported compilers conforming to the specified Standard exist on 3 or 
more operating systems)

   and

B) the solution does not rely on "operating system specific" API's, "calls" or 
other non-portable non-COBOL features.
```

```
On Tue, 01 Mar 2005 00:35:09 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>So you agree that by YOUR DEFINITION, the code that you provided does NOT 
>qualify as "general", right?

No.

>Wouldn't it have helped your credibility (as at least I see it) if you admitted 
>this when it was first pointed out to you?

No. 

>FYI-
>  My personal definition of a "general" solution is one that:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>other non-portable non-COBOL features.

Your definition is unreasonable TODAY because it requires compliance
with a Standard that hasn't been implemented. My definition of
"general" is:

A) Works without modification on the vast majority of commercial
compilers available today.

and

B) The solution does not rely on OS APIs. It uses platform-independent
memory management functions widely available in ANSI-standard
languages -- Cobol or C.

and

C) Is supported by the current (unimplemented) Cobol Standard with a
very minor syntactic difference. It is unlikely that any change will
be required because compiler vendors will probably preserve backward
compatibility.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW refuses to admit his error(was: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T04:18:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<s1ru11ppgmu71f7hbdooebl8kdekc0f6dk@4ax.com> <IoLTd.3384325$f47.608417@news.easynews.com> <5qb121ps51qunkgqvadpjl1s2m0p7u9pdf@4ax.com> <kn3Ud.3468980$f47.622899@news.easynews.com> <5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com>`

```
OK,
  Whether you like it or not, you have CHANGED your definition.  Your *NEW*

"C) Is supported by the current (unimplemented) Cobol Standard with a
very minor syntactic difference. It is unlikely that any change will
be required because compiler vendors will probably preserve backward
compatibility."

is quite different from your original definition.  If you can't see the 
difference, then you are simply blind.

NOTE:
  I (personally) know of know compiler that provides
    "Flag syntax that has "major" (non-minor) syntactic differences from the/a 
Standard"

Therefore, it will be difficult to "code" general programs that meet your 
definition but will (eventually) be used on other compilers.  However, at least 
you have now come up with a definition that your code matches.

P.S.  Your statement

"Your definition is unreasonable TODAY because it requires compliance with a 
Standard that hasn't been implemented."

is wrong (and you probably will have difficulty admitting this too).  My 
statement was

"A) conforms to a current (or past) generally implemented Standard (i.e that 
currently supported compilers conforming to the specified Standard exist on 3 or 
more operating systems)

which very DEFINITELY does NOT rely on a non-implemented Standard.  In fact, it 
"rejects" syntax that is ONLY supported in a Standard that has NOT been 
implemented in at least 3 operating systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW refuses to admit his error(was: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T07:29:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5qb121ps51qunkgqvadpjl1s2m0p7u9pdf@4ax.com> <kn3Ud.3468980$f47.622899@news.easynews.com> <5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Wed, 02 Mar 2005 04:18:17 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Your definition is unreasonable TODAY because it requires compliance with a 
>Standard that hasn't been implemented."
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>implemented in at least 3 operating systems.

IBM, Micro Focus and Fujitsu each, individually, run on three or more
operating systems and share a common  pointer syntax. The '85 Cobol
Standard does not support pointers, so those compilers are
disqualified by your A).

What we have is a disconnect between the Standards process and
reality. The '02 Standard acts like pointers are a new idea when, in
fact, they've existed for many years. 

Memory allocation has nothing whatever to do with "bit twiddling", as
you asserted in another message. The only things they have in common
are being low level and confusing managers who 'always had trouble
with logic'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW refuses to admit his error(was: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T17:29:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PPHVd.3954470$f47.703134@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5qb121ps51qunkgqvadpjl1s2m0p7u9pdf@4ax.com> <kn3Ud.3468980$f47.622899@news.easynews.com> <5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com>`

```
Huh???

Source code that DOES comply with the '85 Standard *will* compile with IBM, 
Micro Focus, and Fujitsu.  If a program is limited to that source code (which 
can be detected by using each of their '85 Standard flagging mechanism), then 
that source code would fit my definition of "general". It is certainly TRUE that 
this eliminates the possibility of using POINTER solutions - but that is why I 
(along with several - but not all - of the CLC responders) would say that YOUR 
solution (or ANY POINTER solution) does not qualify as "general".

There are, however, a number of ways of solving the original (and other) tasks 
with '85 Standard conforming source code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW refuses to admit his error(was: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T05:20:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rkbWd.295780$Zm5.39290@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com> <PPHVd.3954470$f47.703134@news.easynews.com> <cb2i219npbv3eh1jnenj7ja769lqp69oc2@4ax.com>`

```
I have NEVER argued against pointers.  (see below for recap of my "bad" 
comments)

What I have (and continue) to say is that *IF* your definition of a "general 
solution" to a problem is that it MUST conform to a current Standard *AND* 
compile on 90% of compilers, then any POINTER solution fails this test.  Once 
you admitted that your original description of "general" (in your words) was in 
ERROR and that what you REALLY meant was that "with minimal changes it conforms 
to a current Standard" (regardless of whether or not THAT Standard is actually 
implemented by anyone - much less many vendors - then I say your code meets your 
NEW definition.

NOTE:
  Again on my comment on "bad".

A) I think that IBM customers do not want to have to pay for development of new 
"BASED" syntax (and - by the way - there are also new rules concerning 
addressability for BASED items in Working-Storage, Local-Storage, vs Linkage 
Section)  when IBM customers find the current syntax FINE for their NON_portable 
code.
   and
B) Most IBM shops that I know of (or at least new of) did not want application 
programmers doing dynamic storage manipulation.  This was left for "more senior" 
programmers and specific "system level" code.

Personally, I think that the ALLOCATE and FREE statements are a reasonable 
enhancement to the COBOL Language and the fact that BASED items can be in a 
variety of sections of the Data Division provides for added functionality. 
However, I don't know of any vendor that has implemented this feature of the '02 
Standard yet.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110002083.070383.115860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<rkbWd.295780$Zm5.39290@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com> <PPHVd.3954470$f47.703134@news.easynews.com> <cb2i219npbv3eh1jnenj7ja769lqp69oc2@4ax.com> <rkbWd.295780$Zm5.39290@news.easynews.com>`

```
> *IF* your definition of a "general solution" to a problem

The stupid thing is that it wasn't even a 'solution'. It didn't solve
the problem at all, the code just read the file into a pointer list.
It didn't isolate the lines that were to be shifted or move them or do
anything that would count as a specific solution, let alone a general
one.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW refuses to admit his error(was: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T20:37:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<614k21tca9vk957taffspkof441nrl2hhf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com> <PPHVd.3954470$f47.703134@news.easynews.com> <cb2i219npbv3eh1jnenj7ja769lqp69oc2@4ax.com> <rkbWd.295780$Zm5.39290@news.easynews.com> <1110002083.070383.115860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 4 Mar 2005 21:54:43 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> *IF* your definition of a "general solution" to a problem
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>one.

I wrote:

1. Read the file into a linked list.
2. Re-link the last two lines into their new location.
3. Write the list to an output file.

That implied letting Cobol parse lines from a LINE SEQUENTIAL file,
copying the records/lines to a list, then copying them to the output
file.

On second thought, and in light of the 'Bible challenge', don't bother
copying them. It would be faster to suck in the file with a single
read, parse lines yourself (with a SEARCH verb) and make a list of
pointers to the lines in-situ. On the output side, you'd have at most
five writes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW refuses to admit his error(was: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T13:46:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110059189.909998.102990@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<614k21tca9vk957taffspkof441nrl2hhf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com> <PPHVd.3954470$f47.703134@news.easynews.com> <cb2i219npbv3eh1jnenj7ja769lqp69oc2@4ax.com> <rkbWd.295780$Zm5.39290@news.easynews.com> <1110002083.070383.115860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <614k21tca9vk957taffspkof441nrl2hhf@4ax.com>`

```
>  I wrote:
> 1. Read the file into a linked list.
> 2. Re-link the last two lines into their new location.
> 3. Write the list to an output file.

I input this to both MF and Fujitsu and neither compiled it cleanly.
It also doesn't seem to match either the '85 standard or the '02
standard.

> It would be faster to suck in the file with a single
> read, parse lines yourself (with a SEARCH verb) and make a list of
> pointers to the lines in-situ. On the output side, you'd have at most
> five writes.

That is certainly how assembler programmers in the 60s could have done
it.  Maybe even C programmers in the 70s.

Cobol is supposed to be a high level language, and above that sort of
bit-twiddling.
```

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T03:19:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r8ok21d27022c1q49q3ugfbi6grbk79kg8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com> <PPHVd.3954470$f47.703134@news.easynews.com> <cb2i219npbv3eh1jnenj7ja769lqp69oc2@4ax.com> <rkbWd.295780$Zm5.39290@news.easynews.com> <1110002083.070383.115860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <614k21tca9vk957taffspkof441nrl2hhf@4ax.com> <1110059189.909998.102990@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
On 5 Mar 2005 13:46:29 -0800, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> It would be faster to suck in the file with a single
>> read, parse lines yourself (with a SEARCH verb) and make a list of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>bit-twiddling.

How many Cobol programmers does it take to change a light bulb?
Five. One to handle the bulb, three to say how much better they can do
it, and one to reminisce about how Grace would have done it.
Three. One to change the bulb and two to code-review the change.

What do you call a contract Cobol programmer with a beeper and a
cellphone?
An optimist.

What's another definition of optimist?
A Cobol programmer with a thirty year mortgage.

What's the difference between a dead Cobol programmer in the road and
a dead squirrel in the road?
Skid marks before the squirrel.

What do you call someone who hangs out with programmers?
That Cobol guy.

What do Cobol programmers use for birth control?
Their personalities.

If you drop an RPG programmer and a Cobol programmer off a twenty
story building, which lands first?
Who cares?
The RPG programmer. The Cobol programmer has to stop midway to look up
directions in a manual.

What's the difference between God and a Cobol programmer?
God knows he's not a Cobol programmer.

A Cobol programmer, a C++ programmer, a world-class Java programmer
and a PERL scripter are standing on four corners of a field. Someone
drops a hundred dollar bill in the center. Who gets it?
The C++ programmer because:
  A Cobol programmer won't go anywhere for only a hundred dollars.
  There's no such thing as a world-class Java progrmmer.
  The PERL guy can't figure out what's going on.
```

>Personally, I think that the ALLOCATE and FREE statements are a reasonable

ALLOCATE/FREE. BASED structures could be in WORKING-STORAGE or
(non-standard) BASED-STORAGE SECTION.

- **References:** `<5qb121ps51qunkgqvadpjl1s2m0p7u9pdf@4ax.com> <kn3Ud.3468980$f47.622899@news.easynews.com> <5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Robert,

I have no interest in the topic or J4 - but that seems to me an oddball 
way of referencing something. To the unknowing you make it sound like 
F/J, IBM and M/F are "Us" and that the 02 Standard (J4) is 'Them'. The 
reality is that Us = Them.

F/J was represented by Artur Reimann for the longest time until he was 
taken ill in 2000. I've no idea when F/J joined J4. Then subsequent to 
Artur's departure, Thane Hubbell represented F/J until they opted out, 
some two years back.

IBM - I'm guessing at least a 10 year stint or more from Ann 
Bennett/Wallace and was also Covenor for WG4(ISO).

M/F - again probably a 10 year or more stint from Don Schricker who is 
now the chairman for both J4 and WG4.

Apart from F/J they are still at it with Chuck for Unisys, Jeff for HP, 
plus retired Don Nelson who was HP.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RW refuses to admit his error(was: moving data from one place to another in a text file

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T03:40:07+00:00

- **References:** `<kn3Ud.3468980$f47.622899@news.easynews.com> <5n9221t2843q5pqh1aroijpvr2c9i94fiu@4ax.com> <I9cUd.3506126$f47.627807@news.easynews.com> <adt421la4994qrhabldhqosj3s28j10t81@4ax.com> <WPxUd.3593762$f47.643349@news.easynews.com> <gpa721l9dcvcukbrjma47hnhs2k7ukm0q9@4ax.com> <1NOUd.3386046$B07.531319@news.easynews.com> <k92a21p89hlh449bgdshqsud8ooo71ektb@4ax.com> <d8bVd.2207$Zm5.119@news.easynews.com> <53cd219eiqpd07dmsnpvv6k6sa8j3j3goq@4ax.com> <iSHVd.564242$8l.95358@pd7tw1no> <3o2i21l4dnbd1c9qkb4aoh9prsu10tk299@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3o2i21l4dnbd1c9qkb4aoh9prsu10tk299@4ax.com...

> Why are Hitachi,
> Fujitsu, LegacyJ, Acucorp and Kobol not represented?

That's a question only the management of Fujitsu, LegacyJ, and Kobol to
answer;  unless senior management at those companies is actively
participating in this forum, anything that might appear here as to their
motives is, and of necessity must be, sheer speculation unless someone has
certain knowledge.

Hitachi has actively supported ITSCJ participation in J4; the primary ITSCJ
representative on J4 is an employee of that company.  ITSCJ fairly recently
became an advisory member; the primary (sponsored by Hitachi) and alternate
(sponsored by Tokyo International University) representatives have expressed
hope to return ITSCJ to active voting status this year.

Acucorp is, and has been for a long time, an advisory member of J4.

The committee encourages, and has continued to encourage, users, vendors,
onsultants, academicians and anybody else with an interest in the future of
COBOL to participate in the standards process, and has done so for my entire
tenure on that committee.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

The only message I've seen with "bit twiddling" and "allocate" from Bill was

B) items that existing IBM mainframe experience have indicated that most

ALLOCATE
and FREE statements and "bit twiddling").

thread is accurate or not:

RW refuses to admit his error - does he have an error to admit too?
YES or NO

% of respondents with a margin of error of y %" and maybe some of us would 
be satisfied.  It's not worth it because this is better than Counterpoint on 
TV.

JCE

programs...thanks for clearing that up.

JCE

## Thread: Treeviews — 400 this month / 524 total

_Thread started [2005-02](2005-02.md#t-ade484509a)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T00:21:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38j1h3F5ocqh7U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <cvo5k2$mas$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109446377.120385.263750@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <cvvlgu$838$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cvvlgu$838$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 26-Feb-2005, "Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> This was the logic that seduced MicroSoft.   Allowing all programs access
to all
> tools gives tremendous power to the user.   It also gives tremendous power
to
> the hacker, and allow bugs tremendous footprints.
>
> Integrated systems are powerful.   And dangerous.    As with all choices,
there
> are tradeoffs.
>
This is a reasonable post and seeks to stike a reasoned balance.

Nevertheless, I disagree strongly with it.

I don't believe MicroSoft were seduced by anything (other than the desire to
make obscene amounts of money), I don't believe having a common interface
made life easier for hackers( that was a result of the specific software and
what it had to do), and, in my personal experience, a generalised (or
abstracted) solution has ALWAYS been superior to a specific one-off
solution.

I have had exactly this argument with managers (who want it NOW) and
programmers  (who want an easy life) , right throughout my programming
career, since I first started writing live applications in the mid 60s.

I have NEVER found it "much hard to generalise" a solution, and doing so has
ALWAYS been a great asset.

Extending a solution to its abstract conclusion has always paid off in not
having to revisit  it anything like as often as its one-off counterpart, not
having to keep amending it as processes change,and being able to implement
it with minor modifications into similar applications in the same company or
elsewhere.

One of the reasons I took to OO like a diuck to water is because it
encourages this abstraction and generalisation.

Give me a comprehensive solution over a specific one any day.

(If it takes a bit longer to develop or requires more thought and
investigation, I don't care. It is worth waiting for. Hurried solutions are
half arsed solutions that require constant attention and cost more in the
long run than doing it properly in the first place.)

Do it once; do it right.

Pete.
```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d01qae$2he$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <38j1h3F5ocqh7U1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Lol! I know what you are saying, Doc, having encountered exactly this on
numerous occasions.

Nevertheless, if professional programmers insisted on doing things properly
and refused to be pushed into 'band-aid' solutions, there would be no option
for management but to comply.

There are occasions when a band-aid can successfully be applied (the scratch
is trivial and is unlikely to need revisiting...) but putting band-aids on
gaping wounds that should be stitched, or in cases where a system limb is
hanging off...or needs amputation, is just asking for more trouble later.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

In article <38kpu2F5q3kc4U1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d01qae$2he$1@panix5.panix.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>for management but to comply.

Really, Mr Dashwood... if professional programmers had wheels would they 
be trolley-cars?  An alternate conclusion might be 'if professional 
programmers insisted on doing things properly and refused to be pushed 
into 'band-aid' solutions, there would be no option for management but to 
hire amateurs'.

>
>There are occasions when a band-aid can successfully be applied (the scratch
>is trivial and is unlikely to need revisiting...) but putting band-aids on
>gaping wounds that should be stitched, or in cases where a system limb is
>hanging off...or needs amputation, is just asking for more trouble later.

And if that 'later' comes after a particular 'manager' has been promoted 
or moved to another assignment - after demonstrating value to executives 
by showing, among other things, cost savings - then the problem is, at 
times, seen as 'someone else's'.

DD
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<idgf219pichotq83046gtehfrghr820dl4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <2n8a21pfgjptafulue2578e1kop2oel5gc@4ax.com> <d04cgc$65b$1@panix5.panix.com> <d07cko$6fq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d07d7b$t4m$1@panix5.panix.com> <d07fbi$7to$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Thu, 3 Mar 2005 16:49:35 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:


>I also see Mr. Wagner arguing simultaneously for tougher and more lax standards.
>  Which really isn't the conflict it appears to be, as all standards don't fit
>the same problem.

I want laxer standards for programming style; tougher standards for
management's quality control. Along the line, techies decided the way
to reduce errors is to tighten down on programming standards and
impose Change Control. Make everyone program in the style of the '70s
and review changes. That's not right. 

The right way is to compare results against business requirements. If
it works, don't sweat the details.
```

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d05s59$4ho$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <d01qae$2he$1@panix5.panix.com> <2n8a21pfgjptafulue2578e1kop2oel5gc@4ax.com> <Z_oVd.98002$pc5.15438@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
In article <Z_oVd.98002$pc5.15438@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>,
jce <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:
>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
>news:2n8a21pfgjptafulue2578e1kop2oel5gc@4ax.com...

[snip]

>> They won't respond truthfully. You'll learn the answers after you've
>> been on the job two weeks. At this stage they're looking for questions
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>only _ON_ the plan, so my need to know is limited.

You get web access?  More than one shop I've consulted with - the most 
recent being in November of 2000 - did not allow 
consultants/contractors/hired guns web access.  This lead to an 
interchange of:

'Damnation... funky error here.  Where's the SYNCSORT manual?'

'We don't need manuals here, they're all out on the web.'

'I don't have web access, how do I check manuals?'

'Hey, that's why we pay you the Big Bucks.'

'You pay me the Big Bucks to have manuals memorised?  If that's all you 
need it'd be cheaper to buy the manuals and let me go... how do I check 
manuals?'

'Uhhhhhh... use someone else's computer.'

'I'd have to log on to their computer and my ID doesn't have web access.'

'So use their ID.'

'That's against the Security Agreement everyone signs here, someone could 
get fired if they let me do that.'

'Uhhhhh... wait until they go to lunch.'

'What if they eat at their desk?'

'*Somebody's* got to leave their desk and go to lunch!'

'You're right... so each day I should go around asking everyone on the 
floor if they're eating at their desk and if not when they'll be off to 
lunch?'

'Uhhhhhh... something like that, sure.'

'No prob... let's see, there are about a hundred people in this section, 
if it takes me about a minute each that's a hair over an hour and a 
half... to which project ID should I charge that time each day?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T03:03:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<52if2117tg2ol332joj4ktvcmq8fgdld5s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <d01qae$2he$1@panix5.panix.com> <2n8a21pfgjptafulue2578e1kop2oel5gc@4ax.com> <Z_oVd.98002$pc5.15438@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <d05s59$4ho$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 2 Mar 2005 21:18:17 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <Z_oVd.98002$pc5.15438@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>,
>jce <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
>half... to which project ID should I charge that time each day?'

You are what's known as a TROUBLEMAKER. In the world of contracting,
you're expected to click your heels and say Yes Sir. Look to your
Indian colleagues for guidence in meekness.

In 2000, denying Internet access to contractors was semi-common. I
haven't seen it since then. When I needed access to a manual back
then, I went home. I go home for lunch every day anyway. After 2000,
I've been able to access manuals from the office. 

Back then, support people REALLY NEEDED internet access to download
the current versions of device drivers. They got around company
restrictions by installing a modem and calling their personal ISP.
Talk about a security leak! They had a completely unprotected
connection that bypassed the corporate firewall and gave anyone in
internet-land access to the corporate network. In places that groked
security, that would have been grounds for firing. Most companies
didn't understand the risk. They mistakenly thought they were more
secure because support people couldn't waste time with internet porn
or Web browsing. It's another case of technically incompetent
management.

As I said, management gave internet access to everyone after 2000. If
your place doesn't, you're in a time warp.
```

[snip]

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

competent managers as it would be to wish for managerially competent 
technicians.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T21:59:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6e3b2$42292eba$45491f85$22262@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6m9i21hb0sto5csvhs6blknijujuorjone@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <Z_oVd.98002$pc5.15438@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <d05s59$4ho$1@panix5.panix.com> <52if2117tg2ol332joj4ktvcmq8fgdld5s@4ax.com> <d09le6$1m8$1@panix5.panix.com> <6m9i21hb0sto5csvhs6blknijujuorjone@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
>>
>>>On 2 Mar 2005 21:18:17 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> I hate that phrase. It's most often used by conformists.

You know, I was thinking about this kind of stuff earlier today - 
brought on by a discussion on why some folks who run Linux seem to 
change distributions so often.  One of the possible reasons put forth by 
someone was that perhaps these were fad distributions.

These days, at least around here, it seems every second car or so has 
one of those "Support Our Troops" ribbons; some yellow, some with a flag 
design, some with a camouflage design.  A lot of them are being 
displayed sideways, so it's both a ribbon and a fish.  As a 
servicemember, I'm grateful for the support, and I don't at all want to 
sound like I don't appreciate it.  I just wonder - are we all of a 
sudden *that* patriotic?  The cynical part of me wonders if it 
patriotism is becoming a fad, and not something that's rooted in true 
heart belief.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T04:46:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YQaWd.128780$qB6.128402@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <Z_oVd.98002$pc5.15438@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <d05s59$4ho$1@panix5.panix.com> <52if2117tg2ol332joj4ktvcmq8fgdld5s@4ax.com> <d09le6$1m8$1@panix5.panix.com> <6m9i21hb0sto5csvhs6blknijujuorjone@4ax.com> <6e3b2$42292eba$45491f85$22262@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:6e3b2$42292eba$45491f85$22262@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Robert Wagner wrote:
>>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> was that perhaps these were fad distributions.
Why reinvent the wheel when you can have fun repackaging it.  I think people 
do it for fun  Part of the issue that I see is a very unclear upgrade and 
maintenance path.  You know the age old case of wanting 3d support for your 
graphics card - you can either go down the path of downloading, compiling, 
finding dependencies, or you can read which distribution is the newest and 
upgrade.
Some distro's lose popularity as the companies realize they need to become 
commercial.
I think the "fad" is the greatest likelihood...most linux bigots (and they 
are more so than windows bigots) tend to play around a lot, try lots of 
things, and due to this they switch as what they are playing with changes.

 > These days, at least around here, it seems every second car or so has
> one of those "Support Our Troops" ribbons; some yellow, some with a flag 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> something that's rooted in true heart belief.
Deep down you want it to believe it's the cynical part of you, but I think 
you know the answer
Unfortunately, after 9/11. to express any other opinion in the US got you 
the evil looks - it wasn't good enough to abstain from the issue.
The way I see it, America was not made special by 9/11, it just joined the 
rest of the world. Welcome.

I do think that in general, even with the fad, and even those with antiwar 
stickers generally "support the right to survive" of the military. 
Generally speaking people support the troops but they don't even know why. 
People are perpetuating this myth that the military is dying for "You and I" 
when it's clear they aren't - they are dying for something or someone else. 
It's not mine to judge whether that something or someone is/was worth it.

To quote a not so famous crusty band....about a different war...same 
sentiment

"Dead men in the South Atlantic, It's meant to warm our hearts.  They think 
that they died for you and me Oh God, what a farce"

Everyone acts in a manner that is self serving.  No one likes being alone, 
so we do what is necessary to belong.  A fad is a good way to belong...it's 
why there's religion, it's why there are fraternities, it's why there is 
clc.

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-07T14:38:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0iakf$kt6$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <d0i33l$7m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0i524$fop$1@panix5.panix.com> <d0i7et$9vi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <d0i7et$9vi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  7-Mar-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>have more success selling.

Perhaps I was being obscure... again.  What I attempted to convey was:

A says to B 'Everyone acts in a manner that is self-serving.'

B then knows that A, being a subset of 'everyone', is acting in a manner 
that is self-serving.

How does it self-serve A for B to know that A is acting in this manner?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-07T19:44:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0ib2q$buv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <d0i33l$7m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0i524$fop$1@panix5.panix.com> <d0i7et$9vi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0iakf$kt6$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On  7-Mar-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> Perhaps I was being obscure... again.  What I attempted to convey was:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> How does it self-serve A for B to know that A is acting in this manner?

In particular only that A has analyzed his aspects that don't appear at first
glance to be self-serving.   When A does something that appears to be
altruistic, he is gaining satisfaction from it - and knows that.    Whether or
not A gains from telling B this, who knows?   A thinks he does, or he wouldn't
say that.

And in general, that others also derive satisfaction from what they do - even if
they don't know it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-07T15:05:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0ic5f$fv2$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <d0i7et$9vi$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0iakf$kt6$1@panix5.panix.com> <d0ib2q$buv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <d0ib2q$buv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  7-Mar-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>glance to be self-serving.

Quite the reverse, in fact... it seems that A is 'tipping his hand' 
regarding motive.

>When A does something that appears to be
>altruistic, he is gaining satisfaction from it - and knows that.    Whether or
>not A gains from telling B this, who knows?

I don't, among others... an hence my question.

>A thinks he does, or he wouldn't
>say that.

That seems, Mr Brazee, to be assuming the conclusion:

'Since everyone acts in their self-interest A, in revealing that he is 
acting in his self-interest and removing the possibility that he might be 
acting altruistically, thereby depriving himself of the mechanism of 
apparent altruism, which is an advantage, is acting in his self-interest.'

In other words... 'Denying one'sself an advantage is an act of 
self-interest'... which seems absurd.

DD
```

as well.

acting against his own interest.

Pete.

> Kind of hard for Mr Brazee to do that to anyone over the UseNet... but
> it is interesting that so low an opinion should be expressed of him.

My opinion of Howard is a matter of record. I have nothing but respect for
him, though I may diasagree with him on occasion. (And even those are
rare...)

As we were discussing A and B, your attempt to personalise this is seriously
out of order.

Why would you do that?

Is it because I pointed out that there COULD be an occasion where what you
considered absurd, actually wasn't?

Let it go, Doc. It isn't important. My observation was meant to amuse as
much as inform.

> Seems like one is being encouraged to greets 'everyone acts in their own
> self-interest' as the sort of flag waved by a stranger's oleaginous 'trust
> me'.
>

The relationship between an oily "Trust me..." and "Fuck you..." is pretty
well understood on the street... (well, it is on SOME streets...) But
surely, that is a horse of an entirely different colour... have you heard
the one about the green horse?  maybe some other time... :-)

Pete.



> DD
>
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-09T07:34:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0mqg2$7j2$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <3955unF5uj3ejU1@individual.net> <d0ju4c$pd0$1@panix5.panix.com> <3982urF5ulq71U1@individual.net>`

```
In article <3982urF5ulq71U1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d0ju4c$pd0$1@panix5.panix.com...
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why would you do that?

Leaving aside the possibility that 'when someone says 'everyone does (x)' 
or 'you know that when someone says (x) they mean (y)' it is often an 
expression of said someone's saying '*I* do (x)' or 'when *I* do (x) I 
mean (y)'... Mr Brazee was the one who introduced the assertion of 
'Everyone acts in a self-serving manner' and, last I looked, Mr Brazee is 
a subset of 'everyone'...

... but perhaps I get too dry here; if I were any more wry I might be 
pumpernickel.

(note to those for whom English is a learned, rather than native, language 
or to those unfamiliar with baking: 'wry' is being used in the sense of 
'ironically or grimly humorous' (http://www.m-w.com) and is a homonym of 
'rye', a kind of grain used, at times, to make bread-dough; 'pumpernickel' 
is defined (same cite as above) as 'a dark coarse sourdough bread made of 
unbolted rye flour'... proving, once again, that a joke explained is a 
joke lost)

>
>Is it because I pointed out that there COULD be an occasion where what you
>considered absurd, actually wasn't?

No conditional in your assertion (quoted above beginning with 'It disarms 
B...'), Mr Dashwood... my apologies for not supplying it.

>
>Let it go, Doc. It isn't important. My observation was meant to amuse as
>much as inform.

Likewise... but evidently my ryeness was not so well-bred.

(note to those for whom English is a learned... oh, never mind)

DD
```

jce wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:6e3b2$42292eba$45491f85$22262@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> upgrade.

That was a debate over on the White Box Enterprise Linux (WBEL) user's 
list (it's a clone of Red Hat Enterprise Linux), over whether to make 
new CD images every other release (as has been the custom), or just let 
someone run a really long yum transaction once they got it installed. 
The decision was made to do a re-spin this time because the installer 
has much better SATA support.

And, of course, tracking Red Hat's solid commercial product for free is 
what pretty much drew me to WBEL.

> Some distro's lose popularity as the companies realize they need to become 
> commercial.
> I think the "fad" is the greatest likelihood...most linux bigots (and they 
> are more so than windows bigots) tend to play around a lot, try lots of 
> things, and due to this they switch as what they are playing with changes.

And, what some of the other folks said, they use Linux, but they don't 
really see anything to gain from learning the latest installers - 
they've moved on to other things.  (I'm in that crowd as well - I don't 
want to *try* this or that distro, I want to make my app *work* on the 
one I've chosen.)

>  > These days, at least around here, it seems every second car or so has
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> you know the answer

I don't want to believe it, but it's hard not to think about that...

> Unfortunately, after 9/11. to express any other opinion in the US got you 
> the evil looks - it wasn't good enough to abstain from the issue.

If you're here, why would you feel differently?  "We brought this on 
ourselves, so we should just sit back and let these Islamo-fascists nuke 
the Super Bowl - it's our fault anyway..."

> The way I see it, America was not made special by 9/11, it just joined the 
> rest of the world. Welcome.

??

> I do think that in general, even with the fad, and even those with antiwar 
> stickers generally "support the right to survive" of the military. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's not mine to judge whether that something or someone is/was worth it.

It's either an isolationist or a short-sighted person who can't see that 
what we are doing today will help make ours and future generations 
safer, as well as stopping the horrible atrocities committed by the 
leaders we've displaced.  Even if *every* single charge about Guantanamo 
Bay and Abu Ghraib is true, it's just a drop in the bucket compared to 
what these men did to their own citizens.

> To quote a not so famous crusty band....about a different war...same 
> sentiment
> 
> "Dead men in the South Atlantic, It's meant to warm our hearts.  They think 
> that they died for you and me Oh God, what a farce"

And I thought *I* was cynical...
```
