###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

_(thread depth: 47)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T03:22:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NftYd.646309$Xk.156984@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d0tkam$ok$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d09le6$1m8$1@panix5.panix.com> <TnnYd.647614$6l.386412@pd7tw2no> <d0teba$sun$1@panix5.panix.com> <kJrYd.647241$8l.588697@pd7tw1no> <d0tkam$ok$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <kJrYd.647241$8l.588697@pd7tw1no>,
> James J. Gavan <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Nope. My comments are post-mortem. She shuffled off this mortal coil 
some 10 or more years back, subsequent to my father, whose latter years 
she made miserable. None of this box jazz, both just some cremated bits 
of bone, quietly resting somewhere near Porchester Castle.

Well I'm reasonably sure my father is *quietly* resting. Not so sure 
about her. I think she would kick up a stink wherever she is !

But let me give the lady her due. I had a beautiful childhood, she was a 
good and loving mother. Can still visualize her reading to me, 'What the 
Moon Saw' from Hans Christian Andersen - it's when I became a teen 
(non-rebellious) and started thinking for myself that the troubles first 
started for me. My younger brother at 6' 3" had her over here on holiday 
decades ago. He was constantly popping happy pills to keep his act 
together until she returned to Blighty.

Perhaps the most telling comment from her youngest sister from a family 
of eight. "I loved my sister, but I didn't like her".

Sad - but that's how it turned out. (Then I think of Pete losing his Dad 
when he was about 13. And he would have LOVED to have had him around for 
much longer).

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

_(thread depth: 48)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T09:18:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0utnt$qui$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d09le6$1m8$1@panix5.panix.com> <kJrYd.647241$8l.588697@pd7tw1no> <d0tkam$ok$1@panix5.panix.com> <NftYd.646309$Xk.156984@pd7tw3no>`

```
In article <NftYd.646309$Xk.156984@pd7tw3no>,
James J. Gavan <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <kJrYd.647241$8l.588697@pd7tw1no>,
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>of bone, quietly resting somewhere near Porchester Castle.

My error and apologies, Mr Gavan... silly me, I interpreted the 'will 
walk' in your statement of 'My mother and my beloved, both called Eileen, 
(and believe me there the resemblance really ends), will walk into a 
retail outlet...' as indicating that 'walking' was an activity of which 
both participants were capable.

>
>Well I'm reasonably sure my father is *quietly* resting. Not so sure 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>started for me.

It might be, Mr Gavan, that she was the sort of woman who was more at ease 
dealing with children than adults... or proto-adults, as teenagers might 
be seen to be.

Be that as it may... unless one hopes that others suffer post-mortem 
Eternal Torment then 'may she sleep with the angels' may be seen not only 
as 'may she have as sweet an afterlife as she gave sweetness in life to 
others' (ugly construction there... but the intention comes across, I 
hope) but also as a variation of 'requiescat in pacem'... since, in my 
experience, people who are comfortable with themselves don't spend too 
much time causing misery to others it might be concluded that some kinds 
of misery-making behaviors are the result of a lack of self-comfort.

There was a guitarist, years ago... a man named John Fahey who constantly 
danced on the edge of madness and yet in this dancing produced sounds that 
some found would resonate deeply... others found them to be really bad 
news and would leave the room when his 'music' came up... at one point he 
was living in Seattle, Washington, USA as a street-drunk, he'd pawned his 
guitars and spend his time intoxicated and raving.  He was 'discovered' by 
some fans, detoxified and cleaned up and given support... he turned out a 
few more albums and then died.

Upon hearing of his death my first response was 'May he find victory over 
the demons he's spent so much of his life battling'... a bit 
longer-winded, perhaps, but the same sentiment of 'may he sleep with the 
angels'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-13T13:17:04+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39hf44F624bjeU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d09le6$1m8$1@panix5.panix.com> <TnnYd.647614$6l.386412@pd7tw2no> <d0teba$sun$1@panix5.panix.com> <kJrYd.647241$8l.588697@pd7tw1no> <d0tkam$ok$1@panix5.panix.com> <NftYd.646309$Xk.156984@pd7tw3no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:NftYd.646309$Xk.156984@pd7tw3no...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> > In article <kJrYd.647241$8l.588697@pd7tw1no>,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > That's how it is, now... and in future it might be a bit different;
things
> > might change after she's shuffled off this mortal coil, placed in a box
> > and had some dirt thrown on top of her.
> >
> > As the Hebrews used to say, 'de mortuis nil nisi bonum'... and stuff
like
> > that-there.
> >
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sometimes, when people are alive, we take them for granted and only realise
after they are gone that they impressed themselves on our lives and we are
poorer without them.

Even 'difficult' people have their value.

Your Aunt's comment is certainly poignant and hints at deep wells of family
bickering, with love trying to hold it together.

I hope that as time passes, the positive memories of your mother you
described will help to cancel the bad ones.

I had a Liverpudlian Mother-in-Law. She raised 5 kids in Kirkby with an
alcoholic husband who was seldom there. All of that family were forever
fighting with each other and there was never a time when someone in the
family wasn't  'not talking' to someone else... I had nothing but respect
and regard for this lady; salt of the Earth, now sadly departed. You might
think that loss would teach people to value each other and treat each other
better, but it doesn't seem to. They were all howling crying at various
family funerals, and within a day or two were back at each other's throats.

Maybe it's a Liverpool thing... (but I don't think so...).

You are right that I would have given much to spend more time with my Dad,
(so would my Mother) but we both appreciated the time we DID have. I believe
his attitude was coloured by knowing he could die at any moment (after his
first heart attack)  and that made him spend as much time as possible with
me as a child and communicate values and encourage me to be independent.

There is a positive side to most things.

Pete.
```

```
> Well sort of :-). My mother and my beloved, both called Eileen, (and

stations to have a machine on the pump than to pay for the risk of handling cash
for instance.    There are costs to handling cash as well, so tradeoffs are
made.
```

```
On 14-Mar-2005, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

> But I was talking furniture Howard. Let me know when you find a gas
> station that lets you haggle over the price per gallon/litre with either
> cash or plastic.

I was just talking costs.

> Got to be about 15 years ago, my second visit to Seattle, Wa. We pull
> into a downtown gas station  with an *old* Plymouth Chrysler station
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> take those things". Times change.

The cheapest station in my town only takes cash.   But that doesn't mean that
costs aren't changing to make plastic more preferable and cash less preferable.
 In some high crime areas these may have passed each other.

Trouble is, in many high crime areas, people often buy $10 worth of gas because
that's how much money they have.   Credit won't work.

The future of money is one which should interest IS people.    It's dangerous to
have most of our paper money overseas where our governments have less control
over counterfeiting.   If we switch to virtual money, we don't eliminate fraud.


It is quite possible to create a system where paper money is traced by serial
number.   Everybody passes money through a reader or money contains some RFID
type enhancement.   States would like that, bad guys hate it, and people
concerned with privacy would be concerned.    Looking at how cost cutters such
as Wal*Mart are willing to spend money on RFID, I wonder how privacy concerns
are going to be met when every dollar is accounted for.   And I expect it will
be.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> It is quite possible to create a system where paper money is traced by serial
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> be.

On your last comment. My wife has worked for quite some years at the Bay 
department stores (Hudson's Bay Company). The sweet lady is computer 
illiterate - but as she describes it, their POS systems haven't been too 
swift. Can't judge, because I've never seen a screen layout.

Now we move to pilferage, theft. At long last retail seems to have got 
its act together, although the features have been there for YEARS in 
their POS applications.

1. You buy something - say for somebody's wedding.
2. The future bride can now return it - doesn't like choice, colour or 
whatever. Can also swap for something else.
3. Slippery Sam picks up another item, (same SKU), on display, goes to 
cashiers and says he wants a refund - doesn't like it etc. (They, 
cashiers, phone security with a 'Code 3').

First transaction recorded date, store ID, plastic ID and SKU ID - and 
naturally, subtracted it from stock. Second transaction also records 
date and IDs and puts back into stock. Sam is SOL - how come he's trying 
to return something already back in stock ?

At long last - sensible use of their DB.

Of course doesn't cure theft - it will still occur. It's an art :-) 
Interesting observation from my wife from her last trip to UK. Being oh 
so politically correct on this side of the pond we have - "Shoplifters 
will be prosecuted". In the UK - "Thieves will be prosecuted".

Jimmy
```

```
say, gets you airline miles, and paying it off each month, means that

getting benefits for it.

extra to use a credit card.  :)
```

- **References:** `<6m9i21hb0sto5csvhs6blknijujuorjone@4ax.com> <6e3b2$42292eba$45491f85$22262@KNOLOGY.NET> <255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <5cd44$422a5c3c$45491f85$6618@KNOLOGY.NET> <q98l21th982n0mvlsotd9if55t2426ln0p@4ax.com> <9e1f6$422c687a$45491f85$15110@KNOLOGY.NET> <hN2Xd.596757$8l.120899@pd7tw1no> <aed87$422d1def$45491f85$27427@KNOLOGY.NET> <d0ki3f$qh$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <rtrr21houoicr1of66ac1rfpdlhprb7go6@4ax.com> <ks8u215oul6aruvgafpnvtn7704vv2uri4@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 09 Mar 2005 11:29:55 -0500, SkippyPB
<swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote:

>On Tue, 08 Mar 2005 12:34:38 -0600, Robert Wagner
><spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> enlightened us:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>third and the stock market took its "Black Monday" plunge....

Fuzzy thinking here. There isn't one ("the") deficit, there are two.
The budget deficit causes importation of money to finance government
debt; the trade deficit causes exportation of money to finance private
investment and consumption. The latter MIGHT be financed by private
debt in the form of selling stocks and bonds to foreigners, it might
be financed by domestic savings/investment (fat chance), or it might
be, in fact will be, financed by cheapening the dollar. 

Many find money supply and economics mind-boggling. I'll simplify it.
Picture four players at a card table: the US government, rich people,
poor people and foreigners. The result of current economic policies is
that rich people and foreigners will have a net cash inflow,
government will break even, poor people (consumers) will have a cash
outflow. If that's too simple, consider this analysis:
 
-- begin quotation -- 

The size of the U.S. deficit is causing alarm among some commentators
who are using it to draw attention to America's foreign debt. Even
though mercantilist fallacies are lurking behind these warning that is
no reason to dismiss them outright.

However, this is basically what optimists are doing when they assure
the public that there is nothing to worry about because the country is
borrowing to expand its productive capacity. As evidence of this view
they point out that the US stock market has risen relative to the rest
of the world, inferring that this rise is a reflection of increased
investment.

They also correctly point out that the US had deficits throughout the
nineteenth century. In fact, from the end of the War of Independence
in 1784 right up to 1914 America was a debtor nation, running an
annual deficit on its balance of trade until the 1870s, even though
she had built a tariff wall. (Protectionists argue that tariffs cure
the alleged ills of trade deficits).

How could this be? The answer is simple: most of the debt was incurred
by individuals who used it to import capital goods, mainly from
Britain. In other words, American entrepreneurs used British savings
to expand America's productive capacity. During this period thousands
of these entrepreneurs repaid their British loans, others took out new
loans while thousands more borrowed for the first time.

[Now, replace Britain with US and US with foreign.]

And so it went on, decade after decade and each decade saw the
American economy expand and living standards rise. Of course, some
entrepreneurs failed. But the consequences of failure were shared
between the American borrower and the British investor and not their
respective governments.

It ought to be clear that the so-called debt problem is really a
non-problem in the sense that what really matters is not debt but how
it is acquired. Therefore, running a trade surplus is not necessarily
a sign of economic health. Those who think otherwise have forgotten
that during the depressed 1930s when tariffs were strongly defended
and unemployment averaged 17 per cent America was a creditor nation
with a trade surplus.

But what the optimists overlook is that the gold was king during the
nineteenth century, which meant that countries that deviated from the
standard soon found themselves having to make the necessary monetary
corrections.

The importance of the gold standard lies in the fact that when the US
borrowed from, for example, British investors it was borrowing real
savings and not phony bank deposits. That is to say, these saving
actually consisted of deferred consumption.

Once Keynes persuaded politicians to abandon gold, that "barbaric
relic", as he called it, countries had to rely entirely on fiat money.
This created unprecedented inflation on a global scale. It also
generated bad deficits. These occur when central banks let loose with
the money supply, usually through our old enemy credit expansion, the
same process that triggers booms and inflates asset values.

But there is another side to credit expansion and that is its effect
on the trade balance. Monetary expansion inflates domestic spending
which in turn raises the demand for imports. This demand continues to
grow until the deficit reaches a point where the central bank sees it
as another warning signal that monetary policy needs to be tightened.

Now the central bank brings about credit expansion by forcing down
interest rates. This eventually causes businesses expand their demand
for loans for investment purposes. There is no reason that some of
these loans should not be used to import capital goods.

These imports would be seen by the optimists as evidence that the
deficit didn't matter because it was adding to the nation's productive
capacity. As the Keynesians say, it merely shows that investment
exceeds savings. This view only demonstrates that they are oblivious
to the fact that investment in excess of savings is just another way
of saying that the country is suffering from inflation.

Therefore, what the optimists call evidence of a healthy demand for
capital goods is really a symptom of an inflationary process.

So is the US deficit a symptom of a loose monetary policy that is
misdirecting production or a sign of healthy economic growth? At this
point I think we should let the monetary facts speak for themselves.

Using the Austrian definition of the money supply, from January 2001
to March 2004 money supply increased by more than 22 per cent, an
annual average increase of over 7 per cent.

The problem, therefore, is not foreign debt or trade deficits but a
loose monetary policy, a policy that is laying the foundations for
another recession. 

http://www.brookesnews.com/041005_foreign_debts_deficits.html
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

Republican

> 2 - list all US presidents in historical sequence, starting with FDR

> 3 - against each, give them a rating of 1 to 10, 1 being LOW. (Don't 
> bamboozle us with accompanying arguments - just keep it basic 
> statistical). Any arguments could follow later in the thread.

#32 - 1933-1945 - Franklin D. Roosevelt - 4
#33 - 1945-1953 - Harry Truman - 6
#34 - 1953-1961 - Dwight Eisenhower - 8
#35 - 1961-1963 - John F. Kennedy - 9
#36 - 1963-1969 - Lyndon Johnson - 7
#37 - 1969-1974 - Richard Nixon - 4
#38 - 1974-1977 - Gerald Ford - 5
#39 - 1977-1981 - Jimmy Carter - 3
#40 - 1981-1989 - Ronald Reagan - 10
#41 - 1989-1993 - George H. W. Bush - 7
#42 - 1993-2001 - William J. Clinton - 3
#43 - 2001-     - George W. Bush - 9

> US Deficit figures - what you OWE - no fancy Enron accounting. (In 
> simple non bean-counter terms - what you took in as income and how much 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Prez $43 - what is the latest known deficit

You're saying "deficit", but I think you mean "debt".  Deficit is a 
number that varies from year to year, based on the budget.  If that's 
really what you want, I have those numbers as well.  But, as national 
debt goes, here are the thresholds for the first and last years of each 
of the presidents mentioned above (the table I have starts at 1940)...

Pres  Year   Amount (M)   % GDP
#32 - 1944 -    184,796 -  88.4
#33 - 1945 -    235,182 - 106.3
       1952 -    214,758 -  61.6
#34 - 1953 -    218,383 -  58.5
       1960 -    236,840 -  45.6
#35 - 1961 -    238,357 -  44.8
       1962 -    248,010 -  43.6
#36 - 1963 -    253,978 -  42.4
       1968 -    289,545 -  33.3
#37 - 1969 -    278,108 -  29.3
       1973 -    340,910 -  26.1
#38 - 1974 -    343,699 -  23.8
       1976 -    477,404 -  27.5
#39 - 1977 -    549,104 -  27.9
       1980 -    711,932 -  26.1
#40 - 1981 -    789,427 -  25.8
       1988 -  2,051,819 -  40.9
#41 - 1989 -  2,190,956 -  40.5
       1992 -  3,000,073 -  48.2
#42 - 1993 -  3,248,755 -  49.5
       2000 -  3,410,120 -  35.0
#43 - 2001 -  3,477,472 -  33.6
(est) 2005 -  3,547,672 -  29.2

source:
http://www.whitehouse.gov/omb/budget/fy2003/hist.html
```

```
On Mon, 07 Mar 2005 20:25:49 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> enlightened us:

>LX-i wrote:
>> Robert Wagner wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK.  I'll play.

>1 - you have to state your current political conviction, Republican, 
>Democrats, Libertarians, Ralph Nader, Greens or whoever.

I'm a registered Independent.

>2 - list all US presidents in historical sequence, starting with FDR
>3 - against each, give them a rating of 1 to 10, 1 being LOW. (Don't 
>bamboozle us with accompanying arguments - just keep it basic 
>statistical). Any arguments could follow later in the thread.
>

Franklin D. Roosevelt - 10 - ended the Great Depression, instituted
Social Security, came out of WW2 with a win

Harry S. Truman - 3 - His failre to intercede with France when they
invaded Vietnam set the stage for the illegal, unnecessary
military/CIA actions that became the Vietnam War.

Dwight Eisenhower - 5 - can't think of anything good or bad he did
which is probably why he only lasted 1 term.

John F. Kennedy - 8 - kept Russia in check, brought prestige to the
Whitehouse, focused on Civil Rights and the need of civilians to
become involved in their government.

Lyndon B. Johnson - 7 - mixed feelings since he was president when I
got drafted.  He could have but didn't end the Vietnam War.  But he
did get some very meaningful legislation passed and did much, like
FDR, to try and help the "little guy".

Richard Nixon - 2 - abused his office and power.  Did end the Vietnam
War but only because public opinion was so overwhelmingly in favor of
it.  Also managed to have a budget surplus in 1969, the first time in
over a decade.

Gerald Ford - 5 - another Eisenhower in terms of what he accomplished
while in office.

Jimmy Carter - 4 - Probably the most honest and decent president we've
ever had and certainly the most intelligent.  However, he got blind
sided by the Iranians which cost him getting re-elected.  He also did
little to shore up a sagging economy.

Ronald Regan - 7 - Supply Side economics damn near ruined the country.
But his easy nature and above average communication skills allowed him
to form a solid partnership with Margaret Thatcher of the UK and
Mikhail Gorbachov of the USSR which brought about the end of the cold
war and freedom for much of eastern Europe.  All without a bullet
being fired.

George H. Bush - 4 - Oversaw a successful, albiet too short many
think, Gulf War when Saddam invaded Kuwait.  Complete ignored the US
economy which was still struggling which was why he only lasted 1
term.

William Clinton - 8 - Was lucky enough to be elected at the start of
the .com boon.  Was smart enough not to get in its way and to
introduce policies that took advantage of it.  Was the first president
since Nixon to oversee a budget surplus.  His lying about having oral
sex with an intern was way overblown (no pun intended) and was just
fodder for a vindictive right wing which wasted much time and
resources over nothing.  He still was re-elected.  He also was a
president who cared about the "little guy".

George W. Bush - 2 - Iraq, prisoners sent to other countries to be
tourtered- many never charged with anything.  Enough said.


>When some of you Yanks have had a go, perhaps some of us 'foreigners' 
>might have a crack.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well a little background on deficit.  When the U.S. government came
into existence and for about a 150 years thereafter the government
managed to keep a balanced budget. The only times a budget deficit
existed during these first 150 years were in times of war or other
catastrophic events. The Government, for instance, generated deficits
during the War of 1812, the recession of 1837, the Civil War, the
depression of the 1890s, and World War I.
 
However, as soon as the war ended the deficit would be eliminated and 
the economy which was much larger than the amounted debt would quickly
absorb it. 

 Budget deficits have grown larger and more frequent in the last
half-century. In the 1980s they soared to record levels. The
Government cut income tax rates, greatly increased defense 
spending, and didnï¿½t cut domestic spending enough to make up the 
difference. Also, the deep recession of the early 1980s reduced 
revenues, raising the deficit and forcing the Government to spend much
more on paying interest for the national debt at a time when interest 
rates were high. As a result, the national debt grew in size after 
1980. It grew from $709 billion to $3.6 trillion in 1990, only one 
decade later.  

In some cases a deficit is not bad.  Keynesian economics is an
economic model that predicts in times of low demand and high
unemployment a deficit will not cost anything. Instead a deficit would
allow more people to work, increasing productivity. A deficit does
this because it is invested into the economy by government. For
example if the government spends deficit money on new highways,
trucking will benefit and more jobs will be produced.  As long as the
government spends the money it gains from a deficit on assets that
increase its wealth and productivity, the debt actually benefits the
economy.

But, if the government spends money on programs that do not increase
its assets or productivity, then the governments net worth decreases
putting it into serious debt.

Is the U.S.ï¿½s current debt bad or good? The trick is finding 
out how large the deficit should be in order to allow for growth 
without waste. The U.S.ï¿½s deficit is bad at this point because the 
U.S. is close to its maximum production capabilities, and deficit 
money is being wasted.

(much of the previous information came from WWW.STUDYWORLD.COM )

Regards,

>And the 'deficit' is about home-grown domestic politics, but is affected 
>by your foreign policy and trading.
>
>Jimmy

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <5cd44$422a5c3c$45491f85$6618@KNOLOGY.NET> <q98l21th982n0mvlsotd9if55t2426ln0p@4ax.com> <9e1f6$422c687a$45491f85$15110@KNOLOGY.NET> <hN2Xd.596757$8l.120899@pd7tw1no> <lc9u21536afu8r2gilc391ml41241jum5h@4ax.com> <d0ncmi$d6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <8av031l1eiosbos28oj9gco7d45kagmqbc@4ax.com> <d0q0br$l49$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ogp331hluvnoqc5ppt5kb9acc5r1tpeclc@4ax.com> <d0sqpf$j4b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9r9631pbsbes9gl1ndqkgrllot8jnmsdp2@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> On Fri, 11 Mar 2005 19:13:21 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
> enlightened us:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> the primaries were held here in Ohio.

That seems to me a reasoned approach. Could be wrong but isn't it "McCain" ?

We really are betwixt the Devil and the Deep Blue Sea up here. Last 
General Election was October of 2004. Chretien (Liberal) who really had 
stayed around too long, left it to Paul Martin, waiting in the wings to 
take the Liberals through the election. Can't recall the numbers, but he 
has a minority Liberal government very dependent upon the support of the 
NDP (Labour or Socialists).

Meanwhile the newly constituted Conservatives ( a minority of 'old' 
style conservatives, Alliance/Reform - definitely right of centre, plus 
sympathy from 'active' and also 'non-active' religious groups). Throw 
into that mix the federal Party Quebecois - they'll vote as suits their 
Quebec base, so no reliable way of knowing which direction they will jump.

Myself as  a liberal with a small "l", and possibly others too, we had 
hoped for a fresh start with Martin. It was the Economist that dubbed 
him "Mr. Dithers" for his prevarication. Some he is guilty of, other 
aspects he has to be a realist, if he wants to hang on in politics.

I'll say it again, like his predecessors since Trudeau, all catholics 
with the exception of Kim Campbell (female and led the Conservative 
party after Brian Mulroney quit, followed by their getting decimated at 
the following election which introduced Chretien). Just like John Kerry, 
the catholic PMs didn't come clean about social issues - Kerry got 
clobbered in the States by the 'religious' and that included a high 
proportion of US catholics. Similarly Martin is getting up the nose of 
catholic traditionalists - that's a way of saying the Catholic Church as 
designed by its centuries old set of rules. Don't like the rules - then 
leave the Club.

Both our guys and Kerry should have clearly spelled it out, particularly 
as both Chretien and Martin are referred to as 'devout catholics'. My 
personal observation they should have said something to the following 
effect :-

"My religious beliefs are that same sex marriage is not acceptable. My 
church does not condemn homosexuals, (Forgive the sinner but not the 
sin). Similarly my religious beliefs do not allow me to condemn 
homosexuals (He that casts the first stone ......). Therefore in a 
religious context I see the word 'marriage' as having both a spiritual 
and cultural meaning.

I would not endorse any legislation that attacks homosexuals or 
lesbians. They must be accorded all the rights of partnerships in 
something we can define as a 'Civil Union'. In fact, as happens in some 
European countries, it is the 'Civil Union' which is the state 
recognition of marriage, followed by those of a religious persuasion 
having an official religious ceremony afterwards.

As a politician, although obviously influenced by my religious beliefs, 
I have a duty to my personal constituency to represent their views. 
Should the majority of my voters express a definite change to the 
meaning of the word 'marriage', then although reluctantly, I must 
represent my constituents. However I would be happier if we can agree on 
the dual usage of the words 'marriage' and 'Civil Union' ".

I'm no speech writer - but hope above conveys message.

Now some evangelists, catholics, other Christian groups, Muslims, Sikhs, 
Hindus, Jews etc., might still find the above difficult to swallow. But 
just perhaps, some would see the honesty in the man's words and may well 
adapt their own attitudes.

When I was a kid, homosexuality was a criminal offence, going back 
centuries. Recall the scene from Mel Gibson's 'Bravheart' where Edward I 
(Longshanks) gives his son's boy-friend the heave-ho from the castle 
window ? So 50s/60s it was no longer a criminal offence in UK. We took a 
gulp, thought on it and subsequently saw the injustice which had been 
cured by the new legislation. However, so far as 'religionists' are 
concerned, the airy-fairy approach of politicians has got them suspect.
They see changing the word 'marriage' to mean EXACTLY the same as 'civil 
union' as being a wedge. They question, "What comes next to attack our 
concept of family values ?'

Activists would have us believe that there are a reasonably large number 
of homosexuals. A study done in Chicago contradicts that saying that in 
fact (numbers escape me), that the percentage is only 0.1 or 0.01 
percent. Similarly studies have been done over roughly the last ten 
years in Scandinavia. Even though same sex marriage is allowed in some 
of the countries, it hasn't been taken up as a popular lifestyle, nor as 
sometimes implied, is there longevity to same-sex partnerships, even 
allowing for all the counter arguments about family unit divorces.

The issue which really concerns the sociologists that did this study 
(not a bunch of religious freaks), is the social time-bomb. Not now, nor 
next ten years, but somewhere down the road Gudrun with her two Daddies 
or Sven with his two Mommies is going to get curious and ask about 
"hers" or "his" family. "Where did I come from ?"  "Out of a test tube, 
Gudrun/Sven".  Real neat sci-fi scenario.

Boy am I in a quandary. First and last Tory I voted for was Winston 
Churchill - I'm having great problems sticking with Martin, but I'm not 
a happy camper with our Canadian Tories either - but on this particular 
issue, while I don't agree with their element of 'extremists', (they 
aren't all rednecks), I'm seeing myself reluctantly veering to the Right.

There are other issues with Martin - but the one above coupled with an 
active judiciary (our Supreme Court interpreting our Bill of Rights to 
make judicial rather than parliamentary law), really sticks in my craw.

Worth noting the difference between the US system and parliamentary 
systems. Like it or lump it, George W. Bush is around until the end of 
2008 - other than death, impeachment or resignation.

Paul Martin could be gone by the end of March. If he introduced some 
Bill that the various opposition groups strongly disagreed with, and 
assuming NDP (Labour) supported him - given that enough of his own 
Liberals voted with the opposition - on a vote of non-confidence - then 
he is gonzo. That's not about to happen - but that's how easily a 
parliamentary 'king' is toppled.

PS: Skippy - different topic. I see you made reference to time overseas. 
  Was it you in Cyprus handling both Greek and Turkish - if you confirm 
'Yes' I'll raise a separate thread to quiz you.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

_(thread depth: 41)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-13T02:19:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WqNYd.656187$Xk.17621@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4bec4$42338828$45491f85$14816@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <5cd44$422a5c3c$45491f85$6618@KNOLOGY.NET> <q98l21th982n0mvlsotd9if55t2426ln0p@4ax.com> <9e1f6$422c687a$45491f85$15110@KNOLOGY.NET> <hN2Xd.596757$8l.120899@pd7tw1no> <lc9u21536afu8r2gilc391ml41241jum5h@4ax.com> <d0ncmi$d6$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <8av031l1eiosbos28oj9gco7d45kagmqbc@4ax.com> <d0q0br$l49$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <ogp331hluvnoqc5ppt5kb9acc5r1tpeclc@4ax.com> <d0sqpf$j4b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9r9631pbsbes9gl1ndqkgrllot8jnmsdp2@4ax.com> <kKIYd.654568$Xk.65709@pd7tw3no> <4bec4$42338828$45491f85$14816@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
LX-i wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

A joke, but with an element of truth. Something I saw a short while 
back, but can't remember specifically. Historically, as humans we do 
things in cycles. While not blatantly apparent, there is a current 
tendency to move towards the right in political circles. The move to the 
left in Europe, say 30's - 60/70's resolved many of the social issues 
which had been around since the Victorian Industrial Revolution.

No massive inequities for blue collar workers now - then not much appeal 
for Socialist politics. In UK the TUC is no longer a major force in 
politics and in the States, although of a nuisance value, groups like 
the AFCIO (have I got the initials correct ?), don't have appeal.

But NOTE ! I said Socialist not Liberal. There's a man I meet when 
walking the dogs, not on a regular basis, because we don't both walk to 
a set time. Some five years younger than me, a Romanian who has never 
been back since he left. (In his early childhood they had a Fascist 
regime with sympathy for the Nazis as Uncle Joe was looking over there 
shoulders from the East, then WWII finishes - the commies were in. The 
Soviet Empire ceased to exist - so a new crowd takes over - he says they 
are all crooks).

A trained engineer, likes history - so that's my attraction and he can 
fill me in on some of the stuff from an Eastern European angle. He is 
also conversant with English and European history, generally. Speaks 
excellent accented English, but does have some difficulty getting his 
way around certain words because of his false teeth - I'm not joking. 
The teeth are a bit of an impediment.

Sensing one day that he was a liberal, or close, I asked him outright. 
He smiled and replied with a nod :-

"Yes, I suppose so. I oscillate between the Conservatives and Liberals, 
considering their points-of-view and take features from both".

The point is, he is a centralist, not dogmatic to one party creed - 
making his own judgments and arriving at a compromise position. Think on 
it, that's what most voters really want - a vanilla flavour which is 
netiher right nor left - but attempts to statisfy all. Well, at least 
the majority. ("Majority" does NOT mean 52%, but something like 70% plus).

You aint there yet on the last two paragraphs kiddo :-)

Jimmy
```

```
On 12-Mar-2005, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

> "Yes, I suppose so. I oscillate between the Conservatives and Liberals,
> considering their points-of-view and take features from both".
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the majority. ("Majority" does NOT mean 52%, but something like 70% plus).

Not necessarily.   People don't have to be centrist to have views that agree
with either the left or the right.    An example is someone who agrees with the
Pope in all issues.   Some of these are far right - others are quite liberal.  
Maybe there are some centrist positions as well, I don't know.

Would it be a liberal or a conservative value to offer all veterans the same
medical care as Medicare beneficiaries get?
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 12-Mar-2005, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe there are some centrist positions as well, I don't know.

That somewhat muddies the waters, introducing catholicism. As I've 
indicated, I don't go to church but I'm fairly close to the current 
Pope's concept of catholicism. The bit I'm not happy with, and which he 
can't turn back, is John XXIII's Vatican II 'liberalism' - mass now in 
lingua franca rather than Latin, altar girls etc. No big deal in a 
religious context - just doesn't sit well with me. Note that some of 
that 'liberalism' is 'social liberalism' - the well-being of the have-nots.

There are opponents who argue for religion not getting mixed into 
politics - there is no concrete dividing line between the two on social 
issues.

Bear in mind the Pope consistently says, "No war". It is the ultimate 
obscenity. As realists we do however accept, albeit reluctantly, that 
war is sometimes the only solution, i.e. unless your are a Quaker, 
Mennonite, Hutterite etc. Think on folks like Ollie North (a catholic), 
who appears to have thought 'the end justifies the means'. He is not 
going to buy into an 'absolutely no war stance'. On the other hand those 
on the Left, Labour/Socialist and perhaps veering a tittle to the old 
style communist 'idealism' will echo, "Right on, Papa ! War is bad".

Do all catholics follow the Pope's dictates. Have you counted any 
catholic families lately with a brood of eight kids ?

> Would it be a liberal or a conservative value to offer all veterans the same
> medical care as Medicare beneficiaries get?

Can't comment on your question as it is framed with US in mind.

Jimmy
```

```
back something like, "If we become immoral and as uncaring as our 
enemies, then we become our enemies". Nothing to do with the Guatanmo 
occupants returned to UK. They had their own bunch imprisoned awaiting 
trial for about a year or more. British intelligence knew, just like the 
troops who captured the el Qadae people in Afghanistan, that they had 
some 'baddies'. Went to trial, insufficient evidence and they were all 
released. Hellishly frustrating but if we believe in Western Law then we 
can't just take the bits which suit our current preference. It's the 
whole package or anarchy.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 14-Mar-2005, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mid East?

First, Israel is not the #2 bad guy - Iran is.  Israel doesn't even come 
close.  Second, you're changing the subject.  The point is that the "No 
WMD!  No WMD!" crowed may soon be proved to be wrong.

But, to answer your question, we're safer *and* less safe at the same 
time.  These parts, under coordination of Saddam Hussein, could have 
produced very harmful weapons quickly.  In pieces, smaller quantities of 
weapons can be made, and it will take longer.  The downside is that if 
the pieces are all in one place, once you find them, you've eliminated 
the threat - with them dispersed, it's more difficult to eliminate *all* 
the threats.
```

```
>>>The point is that the "No 
>>>WMD!  No WMD!" crowed may soon be proved to be wrong.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Probably "C" - but what are they going to fertilize in the desert?  Sand?

I was taught, e'er-so-long ago, that sometimes a bit of research can help 
prevent foot-in-mouth disease.  Last time I looked 6% of US$37,920,000,000 
(the latter figure being 21.8% less than the previous year's) is 
US$2,275,200,000... not, say, what the US or Brazil produces but still a 
fair bit of ' wheat, barley, rice, vegetables, dates, cotton; cattle, 
sheep'.

(figures taken from 
http://www.cia.gov/cia/publications/factbook/geos/iz.html ... search for 
the word 'agriculture')

That, of course, assumes that they kept it all for their own use and 
didn't sell any to anyone else... but nobody would ever dream of making 
money selling fertiliser, right?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

_(thread depth: 50)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-18T18:52:15-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <9098e$4238e8ea$45491f85$11272@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1bl5o$3if$1@panix5.panix.com> <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> answers 'I choose not to tell you.'

That's not what's happening here.  The proven mass-murder hides 
bomb-making materials.  However, he hasn't been prohibited from 
producing products to help his crops grow.  Those are *not* the actions 
of an innocent man.  A condition of peace in 1991 was his future 
transparency in weapons programs - he failed to live up to his side, so 
we come a'lookin'.  No tyranny there at all - just enforcing the contract.

> As for 'dismantling'... that's what looters do, last I looked.

This dismantling was *far* more organized than the random looting that 
happened, for example, in Baghdad after the statue fell.  This 
dismantling had government approval.  Can a government loot its own stuff?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

_(thread depth: 52)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-19T12:57:02-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d1fufq$p4t$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com> <e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1fufq$p4t$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> The materials were described as 'dual use', if I recall correctly.

Okay - where are the fruits of the permissible use?

>>Those are *not* the actions 
>>of an innocent man.  A condition of peace in 1991 was his future 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I see... who determined, then, that it was explosives and not fertiliser?

They didn't show us the fertilizer, and the regime had a penchant for 
producing chemical weapons - the only safe assumption is that they were 
*not* making fertilizer.  Remember, we didn't just spin the globe, throw 
a dart at it, and decide we're going to reshape Iraq in our image.  This 
is a proven dangerous regime who routinely raped, tortured, and murdered 
its own citizens; who trained terrorists; and who has shown that they 
have no qualms about using chemical or biological weapons.

They're not on trial for petty larceny - the stakes are much higher, and 
they failed to live up to their end of the UN agreement.

>>>As for 'dismantling'... that's what looters do, last I looked.
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> described as 'looting'.

Well, let's go back to the NYT story...

http://www.nytimes.com/2005/03/13/international/middleeast/13loot.html

Online page 4, first paragraph...
"In its most recent report to the United Nations Security Council, in 
October, the agency said it 'continues to be concerned about the 
widespread and apparently systematic dismantlement that has taken place 
at sites previously relevant to Iraq's nuclear program.'"

Online page 4, next-to-last paragraph...
"In interviews, officials of the monitoring commission and the atomic 
energy agency said the two agencies had heard nothing from Baghdad - 
with one notable exception. On Oct. 10, the Iraqi Ministry of Science 
and Technology wrote to the atomic agency to say a stockpile of high 
explosives at Al Qaqaa had been lost because of 'theft and looting.'"

It's being called looting because that's what the oh-so-honest Iraqi 
government, who did not report to the IAEA and UNMOVIC as required, is 
calling it.  As tightly as these materials must have been guarded, to 
keep the inspectors from finding it, do you honestly believe that the 
government knew nothing of this?  Somehow, they didn't notice huge 
trucks and cranes "systematic[ally] dismantling" their chemical weapons 
plants?
```

- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com> <e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1fufq$p4t$1@panix5.panix.com> <7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET> <PbidnTV08tqXPKHfRVn-vQ@rogers.com>`

```
> I don't think there's any chemical or bio weapons that use fertilizer
as a base.

The Bishopsgate bomb was a truckload of fertiliser, I was in London at
the time.  Several other attacks around the same time were much the
same.
```

```
I suggest you do a web search for ammonium nitrate, when I just looked
on Yahoo, I found entries from the UK government about safe storage
regulations, a Wikkpedia entry and an article by the BBC.  Sugar is
also a common ingredient.  I don't want to go into too much detail as
this isn't a bomb making FAQ, but many explosives are made from
derivatives of nitrates, sugar, wood pulp, etc, including dynamite,
trinitrotoluene, amatol and guncotton.

Robert
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Hmm...  Sounds suspicious, no?

>>>I see... who determined, then, that it was explosives and not fertiliser?
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> been discovered.

Well, we know he used some of them on his own people...  Hard to measure 
them once they've dissipated into the atmosphere, but once someone has 
used a weapon like that, they'll use it again.

> According to his interview with Barbara Walters President Bush is unaware 
> of any such discoveries, as well... but what does *he* know, eh?

I'm not quite sure what to make of his statements...  He knows that 
Iraqis trained Al-Quaeda, he knows that the UNMOVIC inspectors found 
banned rockets several months before the invasion started, and he knows 
what we've found there to this point.  I think it's part of his 
"changing the tone" thing...

>>>Document this assertion, please... according to the report I read it was 
>>>described as 'looting'.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> say that FICA isn't a tax.

Does it make it any less true if you can't link to a web site that says 
what you saw?  This is the part of your argument that I find 
particularly infuriating.  People see things, and the things that these 
people see aren't written down.  If they tell someone, then that record 
is preserved orally.

Why do the police like to have eyewitnesses?  It's only the word of 
someone who observed something at the time.

> At any rate... it seems that you believe the parts of his story that 
> support your view and disbelieve those that do not.

No, you read the story with a presumption of innocence are the part of 
the Ba'athist regime - I read it with a presumption of guilt.  When 
you've broken the law (invading Kuwait), and violated your parole 
(kicking out inspectors), it is up to you to prove your innocence.

You've yet to address my question of how something so heavily guarded 
against discovery by inspectors could be infiltrated and broken down so 
easily without cooperation from the Iraqi government.  No, the article 
didn't say that - but it's not too hard to connect the dots!
```

```
says in a public interview that he is unaware of any discoveries of 
weapons of mass destruction.  I barely know what *I* know, let alone 
anyone else... so all I have to go on is his public statement.

>
>>>>Document this assertion, please... according to the report I read it was 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>what you saw?

I do not speak of truth, I speak of evidence... and all the article

>particularly infuriating.  People see things, and the things that these 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

nothing more, nothing less.

>
>> At any rate... it seems that you believe the parts of his story that 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the Ba'athist regime - I read it with a presumption of guilt.

society.  If you want to use the Napoleonic Code then I'm sure the French 
will be *very* impressed.

>When 
>you've broken the law (invading Kuwait), and violated your parole 
>(kicking out inspectors), it is up to you to prove your innocence.

Not in *my* USA, it isn't... the onus probandi *always* rests upon the 
accuser.  Not once, not twice... *always*.

>

>against discovery by inspectors could be infiltrated and broken down so 
>easily without cooperation from the Iraqi government.  No, the article 
>didn't say that - but it's not too hard to connect the dots!

Let's see... a country is invaded and internal security collapses.  Rather 
easy dots to connect, I agree.

DD
```

- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1ioa1$o9c$1@panix5.panix.com> <cdb32$423f4c30$45491f85$31814@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1nrm5$nob$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <cdb32$423f4c30$45491f85$31814@KNOLOGY.NET>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> will be *very* impressed.

For our citizens, yes.  For people who have perpetrated terrorism 
against our country, not a chance.

>>When 
>>you've broken the law (invading Kuwait), and violated your parole 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> accuser.  Not once, not twice... *always*.

That only applies to matters within our country.  Treating national 
defense like a criminal proceeding is what #42's administration did - 
and it severely weakened us.  You speed, let the cop produce the RADAR 
reading.  You shoot somebody, let the state produce the murder weapon 
with your fingerprints on it.  However, you threaten or attack our 
nation, you've crossed the line, and will be dealt with accordingly.

Saddam Hussein was guilty - guilty of gassing his own people, guilty of 
violating 12 international resolutions - many of which having the 
defined consequence of removal from power.  If you want to look at it 
that way, we're just enforcing the law.
```

```
part of the world who used to think he was a bad guy and the US the victims have
switched that believe 180�.   Which doesn't make us safe.
```

- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com> <e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1fufq$p4t$1@panix5.panix.com> <7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1mvoh$12b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <80ac6$423f5dcf$45491f85$28434@KNOLOGY.NET> <k_L%d.5034$JK1.333977@news20.bellglobal.com> <4509e$4240dbdb$45491f85$26179@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
LX-i wrote:
> Donald Tees wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

There were *NO* U.S., citizens killed by the Iraq's until after Bush 
invaded.  Now there thousands, and fatalities are increasing daily. How 
does that equate to "safer"? Even if you blame all the New York deaths 
on him (which is *really* stretching it) then the numbers still do not 
work out.



>> The only way you can expect a law to be accepted is if both sides are 
>> subject to the same law.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Does the USA have weapons of mass destruction?  If a government has 
weapons of mass destruction, does that make the leaders guilty of a 
crime? If a country has weapons of mass destruction, then is it moral to 
fight against that country? Does that make it a good and moral thing to 
go out and hunt down the leaders of that country? Does it make it a 
moral act to declare war on them, and kill thousands of them?

I'm sure you will claim it is different for you, 'cause you are so noble 
and kind and god is on your side ... but pardon me while I barf.

If the United States wants to be the policeman of the world, then they 
had better start obeying international laws.  To date, the United 
States' official position is that they and their president are 
completely above the law, and subject to no law whatsoever outside the 
US. You made the same claim three posts back ... I believe you stated 
that even having a president *try* to treat outsiders within the law was 
a fatal weakness.

As far as I can see, your attitude is to just shoot everyone you do not 
like. Laws are useless, courts are useless, and even US laws are only 
for your own citizen's within your own borders.  Outside those borders, 
the rest of us have to obey your laws, but we have none of your rights.

Can you not understand why I am not that fussy about the idea?

You have tried to justify the invasion of Iraq based on the fact that 
their leader killed his own citizens.  Your leader has also killed over 
100,000 of the same citizens. If you take the deaths-per-year of *all* 
civilians(instead of only counting the US ones) for the last twenty, 
your president's kill ratio is getting right up there with the man you 
are holding prisoner. If you include your leader's father, then it 
exceeds his.

Let me put it in simpler terms.  If you wish the rest of the world to 
treat the united staes as a civilized country, then perhaps you need to 
treat the rest of the world as if they also were civilized. The way you 
act now, our choices are to submit to US soldiers acting completely 
beyond the law, or to fight back.

US foreign relationships consist of bullying and if that does not work, 
then kill a few people, bribe a few more, see if you can find someone 
that will do as they are told, put them in power, and start over. You 
have been doiung that for thirty years. *You* are the ones that armed 
and financed El Quaida to fight the Russians.

What happened in New York was terrorism. Chasing the terrorists was 
dropped within the year. You have *invaded* another country.  When 
citizen's fight back against invader's it is not terrorism. It is the 
same thing you are paid to do, and claim a moral obligation to do. You 
are *not* fighting terrorism, you are fighting a war that *you* declared.

The only laws the US seems to want to defend are the ones that work out 
to the US owning everything, the US dictating everyone's actions, and 
the US being answerable to no one.  The other laws are for failures(like 
#42, as you put it).

Donald
```

- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com> <e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1fufq$p4t$1@panix5.panix.com> <7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1mvoh$12b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <80ac6$423f5dcf$45491f85$28434@KNOLOGY.NET> <k_L%d.5034$JK1.333977@news20.bellglobal.com> <4509e$4240dbdb$45491f85$26179@KNOLOGY.NET> <E860e.18499$nK.859275@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> work out.

You're saying that because soldiers (and isolate civilians) have been 
killed in the prosecuting of a war, that Americans on our own soil are 
less safe?  That's a strange logic you're using there...

>>> The only way you can expect a law to be accepted is if both sides are 
>>> subject to the same law.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> moral act to declare war on them, and kill thousands of them?

What makes is moral is the fact that the international community made 
demands on them for remaining in power, and they agreed.  Then, they 
reneged on their agreement.  The next logical step is that they are 
going back to doing what they have been prohibited from doing.  The US 
has had nukes for a long time - since before the UN was founded.  There 
have been efforts to draw down the arsenal, but we still have them.  So 
far, we haven't used them indiscriminately (in fact, we haven't used 
them since we first used them).

> I'm sure you will claim it is different for you, 'cause you are so noble 
> and kind and god is on your side ... but pardon me while I barf.

Have I ever claimed that God is on my side because I am an American?

> If the United States wants to be the policeman of the world, then they 
> had better start obeying international laws.  To date, the United 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> a fatal weakness.

We don't *want* to be the policemen of the world - it always falls to us 
because we have a kick-butt military, and due the ineptitude of the UN, 
the situation is worse when we get there than it would have been had 
they taken decisive action sooner.  But, when we're enforcing part of a 
resolution that says, in effect, "you will be removed from power", then 
by golly, that's what we're going to do.

The difference is that we don't go raping and plundering - we just 
liberate, rebuild, and train.  And, proved terrorists need no trial, no 
lawyers - they want to operate outside the law, the military takes care 
of it.

> As far as I can see, your attitude is to just shoot everyone you do not 
> like. Laws are useless, courts are useless, and even US laws are only 
> for your own citizen's within your own borders.  Outside those borders, 
> the rest of us have to obey your laws, but we have none of your rights.

What US laws do we demand that Canadians (or Mexicans, Europeans, etc.) 
follow, other than immigration?  And, I'm not for shooting everyone I 
don't like - but you threaten my country, you pay the price.  One of the 
things that's great about this nation is that we have freedom, but we 
realize that it doesn't come cheap.

> Can you not understand why I am not that fussy about the idea?
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> exceeds his.

How many of those citizens were uniformed combatants or combatants 
affiliated with the Ba'athist party?  There's *no* way we've had 100k in 
collateral damage in that operation.  We took out their freaking 
infrastructure *at night*, when people weren't in the buildings.  Sure, 
we bombed a restaurant - but, had we be 20 minutes earlier, we would've 
gotten Uday and Qusay then.

Collateral damage is inevitable - that's why no country wants home field 
advantage when it comes to war.  It's also regrettable, but it wasn't us 
who flaunted 12 international directives - the blame for those deaths 
lies solely on the one who *did* break the law.

> Let me put it in simpler terms.  If you wish the rest of the world to 
> treat the united staes as a civilized country, then perhaps you need to 
> treat the rest of the world as if they also were civilized. The way you 
> act now, our choices are to submit to US soldiers acting completely 
> beyond the law, or to fight back.

You keep making this claim - apart from Abu Ghraib (which is 
regrettable, but not so much when you think about what *used* to go on 
there) and Guantanamo Bay (another bunch of unsubstantiated whining and 
rumors) - what laws do our soldiers not obey?

> US foreign relationships consist of bullying and if that does not work, 
> then kill a few people, bribe a few more, see if you can find someone 
> that will do as they are told, put them in power, and start over. You 
> have been doiung that for thirty years. *You* are the ones that armed 
> and financed El Quaida to fight the Russians.

At the time, that was the thing to do - stop the spread of Communism. 
It worked - the folks we helped, though, turned despotic.  No one said 
that running this country was easy - you've got tough decisions to make 
(thinking back to JFK and the Cuban Missile Crisis, for example).

> What happened in New York was terrorism. Chasing the terrorists was 
> dropped within the year. You have *invaded* another country.  When 
> citizen's fight back against invader's it is not terrorism. It is the 
> same thing you are paid to do, and claim a moral obligation to do. You 
> are *not* fighting terrorism, you are fighting a war that *you* declared.

We have waged an organized *war* against a regime that sponsors and 
produces terrorists.  No, "insurgent" attacks on our troops is not 
terrorism - but, part of fighting terrorism is breaking up the network 
that produces terrorists.  We got the one in Asia, we broke up a strong 
hold in the Middle East (where democracy seems to be spreading like 
wildfire these days now - wonder where they got that idea?), and we're 
working through diplomatic channels, at the current time, with others.

> The only laws the US seems to want to defend are the ones that work out 
> to the US owning everything, the US dictating everyone's actions, and 
> the US being answerable to no one.  The other laws are for failures(like 
> #42, as you put it).

Terrorists are the lowest form of combatant there is.  It's cowardice, 
it indiscriminately targets civilians, and it is meant to inspire, as 
the term would suggest, terror.  If we did try them in our courts and 
convict them, they'd get countless appeals - and then the folks having a 
"Terri's Dead" party would issue a statement about how we couldn't rush 
to judgment.
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Because they hate us!  They are deluded into thinking that they'll get 
72 virgins if they die killing us.  People love to talk about how 
horrible the Inquisitions were, but now we've got another real-life 
religious war, and all we hear is about how tolerant we should be.  HOGWASH!

WHO CARES WHY THEY HATE US!  It is immoral to kill someone just because 
you disagree with who they are or what they do.  Abortion is wrong, but 
so is shooting abortion doctors.  Prostitution is wrong, but so is 
slapping one around.  Divorce is wrong, but so is killing attorneys who 
advertise for it.  *THEY* alone are responsible for their actions, and 
they alone who must deal with it.  Some oversensitive weenie or 
religious zealot is *not* going to change this country.  If they don't 
like us, leave us alone - if they don't leave us alone, suffer the 
consequences.

No fascist religious group is going to take away our freedoms - but 
their goal isn't even that, it's to take away our lives!  If we do not 
strike back with a vengeance, then we truly *will* be less safe.  If 
someone thinks that their violence may provoke violence back, they are 
less likely to commit their violent act to begin with.

Please tell me you're just playing devil's advocate on some of this stuff...
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<NLP0e.585$vd.465@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com> <e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1fufq$p4t$1@panix5.panix.com> <7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1mvoh$12b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <80ac6$423f5dcf$45491f85$28434@KNOLOGY.NET> <k_L%d.5034$JK1.333977@news20.bellglobal.com> <4509e$4240dbdb$45491f85$26179@KNOLOGY.NET> <E860e.18499$nK.859275@news20.bellglobal.com> <eb36a$4242377e$45491f85$1820@KNOLOGY.NET> <7it0e.201995$pc5.114559@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <e06ee$42439131$45491f85$4979@KNOLOGY.NET> <NLP0e.585$vd.465@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
jce wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:e06ee$42439131$45491f85$4979@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>>resolution?

Don't hold your breath. The Chinese are never going to volunteer to
support anything attacking anybody showing a semblance of being a
dictatorial regime. It's just as likely ex KGB-man Putnin will never 
come on board either.

Remember this thread contains 'Foreign Policy'. Daniel, you are way off
track in your optimism. We've already been there with the Chinese, and 
at the time they were a pretty primitive nation. The only time the UN
military force had significance, while I was still in apprentice
training - the Korean War of the 'Fifties, commanded by Douglas
MacArthur. Like those graphical images we see in modern movies, the
Chinese came in droves into S. Korea. They wiped our asses, just with
unlimited manpower. It finished in an uneasy stalemate, and the Korean
situation is still the same today.

Then through their surrogate, N. Vietnam, YOU (not us), had the tragedy
of the Vietnam War, with a disillusioned America leaving with its tail
between its legs.

As regards the PRC imploding, try and get a copy of Fortune "Inside the 
New China", special issue 2004 Oct 04. With permission, I took my copy 
from the doctor's waiting room :-). They are becoming a powerhouse, and 
just as in Hong Kong, they are prepared to be not quite so communistic 
ally rigid where they can make money. A new entrepreneurial class, both 
home-grown and returnees from the States are the new middle class 
driving force.

Provided the Comintern can keep the rising middle class happy, they can 
go on for decades with their slightly revamped version of capitalism. No 
good making money if you can't spend it, so they are having to consider 
'benefits' to keep the masses happy. A projection of some 2 million 
autos in China by about 2010 (or was that 2020 ?). That's a lot of 
fill-ups at the gas pumps.

I cut out the article from the Calgary Herald - but regret can't find 
it. Written by an Assistant (Chinese) Political Scientist professor at 
the U. of Alberta in Edmonton. Not surprisingly, he speaks the lingo. 
I've referred to his article before. The Chinese military, thinking war 
games, have produced a plan for attacking the USA around 2008. The 
reasoning - while their economy is growing it is blatantly inefficient.
Not accurate - but a small example - it takes us in N. America say 1 
barrel of oil to produce 100 widgets. Currently the Chinese may be using 
5 barrels of oil to get the same 100 widgets.

They are aggressively courting oil producers, Middle East, Venezuela and 
up here in the Great White North. They are direct competitors to the USA 
for the same resource. The military visualize they will have to attack 
the USA to protect the safety of their own oil imports. Meanwhile, 
Petro-China is searching for oil deposits offshore etc.

It is just as improbable that the Chinese would go nuclear any more than 
the USA. Whoever presses the first button is signaling the end of 
civilization. That rather tends to put the boot to Star Wars 2, aka BMD 
(Ballistic Missile Defense). Down at the ranch Paul Martin along with 
GWB and Vicente Fox is, as we write, probably explaining to the Prez why 
he flip-flopped on this issue.

To me BMD is a non-starter :-

1. Nobody would dare go nuclear - except for that rogue in N.Korea
2. It wont work - try getting a 100% hit rate on all incoming missiles
3. Whatever budget figure the manufacturers or military come up with - 
multiply the $BILLIONS by a factor of 5 or 6 or....10. The cost will be 
astronomical to the US.
4. Canada was being asked to contribute - perhaps only 5-10% of the cost 
- BUT - 5-10% of $x BILLIONS.

So we are back to some sort of conventional war, if there is such an 
animal. That implies ground troops with the US having to 'kick a lot of 
butt'. I would suggest your legs are really going to ache before you 
have kicked sufficient butts. You kill a million PRC troops. "No 
problem", says the Central Committee, "We'll feed in one million more".

The Chinese grabbed Tibet, a great tragedy for Tibetans, and in the past 
had border skirmishes with the Indians. Currently they have shown no 
expansionist policies, matching the Nazi or Soviet patterns. Agreed they 
do not accept the policy of the 'Two Chinas', noisily shaking their 
sword in its scabbard over Formosa. And I think they mean business on 
that one. It's a 'homeland thing', not very different from the Japanese 
objecting to the Soviets, (now Russia) occupying those Japanese northern 
islands after WWII, or the latest, South Korea disputing Japanese claims 
to some remote uninhabitable islands situated between the two.

Forget the Afghan and Iraq sideshows - concentrate on a diplomatic 
solution between the USA and the PRC. A while back, Bill pointed out to 
three of us OO-ers that WG4 (ISO) had received requests from the Ukraine 
and China to comment on the OO Collections TR(Technical Report). For all 
I'm likely to use it, that section of a revised J4 Standard might just 
as well be published in Chinese !

I responded to Bill's message along the following lines. The 20th 
Century was America's century. I predict that the 21st century will be 
China's century.

I wont be around, and you wont be around in 2099 to prove me wrong !

Jimmy
```

```
jce wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:e06ee$42439131$45491f85$4979@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> signed up for.

This is true - but still, stop loss provisions are in the enlistment 
contract.  (I've seen it!  :>  )

>>>No nukes in Iraq.  Nukes in US
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> allowed them.

And why not?  If we are to stop proliferation, then *someone* has to be 
able to back up what they say with force, or people will just ignore it.

>>>Have to apply international law to Iraq.  Won't sign up for international 
>>>criminal court.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> can try next?

Wouldn't put it past some of these folks.  You asked toward the end of 
the last message, "Why do you think they're blowing us up?" or something 
like that.  Do you want these folks calling our people before a court 
where we've recognized its jurisdiction?  I certainly don't...  They 
could trump up charges, run a sham trial, and kill our own citizens - 
with the international community's approval!

>>We reduced our nuclear arsenal, just as we said we would in various 
>>treaties.  We still have them, yes.  If you think we're so immoral, then 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> the golden pathway bullshit.

So why do you stay?  (That's meant to be a serious question, not a 
smart-alecky retort...)  If this country is *so* immoral and *so* 
misguided on so many things, why do you continue to be a citizen?

>>>>The next logical step is that they are going back to doing what they have 
>>>>been prohibited from doing.  The US has had nukes for a long time - since 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> accepted Germany's apology for WWII.

I think *way* too much importance is placed on demanding "apologies" 
these days.  People are way too easily offended, and people won't stick 
by what they said.  Take Trent Lott - he praised Strom Thurmond, on the 
occasion of his birthday, for his work in the legislature.  Now, all the 
people get hysterical about the fact that Thurmond used to be for 
segregation.  Lott should have come out and said "Of course that's not 
what I said" and have been done with it.  Instead, he goes on this "mea 
culpa" tour.  The one good thing to come out of it was Bill Frist 
becoming Senate Majority Leader.

In my opinion, an apology, especially this far removed from the original 
  injustice, would be nothing more than empty words.  Focus on something 
more substantive - focus on the future.

>>See, the communists wanted to take over the world.  The fascists wanted to 
>>take over the world.  We *don't* want to take over the world, we want the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> WTF? What communists? What fascists?

The USSR and China (communists) and Italy (fascists).  Don't you know 
your WWII history?

> What freedom?  The freedom of the press doesn't exist anymore (show me a 
> newspaper that has regularly printed a really hard line article critical of 
> something serious in govt and I will revoke this statement)

There's lots of stuff in our local paper, as we have a Republican 
governor...  http://www.montgomeryadvertiser.com

>>>So why not let another country have a kick butt military and let them be 
>>>the policemen?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> WTF ? And why would we want to take them?

Because they're a communist country with nukes pointed at our homeland. 
  That doesn't bother you at all?

>>That's what the resolution said - of course, it wouldn't be the first time 
>>the UN has done something illegal either...
> 
> ?

http://www.washtimes.com/world/20050225-114514-2398r.htm

http://www.washingtontimes.com/op-ed/20040321-101405-2593r.htm

http://www.cnn.com/2005/WORLD/meast/03/22/oil.for.food/

Rape and embezzlement at the least...

>>>Who threatened who? The US put them on the Axis of Evil.  Very WWII
>>
>>Because they were already threatening us.
> 
> When.

They've threatened us for the last 12 years.  They've trained 
terrorists.  They've refused to honor the provisions of the cease-fire 
from the Gulf War.

>>>>One of the things that's great about this nation is that we have freedom, 
>>>>but we realize that it doesn't come cheap.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> kind of things.

Because there are those who would exploit our freedoms against us (and 
already have - remember those two big buildings that used to be in New 
York?).  You don't fight a war from the bench, and you don't defeat 
terrorists in court.

>>>Perhaps one should look into what causes some people to take the act of 
>>>strapping bombs to their bodies ?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Oh my [copulating] [diety].

Do you disagree with that?  They do hate us...

>>No fascist religious group is going to take away our freedoms - but their 
>>goal isn't even that, it's to take away our lives!
> 
> Fascist?

Yes - Islamo-fascists.  There's been a nice discussion of fascism here 
in the last day or so.  I think it's a rather appropriate characterization.

>>If we do not  strike back with a vengeance, then we truly *will* be less 
>>safe.  If
> 
> Strike back....err....so we are counter attacking?

In Afghanistan, yes.  In Iraq, we were being preemptive (but were 
taunted beforehand).
```

```
In article <NLP0e.585$vd.465@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>,
 "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

> >> No nukes in Iraq.  Nukes in US
> > We've had 'em since the 40's - they were trying to make them.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> allowed them.

Those were some awfully realistic simulations the French did.  So 
realistic that it was impossible to tell the difference between 192 real 
bombs and the simulations.


> >> No chemical weapons in Iraq.  Chemical weapons in US.
> > ?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> allowed them.

That isn't what the US has ever said.  We have always expressed 
disapproval of those who USE chemical weapons.  Or the development of 
chemicals by those likely to use them.

Ours are a deterrent only.  And a very effective deterrent -- the WWII 
post mortem was quite clear in the conclusion that our chemical arsenal 
kept the Wehrmacht from using theirs.


> > We reduced our nuclear arsenal, just as we said we would in various 
> > treaties.  We still have them, yes.  If you think we're so immoral, then 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the golden pathway bullshit.

That is some interesting logic you have there.  People are queueing up 
to get in because the come from worse places.  Why then, is it that 
people in the US are not queueing up to go someplace better?  (Some keep 
promising to move to Canada or France, but they never leave.)  Could it 
be that there just isn't any better place for them to go.


> > See, the communists wanted to take over the world.  The fascists wanted to 
> > take over the world.  We *don't* want to take over the world, we want the 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> something serious in govt and I will revoke this statement)

www.nytimes.com

I suppose it depends on what the meaning of 'serious' is.  You seem to 
think that the Iraq invasion was serious -- well they have been quite 
critical of it from hard news coverage to their editorial page.

Is daily regular enough?


> >> So why not let another country have a kick butt military and let them be 
> >> the policemen?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> WTF ? And why would we want to take them?

The freedom of 125m Japanese, 80m Filipinos, 60m Thais, 45m S. Koreans, 
20m Taiwanese, 18m Australians, 4m New Zealanders along with a number of 
smaller island nations in the region that would be directly threatened 
by an expansionist China.

But that is just what the US has done for the last century.  We 
guarantee freedom for people all over the world and we only ask for 
enough land to bury our dead.

What do you think we should do if they invade or friends in Taiwan?  
Doesn't our diplomatic credibility require that we support our peaceful 
friends?  Can we just toss them out the window and say 'sorry guys, you 
are screwed'?

What would you have us do if they re-invaded other allies like India or 
South Korea?  Sit idly by?
```

- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <aa2bb$423a1034$45491f85$10450@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1d3mk$22c$1@panix5.panix.com> <e7450$423b77c5$45491f85$8179@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1fufq$p4t$1@panix5.panix.com> <7f8ba$423c7605$45491f85$25525@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1mvoh$12b$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <80ac6$423f5dcf$45491f85$28434@KNOLOGY.NET> <k_L%d.5034$JK1.333977@news20.bellglobal.com> <4509e$4240dbdb$45491f85$26179@KNOLOGY.NET> <E860e.18499$nK.859275@news20.bellglobal.com> <eb36a$4242377e$45491f85$1820@KNOLOGY.NET> <7it0e.201995$pc5.114559@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <e06ee$42439131$45491f85$4979@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:e06ee$42439131$45491f85$4979@KNOLOGY.NET...

> > The US people wouldn't recognize a good democracy if one got up and bit
it
> > on the rear end.

> Yes we would - that's why we're glad we have a constitutional republic!

I must *strenuously* agree with you here.  Despite its title, the French
Republic is much more a democracy than the US, and from my three visits to
Paris I can certainly see why Jefferson was concerned about the "tyranny of
the majority".  (I can also see, after visiting Versailles, why the French
people demanded democracy!).

Because of the rules for the Initiative and Referendum process, it is
probably easier in California for just about anybody to draft a state
constitutional amendment and get sufficient signatures on the petition to
get it on the state ballot.  Frequently there will be multiple initiatives
on the same ballot that override each other.  My personal policy is to vote
NO on all statewide ballot measures unless I'm *really certain* of their
validity because my observation is that almost all of them turn out to be
cockamamie at best.  There are a whole lot of really cockamamie laws in
California.  I think there are a whole lot *more* really cockamamie laws in
France.

    -Chuck Stevens
```
