>  "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > So one might conclude that FICA is a tax that in part pays
insurance
> > > > > premiums.  And we must also adhere to the fact that FICA stands
for, "
> > > > > Federal Insurance Contributions Act".  There's that word
"insurance"
> > > > > again.  But FICA is also known as the Social Security Tax.
> > > >
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
> Medicare is not part of your 6.2 percent.  It hasn't been for 15 years.

What I showed from the text of the 1965 amendment was that
the Hospital insurance (Medicare) has had its own rate since 1965
(nearly 40 years) and that the 1983 amendment to FICA increased
the OASDI rate while leaving the hospital rate alone.

Here is the complete text from 3101 (3111 is similar), which shows
thirty years of separate rates for Medicare.

-STATUTE-
    (a) Old-age, survivors, and disability insurance
      In addition to other taxes, there is hereby imposed on the income
    of every individual a tax equal to the following percentages of the
    wages (as defined in section 3121(a)) received by him with respect
    to employment (as defined in section 3121(b)) -

    ---------------------------------------------------------------------
    In cases of wages received during: The rate shall be:
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------
     1984, 1985, 1986, or 1987         5.7 percent
     1988 or 1989                      6.06 percent
     1990 or thereafter                6.2 percent.
                     -------------------------------
    (b) Hospital insurance
      In addition to the tax imposed by the preceding subsection, there
    is hereby imposed on the income of every individual a tax equal to
    the following percentages of the wages (as defined in section
    3121(a)) received by him with respect to employment (as defined in
    section 3121(b)) -
        (1) with respect to wages received during the calendar years
      1974 through 1977, the rate shall be 0.90 percent;
        (2) with respect to wages received during the calendar year
      1978, the rate shall be 1.00 percent;
        (3) with respect to wages received during the calendar years
      1979 and 1980, the rate shall be 1.05 percent;
        (4) with respect to wages received during the calendar years
      1981 through 1984, the rate shall be 1.30 percent;
        (5) with respect to wages received during the calendar year
      1985, the rate shall be 1.35 percent; and
        (6) with respect to wages received after December 31, 1985, the
      rate shall be 1.45 percent.
    (c) Relief from taxes in cases covered by certain international
        agreements
      During any period in which there is in effect an agreement
    entered into pursuant to section 233 of the Social Security Act
    with any foreign country, wages received by or paid to an
    individual shall be exempt from the taxes imposed by this section
    to the extent that such wages are subject under such agreement to
    taxes or contributions for similar purposes under the social
    security system of such foreign country.

This is the text of the 1977 amendment, which is the last noted
change for subsection (b).

      1977 - Subsec. (a). Pub. L. 95-216, Sec. 101(a)(1), substituted
    [...].
      Subsec. (b). Pub. L. 95-216, Sec. 101(b)(1), substituted ''wages
    received during the calendar year 1978, the rate shall be 1.00
    percent'' for ''wages received during the calendar years 1978
    through 1980, the rate shall be 1.10 percent'' in par. (2),
    substituted ''wages received during the calendar years 1979 and
    1980, the rate shall be 1.05 percent'' for ''wages received during
    the calendar years 1981 through 1985, the rate shall be 1.35
    percent'', in par. (3), substituted ''wages received during the
    calendar years 1981 through 1984, the rate shall be 1.30 percent''
    for ''wages received after December 31, 1985, the rate shall be
    1.50 percent'' in par. (4), and added pars. (5) and (6).

There is a discrepancy between the text shown for the amendment
and the text of the statute. The statute shows lower rates. I can not
explain the discrepancy. Perhaps, only substantive changes of
amendments are shown and an amendment, after 1977, reducing
the rates for hospital insurance was not considered substantive.
```

news:rclo31tpf4e33e96qemkbieb9s67pea72j@4ax.com...
> On 18 Mar 2005 11:43:01 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:
<snip>
> Don't give in so easily Doc.  In fact part of Social Security is
> insurance.  See http://www.ssa.gov/disability/ and part way down the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As someone currently receiving SSDI "benefits" - I can confirm that this is/was 
treated as "insurance" and that the benefits that I receive are based on the 
amount that I "paid into" Social Security (for the last 5 years that I was 
working).  In addition, it "expires" when I turn 65 - but that I will THEN get 
(or at least be eligible for)  "retirment benefits".  One other thing that comes 
out of this are "surviver benefits" (for children and spouces) as well as 
minimal funeral benefits.

Having said all of this, I still considered FICA a "tax" rather than a "premium 
payment" - when I was paying it.  However, I am not certain what difference this 
makes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Social Security

_(thread depth: 55)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-18T19:40:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1fb19$35u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <1110912158.676799.165270@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <d17bfs$ldn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5583a$4237957a$45491f85$14182@KNOLOGY.NET> <d191kk$dvp$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-23003F.09392918032005@ispnews.usenetserver.com> <113m8rpp5uj8ifa@corp.supernews.com>`

```
The Social Security money is a tax because it is a mandatory payment of money
from us to the government.   That is not sufficient reason for it not to also be
an insurance payment.

Politically, its design was for it to be an insurance program, and that's how it
was sold.    You can be currently insured if you are paying in now, or fully
insured if you have paid 40 quarters.

One big difference between Social Security and a mandatory private insurance
program is that private companies have more stringent fiduciary requirements.  
Another big difference is that the politicians set the rates, not businessmen.
 The third big difference is that the money taken in by SS taxes is spent by the
federal government.   If there is a surplus, more tanks can be purchased.  
There is some accounting going back and forth, but that surplus is taken into
account when determining our deficit.    The line between Social Security and
the rest of the Federal government has been getting more and more blurry over
the decades.   Nobody talks about the military going bankrupt.   The Military,
social security, Medicare, etc.  are all federal expenditures that are paid for
by taxes.

With the sales job that social security is an investment, there should never
have been people complaining about double dipping.   So what if we have a
government job or a military job that invests in a separate retirement plan?  
What we pay for should be what we get.    It is interesting that illegal aliens
pay social security into fake accounts, but are unlikely to get that money back.
  If it were a retirement plan, they should get what they paid for.

The "what we pay is what we get" doesn't mean that married people should get
more than non married people get.    Self employed people appear to pay more
than employed people do - but this is a fiction.   When your company pays half
of your social security, it is really paying all of it as part of the cost of
hiring you - and mentioning half of it on your paycheck.    But people believe
that they are only paying half of this tax.
```

> IIRC Mr. Dwarf, you also claimed it was not a tax.  I believe you
said
> it was some form of 'insurance' program.

Or perhaps that was something that _I_ said: that it was cheaper than
the riots of starving people.

> That would make funds deposited therein to be 'premiums', assuming
you
> were correct in your description.

But why do you think it must be just one thing.  It is a tax on the
people who pay the taxes, it is an investment by the authorities on
maintaining a stable city, it is an income for the people who receive
it.

Of the taxes you pay, a part is for services you receive, a part is a
'premium' for insuring peace and stability (or war when Bush gets his
way), a part is to pay for the running of the state, if some is
specifically tagged for your own retirement than that may be an
'investment' for the future.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-14T19:38:38-08:00

- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <1110823768.373645.168100@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <d14nlk$2i2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5ed2b$42363d24$45491f85$6588@KNOLOGY.NET> <CasZd.683926$6l.230801@pd7tw2no>`

```
> And Richard, "Myth".

Please do not misrepresent what I may, or may not, say.

In many cases much of what is mythology and/or (what are known today
as) religious writing may well be based on actual people and happening.
 The fact is that it is likely that many christians would refer to,
say, the Greek Pantheon or the Hindu dhama, as mythology while
maintaining that _their_ writing were true.

I see nothing to choose between these, and many others.  Parts are
myths and parts are most likely based on real (natural) people and
events, but exagerated and confused by being passed down orally.

For parts of the writings I see no reason, for example, why people of a
few thousand years ago could not have been as inventive in their story
telling as the many thousands of authors are today. Noah's Ark is just
the Star Trek of C20BCE.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1769l$ig9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <1110823768.373645.168100@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <d14nlk$2i2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d174mh$mgv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

On 15-Mar-2005, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> > Not according to the church.
>
> Which "the church"?  "The Church of Jesus Christ" recognizes the simplest of
> legally-binding civil ceremonies as having as much weight as a wedding
> celebrated by the highest-ranking minister in the denomination.

And Roman Catholics have this as the one sacrament that doesn't require a Priest
to perform.   But most, if not all churches recognize non-church marriages for a
very practical reason - otherwise people who don't have access to clergy can't
get married.    But these non-church marriages do not give special authority to
the state.   The marriage can be performed by virtually anybody (or just by the
couple themselves), and does not have to be accepted by the state in order to be
valid.

And many, if not most churches do not accept *all* marriages that all states
accept.

Another thing that I don't like to see is people who decide their marital status
based upon such things as Social Security benefits.    Don't make it a legal
issue what an older couple decide to call their status.   If marriage is a
sacrament - then it's not the government's business.   If it's just what people
call themselves, then it also should not determine legal status.

Interesting - legally if a couple in Colorado represent themselves as married -
they are married.   But I haven't heard of anybody losing social security
benefits by doing so.   It will effect inheritance and can require divorce to
end though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-15T10:49:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d17and$qde$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <1110823768.373645.168100@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <d14nlk$2i2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d174mh$mgv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d1769l$ig9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:d1769l$ig9$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> And Roman Catholics have this as the one sacrament that doesn't require a
Priest
> to perform.

No, I don't think that's quite right.  I think there's more than one.

The Bull "Exultate Deo" of Pope Eugene IV (1388-1447) states, in part, on
baptism:  "The minister of this sacrament is the priest, to whom it belongs
to baptize by reason of his office.  In case of necessity, however, not only
a priest or deacon, but even a layman or woman, nay, even a pagan or heretic
can baptize, provided he observes the form used by the Church and intends to
perform what the Church performs. ..."

Note also that there are two sacraments a mere priest isn't allowed to
perform:  confirmation and ordination. Both require a bishop.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

> > And Roman Catholics have this as the one sacrament that doesn't require a

…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> perform:  confirmation and ordination. Both require a bishop.

My mistake - what I should have said is that the priest does *not* perform the
sacrament of marriage.   He witnesses it.   I learned this while attending a
friend's wedding and the Priest explained it - my education in Roman Catholic
sacraments is quite limited.

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1110084113.380707.86700@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
>> Boomers, aged 40 to 60, are natural hypocrites and
>> synchophants (puritans) who insincerely support the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>> The under-20 generation are sincere patriots.
Because they've not lived any of their formative years under a non Bush 
government.

I think you have all your ideas backwards anyway....as Churchill didn't 
actually say " "If you're not Liberal when you're 25, you have no heart. If 
you're not Conservative when you're 35, you have no brain."


> Well there yer go.  All you need is to be under 20 and that guarantees
> sincerity.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What was wrong with Korea ? Not finishing the job and nuking China ????

Sooner or later _all_ americans will realize that Europe will always be 
greatful for WWII support, even if we don't feel like having to hear about 
it everytime a decision needs to be made in world politics.

In a similar vein, the world needs to realize that Germans are not all 
Nazi's anymore. The French aren't anti american bigots.  I think every 
nation has paid dearly for the price for war -whether needed or not.  The 
period between 1939 - 1954 was not fun for anyone in the UK.....
The sheer numbers of WWII when compared to Iraq are astounding....the thing 
I never understand is how many of the Americans I talk to do not realize 
that at that time the USSR was an ally - I guess that speaks to the success 
of the cold war education system.   Even fewer seem aware of the Chinese 
death toll..

Source: bbc.

Civilian and Military Deaths in the Second World War

Countries  Total Deaths  % of Pre-war Population  Military Deaths  Civilian 
Deaths
USSR  20,600,000  10.40%  13,600,000  7,000,000
China  10,000,000  2.00%  -  -

Poland  6,123,000  17.20%  123,000  6,000,000
Japan  2,000,000  2.70%  -  -
Yugoslavia  1,706,000  10.90%  -  -
France  810,000  1.90%  340,000  470,000
Greece  520,000  7.20%  -  -

>> The under-20 generation are sincere patriots.

> Because they've not lived any of their formative years under a non
Bush
> government.

I know dozens of 'under-20' that have lived _all_ of their years under
'non Bush government'.

Maybe some, maybe most, American teens may be 'sincere patriots' some
others may not even be patriots at all.

> I think you have all your ideas backwards anyway....

In what way do you know what 'all my ideas' are ?

> as Churchill didn't
> actually say " "If you're not Liberal when you're 25, you have no
heart. If
> you're not Conservative when you're 35, you have no brain."

In what way is what Churchill said relevant at all, and more especially
to what you imagine my ideas to be ?

And anyway, why do you think that Churchill did not say that ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-07T05:58:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y4SWd.105301$pc5.16228@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <Z_oVd.98002$pc5.15438@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <d05s59$4ho$1@panix5.panix.com> <52if2117tg2ol332joj4ktvcmq8fgdld5s@4ax.com> <d09le6$1m8$1@panix5.panix.com> <6m9i21hb0sto5csvhs6blknijujuorjone@4ax.com> <6e3b2$42292eba$45491f85$22262@KNOLOGY.NET> <255j215394bc0u68ci6m9sde4vvc2g8oqh@4ax.com> <1110084113.380707.86700@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <yaRWd.130926$qB6.86732@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <1110173190.348982.235710@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1110173190.348982.235710@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>>> The under-20 generation are sincere patriots.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'non Bush government'.

I define formative years to be high school age - that point where most start 
challenging ideas. Assuming they live in the US,  that would mean that they 
lived under Bush using this criteria.
I understand that people in NZ haven't lived directly under a Bush govt, but 
I'd challenge you if you said that they weren't impacted by it.

> Maybe some, maybe most, American teens may be 'sincere patriots' some
> others may not even be patriots at all.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> In what way do you know what 'all my ideas' are ?
Ok - so I messed up with the criteria for a response.  This was a response a 
section of the post to which you were responding.  It has a single '>' not a 
double '>>'
"You" in this instance was RW.  Answering the same question, I don't, I'm 
piecing them together post by post and using the evidence in the particular 
post and inferring.  To be fair, 'all' means within the scope of the post.

>> as Churchill didn't
>> actually say " "If you're not Liberal when you're 25, you have no
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> to what you imagine my ideas to be ?
Because the general notion of the quote was that the young tend to be 
liberal whereas the elders tend to be conservative.  The opinions on the 
state of Iraq in the US generally run along these lines.


> And anyway, why do you think that Churchill did not say that ?
Because there is no evidence that he did? He may have well said it in 
private discourse but I have no evidence of this neither being alive not his 
secretary at the time.

JCE
```

```
> I understand that people in NZ haven't lived directly under a Bush
govt, but

Or even indirectly.  He ain't the boss of me.

> I'd challenge you if you said that they weren't impacted by it.

Certainly not into making them 'sincere patriots of the USA', probably
the complete reverse.

I doubt is more than a very small percent know or care about US
politics, we do have our own, you know.

JCE>> I think you have all your ideas backwards anyway....

>  "You" in this instance was RW.

Oh, in that case I _completely_ agree with you   ;-)

> To be fair, 'all' means within the scope of the post.

Hmmm, be careful.  Many take 'all' to mean 'all'.  In fact just saying
'your ideas' means 'all' unless you explicitly restrict it.

> Because the general notion of the quote was that the young tend to be
> liberal whereas the elders tend to be conservative.

Does that mean you think my ideas are 'liberal' or 'conservative' ?

More importantly, how would you judge my ideas against the norm for New
Zealand.  What you think as liberal, I may see as conservative.
```

jce wrote:

Thank goodness for Auntie Beeb. Following is a set of statistics I've 
always wanted to find. Better go looking at the BBC for other info.

> 
> Source: bbc.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Canadian figures sure look small at 0.3%, considering they 'policed' the 
Atlantic for merchant convoys and their bomber crews served as part of 
Bomber Command. (In case I miss it and he protests - Donald's dad was an 
RCAF fighter pilot).

One of those nice reminiscing articles in yesterday's paper. Two ladies, 
now 80 plus, recounting their days back in the 40's - one served as a 
parachute packer.

Canada as a whole was quite useful for air training but Alberta 
particularly for the southern landscape, (wide, open and clear skies), 
flowing down to Montana. We had the Commonwealth Air Training Program 
with some 18 flying schools in Alberta. Crews were trained from 23 
countries. (My parents next-door-neighbour's son did his navigation 
training here - hated it ! Back then Calgary looked like the set for the 
Gary Cooper movie 'High Noon'). BTW one lady made specific reference 
that the Aussies wore a black uniform - Somebody from Oz-land is that 
still the case or do they wear an 'RAF' blue.

As one of the ladies said Calgary was then awash in military uniforms, 
more than any other city in Canada. She recalls VE or VJ-Day - she 
didn't specify which, where they went snaking through downtown Calgary 
in a joyous conga. Then puff ! Suddenly all the lads were gone.

Reading Daniel and Robert - I think they have got so much wrong about 
Iraq. Presidents get voted in on domestic issues, ('How much are you 
going to tax us ?") - who gives a damn about foreign policy. It takes 
just too much typing to explain - so I'll give it a miss.

Guys forget Iraq - it's smalltime, as probably are Iran and N. Korea.
How's about this, just like Richard pointed out the cause for the Pearl 
Harbor attack :-

'To ensure oil supplies, the Peoples Republic will probably have to go 
to war with the USA by 2008".

Sez who ? The party sponsored Chinese Military publishing house.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
In article <6m9i21hb0sto5csvhs6blknijujuorjone@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 4 Mar 2005 07:48:06 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>I hate that phrase. It's most often used by conformists.

I am not sure of it is most often used, Mr Wagner... but when I am 
presented with it I frequently respond with '... but not *too* far 
outside, right?'... and the response is, often, a vigorous and 
enthusiastic 'Yes!'.

>
>>>In the world of contracting,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Not in my experience.

Our experiences differ, then... and a Good Thing, too, since if I wanted 
only agreement I would speak only with my mirror.  But back to the point 
of contractors being expected to do the unexpected... there's a task that 
is too 'dirty'/ugly/intricate/whatever for anyone else in the shop to have 
accomplished and that is, quite often, the task that the contractor is 
expected to do.  Are you saying that the contractor is expected to fail, 
just like everyone else?

>
>>>Look to your
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
><laughiing> Very good answer.

Glad you enjoyed.

>
>>>They got around company
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Wrong.

Mr Wagner, my memory is, I admit, porous... but not so porous that I have 
misremembered what wires were hooked to what components.

>At some point your laptop must be connected to the network.
>Otherwise, there was no point to the download. A virus running on your
>laptop could then do its damage.

Mr Wagner, please read back and see where I mentioned that I downloaded... 
and then post it here.  The problem I was trying to address with my 
modemed-out access was one of *research*... this all started with mention 
of web-based manuals, remember?  I'd modem out to websites and read things 
or I'd research this group via DejaNews... errrrr, Google.  I did not need 
to download.

>
>>>In places that groked
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>are not that scarce. Problem is, the selection process is screwed up.

Plural majestatus est, Mr Wagner... though from what I've noticed most of 
the folks who post here differ from most of the folks I've worked with in 
meatspace; I've run across folks - more than once, if you can believe it! 
- who come into work at 8:am, turn away from the keyboard at precisely 
10:am to read the paper and drink coffee for 15 minutes, hit the cafeteria 
at exactly noon for an hour at the same table with the same crew to talk 
about sports or the stockmarket or house-remodelling, back to the desk at 
1:pm, more newspaper at 3:pm and at 5:01pm you could set fire to their 
seat and not singe a hair on their backs.

Granted I am not with them at times to verify it... but I'd be willing to 
wager that it is a rare day, indeed, that these folks come up with a new 
slant on a problem or a different, clear resolution of a difficulty as 
they shower... or that they sit up in bed, bolt upright, at 2:am and say 
'*THAT'S* how I deal with it!'... and, for whatever reasons, I think that 
readers here have more of those kinds of moments than the others I have 
described do.

DD
```

On 5 Mar 2005 10:32:32 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <6m9i21hb0sto5csvhs6blknijujuorjone@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>>>>In the world of contracting,
>>>>you're expected to click your heels and say Yes Sir.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>expected to do.

In most places where I worked as contractor, technical plans were
drawn up by SMEs, usually employees, before contractors were brought
in. Lacking a detailed plan, how would they know how many bodies and
what skills to bring in? Contractors then executed the plan. 

At a few places, when I had idle time, I solved technical problems
that others hadn't been able to, or had the time to, figure out. These
tasks weren't assigned, nor the results expected. 

> Are you saying that the contractor is expected to fail, 
>just like everyone else?

I haven't seen everyone, or hardly anyone, fail. Projects were
finished on-time and on-budget; results were as requested.

You must work in really screwed-up companies.

>>At some point your laptop must be connected to the network.
>>Otherwise, there was no point to the download. A virus running on your
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>to download.

I described support people downloading device drivers and fix packs.

> from what I've noticed most of 
>the folks who post here differ from most of the folks I've worked with in 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>described do.

I keep regular hours AND have off-duty flashes of insight. You didn't
show a connection between long/irregular hours at the office and ideas
that arrive at 2:am.
```

JCE

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T17:07:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ksggF5opqicU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <cvo5k2$mas$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109446377.120385.263750@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <cvvlgu$838$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <38j1h3F5ocqh7U1@individual.net> <nx3Vd.540924$8l.239854@pd7tw1no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:nx3Vd.540924$8l.239854@pd7tw1no...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> > "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > investigation, I don't care. It is worth waiting for. Hurried solutions
are
> > half arsed solutions that require constant attention and cost more in
the
> > long run than doing it properly in the first place.)
> >
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I s'pose I should really 'fess up that "do it once; do it right" was an
official Government slogan in New Zealand back in the 70's.

There was concern in some circles that the  laid back Kiwi attitude of
"She'll be right..." was not helping our perceived efficiency in the foreign
markets we were seeking to penetrate. It is pretty hard to change a National
characteristic, but several million dollars of taxpayers money were
funnelled into intensive advertising and training campaigns.  I don't know
how effective it was (how would you measure it?), but it is certainly true
that today we are recognised as efficient and skilful in the things we
export (mainly movies... <g>), and we are managing to work our way back to
prosperity without dependence on sheep and cattle only.

It isn't always possible to 'do it once; do it right', but it is certainly
worth trying for, and there is no doubt in my mind that it beats the 'fix
what you can see and get it working until the next time it breaks down'
approach.

Pete.
```

> >It appears that the future is with lowered standards.
>
> The future is with the most efficient. Mainframe  standards are highly

For various values of efficient.

For some people, efficiency is using binary instead of display.

For others, it is "measure twice, cut once".

Mainframe testing standards are designed around the high cost of being wrong.
Internet testing standards are designed around the high cost of being late.

- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <1109354625.353288.324170@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <cvnq4u$n5m$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109361297.008867.304890@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <cvo5k2$mas$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109369427.368192.123030@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <889121ppm9jcnu6tii0glcuetkvi1vnf4g@4ax.com> <cvvlbv$833$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ul721hctc4dh0e0267b3u9uc460340ho3@4ax.com> <d02382$num$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5oaa21hojugqjcpbib0m3mftoifchue5as@4ax.com>`

```
> Mainframe testing standards are designed around the cost
> of covering your ass if things go wrong. They have nothing
> to do with efficiency or corporate benefit. It's all about the
> high cost of politics.

If things do go wrong then there can be great losses, a large
disbenefit to the corpoarte and a high cost.

Ensuring that things don't go wrong seems to be an excellent objective.

The balance should be the total cost per year of _anything_ going wrong
including consequential losses, such as corporate confidence comapred
to the cost of maintaining high testing standards to ensure nothing
does go wrong (or as little as possible).

It seems that you don't care about that.  You simply like to denigrate
everything that you haven't personally done, the intent seems to be
that everyone except Robert is wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>

- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <1109354625.353288.324170@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <cvnq4u$n5m$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109361297.008867.304890@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <cvo5k2$mas$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109369427.368192.123030@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <889121ppm9jcnu6tii0glcuetkvi1vnf4g@4ax.com> <cvvlbv$833$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ul721hctc4dh0e0267b3u9uc460340ho3@4ax.com> <d02382$num$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5oaa21hojugqjcpbib0m3mftoifchue5as@4ax.com> <1109742733.926913.286230@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1109742733.926913.286230@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
>> Mainframe testing standards are designed around the cost
>> of covering your ass if things go wrong. They have nothing
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> that everyone except Robert is wrong.

You're confusing the real world and the Utopia that people search for.

Requirements are not worked on until they are signed off.  In the Utopian 
world this is to "capture ownership of a quality document", in the real 
world it's "I captured your signature and you're are responsible"

It used to be that you worked stuff down and the upper levels signed off 
acceptace......
Now its stuff gets worked down once the lower levels have forced you to 
accept responsibility......

Same thing but different.

Why is it that a system that has been working fine for 40 years now has to 
make hundreds of changes for SOX?  How is that _NOT_ a cya statement.  That 
_IS_ precisely whas SOX is about.

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T17:29:19+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38ktp3F5ph2ucU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <1109354625.353288.324170@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <cvnq4u$n5m$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109361297.008867.304890@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <cvo5k2$mas$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109369427.368192.123030@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <889121ppm9jcnu6tii0glcuetkvi1vnf4g@4ax.com> <cvvlbv$833$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ul721hctc4dh0e0267b3u9uc460340ho3@4ax.com> <d02382$num$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:d02382$num$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 28-Feb-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
>
> Mainframe testing standards are designed around the high cost of being
wrong.
> Internet testing standards are designed around the high cost of being
late.
>

A very succinct,  though not entirely accurate, statement.

It is not entirely accurate because it includes two generalizations.

NOT ALL mainframe testing standards are designed around the high cost of
being wrong, and NOT ALL internet testing standards are designed around the
high cost of being late.

(nevertheless, it was a very nice turn of phrase, Howard... <g>)

In fact, some testing  standards in both camps are not concerned with the
costs of tardiness, inaccuracy , or any form of cost at all.

They simply want a standard. It makes them feel better, and when people ask,
they can say, "Well, of course, we're a professional shop so we have our
testing standards...". (Besides, everybody knows that standards are a Good
Thing, so better have some...).

That's why there are good and bad standards.

Often the testing standards are bypassed because there isn't time to do
comprehensive regression testing (when it is actually needed) and this
happens in mainframe shops just the same as it does with  web development.

The cost of being wrong in a web environment can be just as devastating as
it is in a mainframe environment (especially if it is credit card processing
that wasn't exhaustively tested.)

Good shops in both camps will consider all of the costs of failure (both in
terms of time and accuracy) and develop a proper testing plan accordingly;
it is not the prerogative of one type of shop to get it right (or wrong).

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT : Credibility of the Web

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T04:06:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JSRUd.530611$8l.59693@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<0u962152ou49op1jp26t237mlnogchvdk5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <1109369817.028632.317290@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <cvpslc$5r0$1@panix5.panix.com> <38cktfF5l4mtrU1@individual.net> <cvrfj0$bqp$1@panix2.panix.com> <38cvapF5mj2j4U1@individual.net> <7ieUd.518617$8l.41818@pd7tw1no> <38f1p4F5mhgr6U1@individual.net> <ZZsUd.520920$Xk.490426@pd7tw3no> <0u962152ou49op1jp26t237mlnogchvdk5@4ax.com>`

```
Jeff York wrote:
> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Jeff,

No more dispute your above statement than I do Pete's about three 
sources for the 'USA/English' thing. Doing a google "Churchill 
Quotations" I came up with the reference to Bessie.

But here's what troubles me. Anybody, but anybody can stick stuff on the 
Web. You might find sites on programming, history, politics or whatever. 
A theme catches your eye and you follow through reading the author's set 
of articles. 'Bullshit Baffles Brains' - he's probably got umpteen 
letters after his name, showing his academic background. OK be cautious 
on that stuff - not all geniuses went to a university - but 
nevertheless, at least as a sign, does indicate he/she has some credibility.

Now when we go looking up 'factual' stuff, geography, history, political 
history etc., we do expect them to be FACTUAL. No good somebody 
producing a geography site and giving me the square mileage of Texas, 
after they have dropped a couple of noughts off the end !

Certainly Quotations, at least I think so, fit into the category of FACTUAL.

1. USA/England - two countries separated by a common language.

Yes, one common theme covering Wilde, Shaw and Churchill - all three, 
witty, writers and belonged to the Edwardian era. Pete concluded, 
'Churchill shown as the source in the Times' - kind of negates a 
reference to the other two doesn't it. As witty as this trio was - is it 
just sheer COINCIDENCE each of them hit on the same phrase, or slight 
variation therof ?

Pete - I said I thought the quote was paraphrased - try a mix on the 
following, ticking away there in my memory cells, "The USA and Britain, 
/two of the world's great English speaking peoples/ two English speaking 
democracies/ two great democracies/.... are separated by a common language".

Perhaps I've blown a fuse and the ole memory cells have let me down - 
but........

2. Our Bessie and Drunk Winnie.

 From what I wrote, you'll see I had the impression it had to be during 
his 'Coventry period' while out of office. As told to me, not read as a 
source, the lady could be a Bonham-Carter or perhaps an Astor.

Now the Irish are of two sorts when mellow or pissed - Funny Ha Ha - 
that's my brother and myself - anyway at 6' plus we don't need to get 
into a fight. Then there's the Irish about 3' tall - give 'em a couple 
of jars and they become "Funny Peculiar" and want to fight every bugger 
in sight, regardless of height !

 From my readings on the man, Churchill, give him some tipple and with 
his cherubic smile he became Funny Ha Ha. I received third-hand he could 
be a miserable SOB when sober. A wealthy couple in Surrey had a chance 
to dine with him and in a morose mood, (probably no drink, or they 
rationed it), they couldn't get any conversation out of him at all.

With Montgomery he once visited the Canadian 3rd Division going up 
through Belgium into Holland. Of course Monty's edict, no booze or 
smoking. Churchill was pissed off before he even got there. One Canadian 
Lt. Col. took pity on the PM and offered to get him a mug of tea. 
Grudgingly he accepted. As soon as he tasted it, there was a cherubic 
smile on his chops - his tea was undiluted brandy - a mug full !

You were very diplomatic about Bessie suggesting she was 'plain' - let's 
tell it like it was - she was fat and bloody ugly, and represented 
constituents from oop North I think.

Now here's my problem; post WW2 - Churchill was either Conservative 
opposition or in power as PM. But whichever, he was sat on the opposite 
side of the Commons to Bessie who was a Labour MP. Had they been 
shouting across the chamber at one another then it should be possible to 
verify 100% from Hansard.

If it isn't recorded in Hansard then where the hell did these two get 
together. It's hardly likely they socialized. Now my quote indicates a 
'lady' sat in the same team as Winston either Conservative or Liberal.

He could be hard; got rid of Dowding and Kiwi Keith Park after they had 
won the Battle of Britain. Similarly he gave enough generals the boot 
over the Middle East fiasco until Monty turned it around at El Alemein.
However he was a Victorian/Edwardian gentleman, and even if pissed I 
find it hard to see him using a phrase like "you are extremely ugly";
more likely in Victorian romantic style, "Madam, you are beautiful", 
then follow that with the knife in the next sentence.

Either quote - doesn't really matter a tuppenny cuss - but would be 
satisfying to really know.

Read up the Web on evolution of man. My great favourite was the book 
'Lucy'. As you well know, on about a five year cycle, and sometimes 
less, we get updates on man's origins and dinosaurs and how the latter 
did/didn't or died this way or that way. Each move on these makes 
previous articles way out of date and nobody is cataloguing this stuff 
for deletion.

Couple of week's back saw that over a period of years Google will be 
digitizing some of the world's great libraries, can't remember list but 
did include Smithsonian and the British Museum I think. What a great 
opportunity for us all when it is available.

However, so now you want to read something written by Herodotus. My 
Greek isn't up to snuff - how is yours ? So we will be dependent upon 
qualified translators. 'Qualified' ? Without knowing specifics, we know 
that there are sometimes problems translating ancient texts and 
interpretation of certain words and focusing in on *exactly* the right 
word or phrase in English.

We accept the translator's text. He gets it wrong - only shows up some 5 
years later when another language expert disputes his translation.

Here's an interesting one, but not the Web. About three months back read 
about this kid 10-12 years of age, (in UK, USA or Canada), who is of 
Polish descent. His parents buy him the set of Encyclopedia Britannica 
plus paid updates. New volume arrives covering the "Ps" so he devours 
all there is written about Poland. He sends a 135 itemized list of 
factual errors to EB. The only one I recall, EB said there was/wasn't a 
particular species of bison in Poland. From other readings, and I think 
he may have seen the animals on a vacation back in Poland, he 
contradicts them saying there are/there are not. EB editors acknowledge 
his list of errors !

So can we ever believe the Internet ?

Jimmy
```

Could be..  Might not be..  All I know is that the version that I
quoted (Bessie) is one that I've known for the better part of 40 years
- *long* before the internet was even a twinkle in anyone's eye..

As to your question in the first post of this thread regarding how WSC
(Con) and BB (Lab) could ever meet outside the debating chamber - you
must remember that the House of Commons (and Lords for that matter) is
also one of the best Clubs, if not *the* best, in London and it's not
unreasonable to assume that such an exchange would have taken place
somewhere like the Members' Dining Room, or, much more likely, in one
of the several bars (which never close) housed in the Palace of
Westminster..

>Use of Web :-
>
>1. Search for a quotation, text and who said it
>2. Having got the quotation now find another site which rejects its 
>accuracy or existence

The satirical magazine "Private Eye" used to run a column based round
quotes from the late Harold Wilson during his time as Prime Minister..
They would post a quotation from one of his speeches or exchanges in
the House - taken from Hansard - followed by another also from Hansard
where he had stated the exact opposite..  :)
```

this evening.

searched it for the partial string 'dialo' and found not only the OED 
citing but a second use of 'dialogue' as a verb.  I'll try to remember to 
post that, as well.

DD

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d01qlh$jks$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <38j2kgF5mnetsU1@individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

So that covers 1/3 of the original sources.... =)

Of course, students reading literature of historical importance, are often 
given a class devoted purely to Shakespearean English -due in part because 
much of it has fallen out of common usage (but will still generally be in 
the OED b/c IT IS Shakespeare).
It is hard to remove words used by Shakespeare from dictionaries, but it 
might be time to move on... but as I said, this entire thread in clc may 
just have enough instances of the use dialogue as a verb that it gets to 
live when they scan for non technical usage.

Ninety-eight percent of the Usage Panel rejects the sentence Critics have 
charged that the department was remiss in not trying to dialogue with 
representatives of the community before hiring the new officers.
I am presuming as it was in the section "dialogue" that the bureaucratise 
was the reason for the rejection, not that they really thought anyone was 
remiss.

Congratulations in standing up for the rights of the minority :-)

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Treeviews

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T11:32:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d025ei$575$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7iLSd.472515$8l.105860@pd7tw1no> <38j2kgF5mnetsU1@individual.net> <d01qlh$jks$1@panix5.panix.com> <xa%Ud.122575$qB6.19815@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
In article <xa%Ud.122575$qB6.19815@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>,
jce <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d01qlh$jks$1@panix5.panix.com...
>> In article <38j2kgF5mnetsU1@individual.net>,
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>So that covers 1/3 of the original sources.... =)

One down, two to go... patience is a virtue, some say.

>
>Of course, students reading literature of historical importance, are often 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>live when they scan for non technical usage.

As I recall This All Began when Ms Fitton cited her instructor as saying 
that 'dialogue is the correct verb'; certain posters then asserted that 
'dialogue' was not a verb or if it was a verb then it was the result of 
the American habit of verbing nouns.  The cites I provided were to 
indicate that according to the Merriam-Webster, American Heritage and 
Oxford English dictionaries 'dialogue' is a verb and that the OED gave 
three cites from non-American authors to show this use.

>
>Ninety-eight percent of the Usage Panel rejects the sentence Critics have 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>remiss.

Perhaps the Usage Panel might do well to read more Shakespeare, Coleridge 
and Carlyle... but whether the word is used as such is one thing, whether 
it is defined as such might be another.

>
>Congratulations in standing up for the rights of the minority :-)

I remember a seminar I had in college, we were reading one of Plato's 
Socratic dialogues and the talk turned to the nature of 'being right' in a 
democracy... I can't recall what generated it but the Senior Tutor (the 
college called them 'tutors', not 'professors', and the seminar consisted 
of eighteen students and two tutors, one Junior and one Senior) turned to 
me and said 'That's well and good but suppose we lived in a society of one 
hundred people; the other ninety-eight believed that Blacks were inferior 
to Whites but we believe the two were equal... we would be right, wouldn't 
we?'

I replied 'Not according to the other ninety-eight, no.'

With a bit more energy he repeated 'But we would be *right*, wouldn't we?'

'Not according to the other ninety-eight, no.'

'But *we* would be *right*, *wouldn't* we?'

'Not According To The Other Ninety-Eight, No.'

... and he just... stopped, and stared at the text in front of him... in 
the uncomfortable silence a few other folks picked up some threads of the 
conversation and began to weave with them... but a moment or two later he 
looked at me and said 'That was well-handled, you did that quite 
properly.', to which I responded with a simple 'Thank you.'

DD
```

- **Date:** 2005-03-01T21:50:30+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<4224d512$0$14978$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<421dc03e$0$10686$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <111fefpjskr1664@corp.supernews.com> <111g0vt6l5g7e12@corp.supernews.com> <9RFya5KeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <111hkq8lmn0rva8@corp.supernews.com> <111hvadtpeu3e4c@corp.supernews.com> <4219b56b$0$39323$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com> <111k7fimf7vag86@corp.supernews.com> <111njuk74313u0e@corp.supernews.com> <421da064$0$17075$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <421db712$0$95898$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com> <421dc03e$0$10686$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
ok, i made some optimizations on my Ada solutions:
- i use another method to read the file
- i read the file as a whole in an array then make the processing on the 
array

the new version is less portable, since it does not read characters but 
rather bytes, so it won't work with non-ascii input, but it is the same 
for many other candidates i think.

timing result:
0.16 seconds on my Athlon XP2000 (pseudo 2GHz) divided into 0.019s 
reading the file, and 0.14s processing data (i do not count the output).

the updated version can be found here:
http://www.stochastique.net/~rien/dev/ada/bible/bible_new.adb
```

.    On  27.02.05

     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  42223644$0$39336$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com
  about  Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)


DF> and does it neater than LR's cryptic std:cin>>line

  well, this is standard C++, and not cryptic at all for someone who  
has learned the language.


>>   As to map, I find this in the source program, which needs some
>> explanation from a Fortran programmer:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

DF> It allows the text to be formed into words as array of chars, and
DF> then in the quicksort the words are accessed/sorted using Fortran's
DF> string syntax.

   Sure, I got that, but I wondered about the "map" and "union".

   Besides, you have proven that Fortran is still alive and can even  
be used for string processing, and this "word processing challenge"  
has prompted quite a number of people to learn or relearn something  
they didn't do regularly. But disputes like "my language is better  
than yours" are not really my thing, and running time for one very  
specific text file cannot be the only criterion.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T07:25:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9S5GboluflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<G1PPd.2059185$f47.360893@news.easynews.com> <9S16wtFPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <bk3a2152bqs8c86e2ootmn0fb0buutqte4@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  02.03.05
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  bk3a2152bqs8c86e2ootmn0fb0buutqte4@4ax.com
  about  Re: Cross-language challenge (of POSSIBLE interest)


>> running time for one very
>> specific text file cannot be the only criterion.

RW> Program logic was not specific to the Christian Bible. Any large text
RW> file would produce very similar results.

   Well, not really. The specifications were very specific and do not  
apply to any text and any language, and the solutions offered in the  
race for speed would cause a slow down with larger texts.

RW> In general, the task was to parse and index words.

   The idea to use the number of words in a text as a measure for the  
size of that text is very specific to then english language which, as  
an isolating language, does not have many long words. In many other  
languages, that obsession makes no sense.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Eine ganze Milchstraï¿½e von Einfï¿½llen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

- **References:** `<111hvadtpeu3e4c@corp.supernews.com> <4219b56b$0$39323$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com> <111k7fimf7vag86@corp.supernews.com> <111njuk74313u0e@corp.supernews.com> <421da064$0$17075$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be> <421db712$0$95898$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com> <111sbrck2cbim23@corp.supernews.com> <42208dab$0$55656$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com> <vA0Ud.110948$qB6.53237@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <4221cc89$0$19982$ec3e2dad@news.usenetmonster.com> <1124ei2jgfq6sc1@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 27 Feb 2005 16:19:30 -0500, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:
<snip>
> Sometime last year, I read a copy of, I believe, the FDIS for
> the latest Fortran. As I understood it, the Fortran standard
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Nowhere near. Among the several features added in "F2003", which was
actually approved in 2004, is "C interoperabilty" which provides a
series of types (or more precisely what Fortran calls KINDs) and some
declaration attributes which will allow Fortran code to access C
variables and functions (procedures) and/or provide variables and
procedures accessible from C on the same platform. This does involve
some _references_ to the C standard to identify unambiguously the
things with which compatibility is provided.

- David.Thompson1 at worldnet.att.net
```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message 
news:1124ei12km2fhc0@corp.supernews.com...
>
> I'm finding that total words is 7982815 and unique words
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm pretty sure its because of the  '  parsing..

Collier's  C program  waits until word is assembled then deletes  wife's  -> 
wifes
but in case there are more than one quote in word (and there are) in your 
blobb'ed text  they are part of the
accepted word. That is not the case in my parsing of quote within word, I 
discard them all.

scan down your output list looking for words with quotes in your perl vs. 
python list output and I bet you will see
what I am talking about.

we could change the rules and just leave them alone.
```

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> discard them all.

that's indeed really strange parsing ' causes that much headaches !

if you are reading the file character by character, then you can just 
ignore those pesky little quotes:
- if it is surrouded by letters, then you will get the entire word with 
all letters concatenated and without the quote, the word will be counted 
as specified in the problem
- if it is not, that is there are characters other than letters either 
before or after the word, then this other character will be treated as a 
separator, thus ignoring the quote will not influence the results

so why bother doing a special processing on the quotes when you can just 
do nothing ?

> we could change the rules and just leave them alone.

just don't change the rules, they already implicitly specify to leave 
them alone...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T14:58:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d02s3a$1fk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b2efc$421fe664$45491f85$14707@KNOLOGY.NET> <cvvukf$13eu$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <85069$4224e942$45491f85$29765@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
The problem is that on MCP/AS systems, the absence of "Page Mode Editing" in
the Command and Edit product (CANDE) meant that the Margin A/B restrictions
had to be *defeated* in that context by default.  With the appearance of
that feature in CANDE some twenty-and-more years ago (under intense pressure
from B1000 CANDE, which had it!), the justification disappeared but the
unwillingness to change existing practice means that in that environment the
default has remained the same to this day.  If the program is compiled
through WFL, the restrictions apply, but if it's compiled "online" through
CANDE in an editing session, they don't.  The user can manually specify,
either way.

Thus, by default, in that environment paragraphs don't have to be in Margin
A, and that means the *sentence* has to include everything up to and
including the terminating separator period.   Just like COBOL 2002.

My guess is that there isn't a "feature" in the 2200 COBOLs to defeat the
margin restrictions, as our ancient editor required for the Burroughs Large
System decades back when the only way to do page-mode editing was with
visible carriage-return characters and the resultant screwup of
carefully-handled indentation schemes.

    -Chuck Stevens

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:85069$4224e942$45491f85$29765@KNOLOGY.NET...
> Chuck Stevens wrote:
> > "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
> > news:b2efc$421fe664$45491f85$14707@KNOLOGY.NET...
> >
> >>>Thus, my opinion is that the overriding error in the above example
isn't
> >>>that the period is missing after "XYZ", it's that the end of the MOVE
> >>>statement is in Margin A.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > "Agrees with IBM" does not always imply "is more consistent with the
COBOL
> > standard", though I acknowledge that in the context of pre-2002 COBOL
format
> > there is an error here.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

Ok, so the dangers as simple as letting programmers do things that might 
cause problems, but as long as the O/S prevents a user from taking all 
available memory on they system, it's only limited to that particular 
application.  You can get into just as much trouble opening up database 
connections and not releasing them, or updating tables without committing.

But I still think that there is a large distinction from _discouraging_ to 
not _providing_.

JCE

- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <cvvp21$vh2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DATUd.96666$pc5.23981@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
.    On  01.03.05
  wrote  defaultuser@hotmail.com (jce)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  DATUd.96666$pc5.23981@tornado.tampabay.rr.com
  about  Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) e


>>> I'm not trying to be funny, but what are the specific dangers you are
>>> worried about?
>> For example, ALLOCATE in a program declared RECURSIVE with no
>> corresponding FREE.

>> That isn't to say an application programmer can't make reasonable use
>> of such statements, but they'd better know what they're doing, and if
>> they don't do it right, the consequences are theirs to bear ... .

d> Ok, so the dangers as simple as letting programmers do things that
d> might cause problems, but as long as the O/S prevents a user from
d> taking all available memory on they system, it's only limited to that
d> particular application.  You can get into just as much trouble opening
d> up database connections and not releasing them, or updating tables
d> without committing.
d>
d> But I still think that there is a large distinction from
d> _discouraging_ to not _providing_.


  A programming system can hide the allocation of memory behind the  
concept of creating new objects, and freeing the memory when this  
object is discarded (destroyed).

  Pascal introduced something like that with its NEW statement, er,  
procedure, which takes a pointer type bound to a specific type as  
parameter.

  Example (I hope, I got the syntax right):

--------- schnipp -----------------------------------------
  Type date = RECORD
                month : (jan, feb, mar, apr, may, jun,
                         jul, aug, sept, oct, nov, dec);
                day   : 1..31;
                year  : integer
             end;

  Type aDate : ^date;


   myDate := New(aDate)

------------------ schnapp --------------------------------

   This is, in my opinion, a much safer way to allocate memory  
dynamcally than the direct access to memory as such.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Diese ganze Lehre taugt zu nichts als darï¿½ber zu disputieren. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T08:08:28-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0241s$2irn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WbYTd.91104$pc5.41771@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <cvvp21$vh2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DATUd.96666$pc5.23981@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```

"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:DATUd.96666$pc5.23981@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...

> But I still think that there is a large distinction from _discouraging_ to
> not _providing_.

Bill's characterization of the way ALLOCATE and FREE work in 2002 COBOL was
that it was "BAD", and as I read it his reasoning that it was "BAD" was that
it wasn't designed the way the IBM extensions to '85 COBOL that accomplish
similar functionality was.

I responded that it struck me as *highly* unlikely that IBM would fail to
provide a similar extension to '02 COBOL.

I would *encourage* vendors to implement the functionality as specified in
the '02 standard, and I would *encourage* shops that strive to maintain
compatibility with the COBOL standard for portability reasons to use the
*standard* syntax rather than the IBM syntax in furtherance of that goal.
A COBOL that lacks the standard functionality altogether is not conformant
with the standard, and that includes any implementation that offers the IBM
extension functionality unless it also supports the complete *standard*
functionality.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T04:03:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_VaVd.1669$Zm5.125@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<dTwQd.2025677$B07.308176@news.easynews.com> <axeTd.2951174$B07.467247@news.easynews.com> <cvns36$1o0k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <WbYTd.91104$pc5.41771@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <cvvp21$vh2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <DATUd.96666$pc5.23981@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <d0241s$2irn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Wc9Vd.137517$JF2.136391@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
Those items that I indicated were "bad" in my article were intended to be those 
items that IBM customers would not want (IMHO) IBM to spend "resources" on 
implementing (i.e. that they - the customers - would not want to PAY for).

These included items:

A) for which existing features were already available and customers would NOT 
want to convert their code - or find any advantage in using for new code (e.g. 
"BASED"  attribute where current Linkage Section extension "works fine" - for 
them)

B) items that existing IBM mainframe experience have indicated that most shops 
would NOT want application programmers "playing with" (and for which there ARE 
system level features available for "more advanced" programmers) (e.g. ALLOCATE 
and FREE statements and "bit twiddling").

   ***

As far as a "standard defined" (and theoretically portable) solution to problems 
that COBOL programmers have found no existing functionality, I do NOT 
(personally) think that the '02 definitions are "bad".

Does this make my position (in that paper) any clearer?
```

## Thread: Word Processing Challenge Question — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [2005-02](2005-02.md#t-bb687c0437)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Word Processing Challenge Question

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T06:48:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9S16wiOPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6712c$4222574f$45491f85$12551@KNOLOGY.NET> <9RhyLS8eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
.    On  28.02.05
  wrote  L.Willms@JPBERLIN.de (Lueko Willms)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  9RhyLS8eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de
  about  Re: Word Processing Challenge Question


LW>    There are two boolean variables which store what _has_ happened,
LW> "have moved a character" and "have moved a space".

   Well, probably I have only misunderstood, and these are two level- 
88 condition-names for the same variable, as in my version, which  
finally got the parsing right:


Verarbeiten-Eingabesatz SECTION.
 Anfang.
D    DISPLAY laenge-Eingabezeile ">" zeichen-Eingabe(laenge-Eingabezeile) "> " text-line UPON Konsole
     MOVE ZERO TO Laenge OF einzelnes-Wort
     MOVE SPACE TO FIX OF einzelnes-Wort
     SET ist-nicht-im-Wort TO TRUE
     MOVE 1 TO Pointer-Char-in-Line
     PERFORM UNTIL Pointer-Char-in-Line > laenge-Eingabezeile
       EVALUATE zeichen-Eingabe (Pointer-Char-in-Line)
         WHEN "A" THRU "Z"           *>  ALPHABETIC
         WHEN "a" THRU "z"
           ADD 1 TO Laenge OF einzelnes-Wort
           MOVE FUNCTION LOWER-CASE(zeichen-Eingabe(Pointer-Char-in-Line))
                TO FIX OF Einzelnes-Wort(Laenge OF einzelnes-Wort:1)
           IF ist-nicht-im-Wort
           THEN
             SET ist-im-Wort TO TRUE
           END-IF
         WHEN "'"
           CONTINUE
         WHEN OTHER
           IF ist-im-Wort
           THEN
             PERFORM verarbeiten-gefundenes-Wort
             MOVE ZERO TO Laenge OF einzelnes-Wort
             MOVE SPACE TO FIX OF einzelnes-Wort
             SET ist-nicht-im-Wort TO TRUE
           END-IF
       END-EVALUATE
       ADD 1 TO Pointer-Char-in-Line
     END-PERFORM  *> finding individual words in one line
     IF ist-im-Wort THEN
       PERFORM verarbeiten-gefundenes-Wort
     END-IF
     .


   Some translation hints:

   German                      English
   ------------------          ------------------
   Eingabe                     input
   Eingabesatz                 input record
   Eingabezeile                input line
   einzeln                     single
   einzelnes-Wort              single word
   gefunden                    found
   gefundenes-wort             word found
   ist-im-wort                 is in word
   ist-nicht-im-wort           is not in word
   Laenge                      length
   Verarbeiten                 to process
   wort                        word
   zeichen                     character (originally: sign)


  So, I dropped the UNSTRING and went thru the input line character by  
character, but not to reproduce the line again with just spaces as  
delimiters, but to extract single words right away and process them  
word by word during parsing of the input line.

  The VAR and FIX refer to a structure which I use for variable length  
strings, and which is included in the source program by COPY REPLACING  
(in a better compiler, I would use a TYPEDEF for it):


000010 01  NAME.
000020     40  Laenge           PIC 9(4) COMP.
000030     40  FIX              PIC X(512).
000040     40  VAR REDEFINES FIX.
000050         41 FILLER        PIC X
000060            OCCURS 0 TO 512 TIMES
000070            DEPENDING ON Laenge OF NAME
000080     .
000090


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Diese ganze Lehre taugt zu nichts als darï¿½ber zu disputieren. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:ci8Ud.515452$Xk.49623@pd7tw3no...
> jce wrote:
>> "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
>> news:UnPTd.508710$Xk.78771@pd7tw3no...
>>
> Good comments but....

It is going to become clear as you read this, that I've since gone back and 
looked at your original posts...in the Treeview thread.


> Firstly the features that I suggested a Treeview component should have.
> Thinking of Warren's analogy to Lego - you suggest you'll take a couple of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> of a computer component - to date I haven't found one !

Any software subsystem or system that can be factored out and has a 
standardizable or reusable exposed interface.
Unfortunately, a component is different depending on the level of 
abstraction that you want to work at.  A component could be a web service, 
it may be a library module, or it may be as others seem to favour an ActiveX 
object.

Personally, I think of it as any well defined software construct that is 
usable in the environment in which you are working that has a well defined
and well documented interface.  This leaves everything open =)

In a network environment - or a web service environment - the platform is 
not significant, similarly if one is using CORBA.  If you are writing a pure 
100% windows client application, it could be any object / class made
available to you.

This is probably not the standard going definition of "component" software, 
and is not I'm sure shared by the group but....I'm not particularly a 
proponent of components to the same extreme as others and it works for me.

I would even consider something like Log4J a form of component software when 
it is nothing more than a third party open source project that supports a 
standard API.

> Your "fairly simple" then takes your mini Lego units and glues them
> together to form one super Treeview Lego. Seeing that your mini Lego units
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> dotNet - how about 'Musical Chairs' when Uncle Bill introduces Longhorn ?

It depends - I suggest that the KIS principle is often overlooked - if the 
requirements were simplified (or god forbid, someone actually had to write a 
business case) I think the days of Abba would be over (Gimme Gimme Gimme).

There are many faults with the component model - I would never claim them to 
be a solution.  I would firmly believe that a component architecture (using 
my def) would survive a Longhorn better than your approach - primarily 
because the work would be spread across tens/hundreds of people/code 
generators not just you working to earn a buck.

That said, some of the problems you would see are:

Components implement more than is necessary to cover the least common 
denominator view of a thing.  This is my big issue with a language like Java 
where the reserved words are few, and the API's are HUGE.  If anyone care to 
dispute this, I would be interested to know if ANYONE has read the latest 
EJB spec cover to cover.   The advantage of components, is that there will 
more than likely be a lightweight version that supports most of your needs.

There will inevitable come a move to standardize certain standards.  For 
example, using log4j again, someone defines logging standards  _after_ many 
highly successful implementations

Platform dependency.  People can argue all they want, but you will always 
have the "windows" component

> But that aside - if we are talking Treeeviews - isn't it understood that
> there must be a standard set of MINIMUM features for any Windows control -
> which is what a Treeview is ?
Yes, but the understanding would not be consistent.  MFC/.NET/Java, all 
provide base Treeview function.  Java has even gone to the trouble of 
providing a JTree, a DataModel and a number of Views for you.

In my mind you are _using_ N/E components.  You are just not using them as 
true components because you are tying into the N/E way of doing things.  So 
maybe Longhorn comes out...and maybe Micro Focus goes broke..I think all I 
was commenting on was that for some reason you were looking for a component 
that did _everything_ you wanted.   What you have are a set of loosely 
coupled components.  All I think I was suggesting is that you could more 
tightly couple those components - only in terms of the glue - to form the 
whole that you are looking for.  This is, for the most part, what I think 
you are doing.  Perhaps you and I differ on the glue.  Bottom line is that 
if you work hard enough to look for something unique, you'll never find a 
component to do anything :-)
Of course the same is true for any of the component models...the thing that 
sets - in my mind - what you're doing apart is that you are much more tied 
to a specific platform and architecture; however, if you're doing simple 
GUI's then your platform of choice is acceptable.

I don't want to overlook the obvious which is that a good GUI design should 
take precedence over the technology to produce it anyway.  A good design 
provides predictable interaction, should be readily repeatable.


> Then from Feb 25th :-
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> exception of what started the APIs query.
I still don't understand where you need the API for this, but I'm not 
familiar with N/E GUI Classes.  I would hope worst case you could subclass 
and the GUI object to add the features you needed, or even better have the 
data model be updated and just have the view be smart enough to draw it 
correctly.


> Somehow, for the 'real world' application, not my dummy test using a
> Cheese hierarchy, I wanted to indicate which nodes had been updated.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> components ?
Because if you wrote it and packaged it up and needed it again, you'd have 
it next time :-)

I thought (and I don't know because I've never used it) that the whole point 
of Net Express is (I cheated and went to the website)

"provides a complete environment for quickly building and modernizing COBOL 
enterprise components" and "Net Express wizards and tools can also be used
to build COBOL COM objects that can interoperate with Windows applications, 
including Microsoft .NET languages".

> Taking the spec and what I *CAN* already do using methods in Net Express
> GUIs :-
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>to dotNet

> Well the answer to 17/18 is MONEY. Why should I be forced to upgrade to
> use dotNet for Components, which is where many of the component makers
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> compiler.
After reading this reponse, you and others will realize I'm not.  Seems to 
me that you and I are talking the same thing :-)
What happens when NetExpress moves to version NetProfitIncreaseExpress, or 
NowWe'veGotYouBytheShortandCurliesExpress :-)

> First off, from memory because I don't squirrel away previous messages.
> Perhaps some 18 months back I described, versus Pete's String2Num
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> flexible as he thinks he is.
Not familiar with this discussion.

> Now you yourself referred to monolithic code. Perhaps that's another way
> of saying spaghetti code.
No, it's a way of saying one giant piece of code that does everything.  My 
point being that you should avoid producing a single component that would be 
the entire Treeview as you have described it.
At the same time you should avoid placing the code in the wrong places - the 
view and the data should be entirely independent, as should the actual tree.

>Firstly there are a finite set of Windows controls and each with a finite
>set of methods applicable to each. Chances are most of us possibly only use
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the <ENTER> key).
In my mind, using the GUI objects, you are using GUI components to an 
extent.

---Stuff I deleted---
> Jimmy

JCE
```

jce wrote:
> Whilst on religion...I find it interesting how over the course of the last 
> decades that God has become a benevolent God whose believers will criticize 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> was dead.   Maybe He softened up.

Well, there are lots of ways to read the Good Book.  God created life, 
but instituted death as a judgment for sin.  Some might take that to 
mean that He likes life a lot more than death, so we should preserve it 
as much as we can.  Others would say that those who do such things are 
allowing people to get out of their rightful punishment.

> That said, congratulations to you and yours, and hope for a full, complete 
> recovery from all....and many years of joy...

Thanks!  :)

> followed by many years of teenage angst....

I've heard...

> adult money problems...

You can say that in two words - "college" and "girlfriends"  :)

> the sadness of them moving back in.....

Our oldest son will say he wants to be (x) when he grows up - later in 
the day, it'll be (y).  Maybe he has a future on Sesame Street.


> the sadness of them leaving (wait, that's joy right, nope, 
> sadness....joy)...the lack of communication when they meet the disapproved 
> boyfriend....

My wife is already sure that no other woman is going to treat them as 
well as she does - their job is to convince her that she will.  ;)

> and the sharing of holidays....the wedding.....the 
> grandkids...

With 3 boys, we'll just have to wait for the grandkids to have someone 
in the family who needs little pink dresses and such girly stuff.

> I think that sums it up.

Sounds like fun - hope I'm around for it!  :)

```
In article <38kuovF5pkicgU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d02ugi$7ih$1@panix5.panix.com...
>> In article <3e905$4224e7ad$45491f85$29765@KNOLOGY.NET>,
>> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

[snip]

>> >Well, there are lots of ways to read the Good Book.
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>'understanding' of it ...

Wonderful language, this English.  While the original assertion of 'there 
are lots of ways to read' could be interpreted as referring to matters of 
circumstance - 'You can do it on the train, you can do it in a field, you 
can do it in a hotel-room (thanks to the Gideons)... there are lots of 
ways to read the Good Book' - I interpreted 'read' in the sense of 
http://m-w.com/cgi-bin/dictionary?book=Dictionary&va=read&x=15&y=13 , 
definition 4a:

--begin quoted text:

4 a : to attribute a meaning to (as something read) : INTERPRET <how do 
you read this passage>

--end quoted text

[snip]

>On the other hand, there is no such problem when attempting to understand
>Quantum Physics.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Give me Science any day... <g>

Well, leaving aside that the scientific method is concerned with 
reproducibility and verification of results, things that are moderately... 
difficult to apply to religion (how do you know that someone's soul has 
gone, post-mortem, to a particular realm?) I'm reminded of a passage from 
a story by Robert A Heinlein I read, decades on back... 

... one of his Cautionary Tales about the future being run by a 
thought-repressing religious order of some kind.  The protagonist, a lad, 
is sorrowful because he is separated from his sweetie; one of his teachers 
points out how in the Great Learning it is told that 'the attraction 
between two bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance 
between their centers'... a clear metaphor for the Human Condition of Love 
and as time passes his pangs will grow less.

DD
```

> 
Well sport, don't know about you - not too concerned about losing my 
seniority but if I lost my marbles - then's the time to start worrying !
How's it working out living in the new location with beloved daughter ?

We're going East to visit my daughter in about a month. Just for ONE 
week. Four kids from the previous marriage, (they wont be there thank 
God), but her newest - about eighteen months and he's cute - but the 
friggin TV is on 24/7, cartoons/kids' movies of course - I may well have 
lost my marbles before we come back. I'll have to take Dan Brown's 'da 
Vinci Code' for a re-read to distract me from the idiot box !

Kinda reminds me of my daughter's youngest, twin boys.

Us :    "We didn't have TV".

Them :  "You didn't have TV ? What did you eat your popcorn in front of ?".

Us :    "We didn't have popcorn".

Daniel you haven't got a little girl - if only. I think our fondest 
memory of our daughter has to be her at just some 12 months with her 
face lit up with delight at seeing her first Tree on Christmas morning.

Jimmy
```
