```
Jimmy,

I can only say that a couple married over 50 years, and most of it
not being exposed to the daughter and her two boys has been an
adjustment we could not have expected.

Mother Nature does not seem to provide a means to recover all that
separation when the daughter is working most of the time, and the
kids do as they  please.  If you have heard the expression,
"a f*** in Sunday school" you get the idea of how I still feel here.

Warren
```

```
In article <d0af3v$5lo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  4-Mar-2005, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> but that didn't seem to jive. Doc has given the clue.

[snip]

>I finally figured it what the missing word is, not suitable for someone of my
>standing to use, but:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>said: 'Greeting my Lord, we have forgotten the fart."

What I like most about my response is that it can be applied equally to 
breaking wind *or* copulating.  Truth be known, it was inspired by a bit I 
saw on Benny Hill a couple of decades back; the interchange between Mr 
Hill and Bob Todd went something along the lines of:

BH: 'You'll have to put on this dress.'

BT: 'Can't do that.'

BH: 'Why *not*?'

BT: 'Wouldn't be right... I'd feel like a woman, wouldn't I?'

BH: 'Well so do *I*, but wait until after the show!'

... making the shift between 'feel like' used as 'feeling similar to' ('I 
feel like an utter fool') and 'feel like' used as shorthand for 'desire to 
have' ('I feel like an ice-cream cone').

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Religion in CLC posts WAS: Re: MF Collection Class speed

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T12:03:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com> <cvhlg9$62l$1@panix5.panix.com> <llss11dmekhmkg0lr8ne2bdl802f1su53c@4ax.com> <388or3F5n9179U1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
<snip>
> 
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It was also a brand new concept, never before thought of in the entire
known history of the world:  not entirely COBOL, but programming
generally.  Not surprising, then, that there was a small group of
practioners who did by default take on some arcane attributes.  To this
day people ask me "I thought you just had to press a button and the
financial statements (members' list, electron orbit calculations, what
have you) come out.  To this day most people do not understand how
computers actually work.  

COBOL was an attempt to simplify programming to make it more practicable
for more people, and it has certainly succeeded.  IBM attempted to do
the same thing with PL/1, but since it wasn't a non-proprietory,
widely-supported initiative, it failed.

I find the rest of the rant antediluvian, to say the least: or
enormously exaggerating the situation; or, to give Pete the benefit of
the doubt, satirical.

<snip>
> I'm coming to a point now where I really can't see value in the procedural
> approach. Certainly, I'm getting older, and certainly, I'm looking for easy
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Do you seriously contend that the "spreadsheet and database/everyone is
a programmer" types will use THIS sort of thing in their daily work? 
The background knowledge to comprehend the concepts involved in this
code requires extensive education; it might as well be Sanskrit if you
don't understand the language and the concepts.  In what way does this
differ from what you called the High Priests' practice vis-a-vis COBOL?

<snip>
>Almost all of the variables above
> are objects with built in methods and properties that I don't have to even
> think about; I can just use them. 

There are a class of functions built into all languages (either by
intrinsic verbs or by called/invoked subroutines) which you'll never
have to examine in detail (such as raising a number to a non-integer
power).  But there are also great numbers of functions that you will
need to understand in intimate detail - either by examining the source
or by specifications so detailed that they might as well be source. 
Payroll calculations come to mind.  Objects that you can use without
thinking about are the ones that you fully understand.  That doesn't
apply automatically.

> I believe Robert may have a point; perhaps the same factors that contribute
> to religious bigotry, are also found to a large extent in COBOL programmers.
> Or SOME COBOL programmers? Or SOME COBOL Programmers who frequent CLC? (I
> don't exclude myself from this group...)
> 

Certainly some of the posters exhibit symptoms of religiosity.  The most
common examples are those who are sure that they do things the only
possible correct way.



This thread has been going on for a while and it took me some time to
decide to respond, as I was taken aback by the apparently urgent
closed-mindedness in the exposition.  And I haven't read the posts for a
couple of weeks.  So: apologies if I'm raising points that  have been
thrashed to death.

PL
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  3-Mar-2005, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> now.

You maybe Howard but not this rocket scientist operating from home. I 
get my tips from here, clc - not Calgary.

My son buys me a DVD player for Christmas. Think to myself, "That's 
bloody useless - I've only got VCRs". Well he loans me his copy of LOTR 
(Part I), then subsequently "King Arthur" and "Troy" - I preferred the 
latter of the trio.

Ah ha ! Nearly forgot this. Couldn't get my VCR to work as a result of 
attaching the DVD - nor could my son, who isn't too high-tech, but 
sensible. Shopping for my new XP and talking to a salesman, a lady 
overhears my comments, and suggests "Contact Shaw they will do it for 
nothing". Shaw is both my TV cable-provider and ISP. Phone - sure enough 
girl says we will send somebody out and not charge you.

Firstly he adds fresh wiring so that I've got leeway to move the 
equipment around - but then - connects firstly through the DVD player 
and then through the VCR player before making the connection to the TV
set. Now unless you are an electrical/electronic geek you'd never figure 
that one. And as he said, they constantly get calls from people with 
$3,000 to $4,000 setups who haven't a clue how to configure their 
entertainment unit.

Some off the cuff remark I made, and my son said, "Sure gimmie one". So 
I gave him a Vivaldi CD - magic - I've got music coming out of my TV. 
Yes I know you can do things with media players on computers - but had 
never considered the possibility of a similar approach with a TV 
configuration.

Not being tech interested - I'm not into cameras. Completely turned off 
by the jargon - apertures, sunlight, whatever. Real simple when I was a 
kid. A box Brownie - point the bloody thing and hope you got a decent 
black and white. Digital cameras ? Roughly four weeks ago, my wife goes 
in for some re-prints. Young girl swings into action, thinking I'm 
another customer. "Nope. I'm with that lady at the counter". Anyway, 
nice girl. She and I chat and she moves me over to the digital display.
I had absolutely no concept of digital = a CD containing your 
photographic images, and more importantly the CD can be re-used. Then 
two weeks later, quite by accident, I read digitals are currently some 
80% plus of camera sales.

Familiarity breeds contempt. Well not really 'contempt', but rather 
'knowledge'.

Jimmy
```

```
I am a keen photographer and I like to use transparency (slide) film. It is
getting much harder to obtain here because everyone has moved to digital.

can take my existing lenses, cannot be justified at the moment so I am still
shooting with my 30 year old Nikon FE...

ago and he is just being proved more and more right about it...

Pete.
```

- **Date:** 2005-03-03T10:24:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109874256.708283.236000@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com> <cvhlg9$62l$1@panix5.panix.com> <llss11dmekhmkg0lr8ne2bdl802f1su53c@4ax.com> <388or3F5n9179U1@individual.net> <42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
> It was also a brand new concept, never before thought of in the
entire
> known history of the world:  not entirely COBOL, but programming
> generally.

Well, no, actually.  Programming was just a progression.  Prior to
electronic stored program computers, business systems were programmed
less formally but just as precisely using unit record equipment, or
human computers, or as procedure manuals for accountants.

Technical computing had their analog computers which were programmed by
changing cicuits or mechanical computers programmed with gears and
belts. Or they just used rows of computers following page after page of
calculations to perform and which neibour to pass the result to - a
network indeed, all carefully planned and written out, tested, and then
run live.
```

```
>It was also a brand new concept, never before thought of in the entire
>known history of the world:  not entirely COBOL, but programming

Consider altering the above, e'er-so-slightly, to read:

The ability to transmit audio signals by wire antedates COBOL by close to

up the handset, dial and talk.'  To this day most people do not understand 
how telephones work.

... or... the ability to transmit video images by electromagnetic waves 
antedates COBOL by close to a half-century... most people do not 
understand how television works.

DD
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com> <cvhlg9$62l$1@panix5.panix.com> <llss11dmekhmkg0lr8ne2bdl802f1su53c@4ax.com> <388or3F5n9179U1@individual.net> <42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> <snip>
> >
> >
> > COBOL was a powerful cult that ruled the world of EDP for at least 25
years
> > before it  (COBOL) went into decline.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > secret in the interests of job security, and held thinly veiled disdain
for
> > people who needed their services.(Did you ever hear a COBOL programmer
> > saying good things about  end users...? And yet these end users were the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Tell that to someone under 25.

> COBOL was an attempt to simplify programming to make it more practicable
> for more people, and it has certainly succeeded.  IBM attempted to do
> the same thing with PL/1, but since it wasn't a non-proprietory,
> widely-supported initiative, it failed.
>

PL/1 did not fail; it simply didn't gain the acceptance COBOL did. As
recently as 6 years ago I  was involved with a major UK company where the
core business was written in PL/1 on a mainframe.

> I find the rest of the rant antediluvian, to say the least: or
> enormously exaggerating the situation; or, to give Pete the benefit of
> the doubt, satirical.

Well, thank you for that. It would be a terrible thing if we actually took
ourselves too seriously... My post was tongue-in-cheek to the extent that it
was wrapped in a religious analog, but the underlying ideas were presented
as serious arguments. As for it being a rant, I can't get worked up enough
about COBOL to rant about it :-)

Antediluvian? My point was that the attitudes of many COBOL programmers is
exactly that; resistance to change, refusal to look at anything that doesn't
fit the preconceived idea of procedural programming, adapt to a changing
environment, or leave their comfort zone in any way. THAT is
antediluvian...as the fossil record bears witness.

I confess to some exaggeration (it wasn't 'enormous') and sweeping
statements, but it was not entirely serious...

There were still some pertinent and valid points about why COBOL is in
decline and the young (apart from Kellie... :-)) generally are not rushing
to embrace it.

>
> <snip>
> > I'm coming to a point now where I really can't see value in the
procedural
> > approach. Certainly, I'm getting older, and certainly, I'm looking for
easy
> > options, but when I can code:
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
Driver};Server=taurangawriters.org.nz;Database=twdbremote;uid=xxxxxxxx;pwd=x
> > xxxxxxx;option=3";
> > //Open the connection...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Request.QueryString("f1newPassword") + "'" + " WHERE ClientID = " + "'"
+
> > currentMember + "'" ;
> >     try {
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> a programmer" types will use THIS sort of thing in their daily work?

I seriously contend that they ARE using it. Not only that, kids still in
school are using it. I had a 14 year old asking me for help on JavaScript
the other day, and I get about 3 or 4 emails a week on the subject. Given
that I'm not participating in any Java or JavaScript forums, this is quite
significant. There is a new generation of people coming on who DO understand
OO concepts (even if they haven't been formally trained in them and cannot
"talk the talk" , many of them are simply "walking the walk"...besides, it
isn't difficult, once you clear your mind of all the procedural approaches
that have been drummed into it over years). Kids have the advantage of never
having learned COBOL, so there is no preconception to discard.

See, Peter, one reason I posted the code is because I knew a number of
people here would react as you have and say it looks like Sanskrit or
Swahili. It does to the uninitiated eye and I totally understand the
reaction. BUT, the difference here is that today, EVERYBODY has access to
the keys that render it comprehensible. You DON'T need a University degree
or expensive training or to be part of a programming elite. It is driven by
need. People want dynamic web sites (especially young people). They start by
grabbing a small piece of code and simply playing with it until they get the
desired effect. It snowballs on from there.

Richard's point about what is familiar has never been so well demonstrated
as in this.

Industry is realising more and more that the future is on the web. The
Intranet and Internet together ensure the smooth flow of information
throughout large and small organisations. (These solutions are scalable in a
way that procedural ones never could be.) What used to be IT departments
with development and maintenance are becoming simply 'Network Support' and
Helpdesk functions. The user base are doing the 'development' with
spreadsheets, databases, and connectivity. The 'IT solution' is becoming
more business oriented becaus it is being done by people in the business.



> The background knowledge to comprehend the concepts involved in this
> code requires extensive education; it might as well be Sanskrit if you
> don't understand the language and the concepts.  In what way does this
> differ from what you called the High Priests' practice vis-a-vis COBOL?
>

You state something as if it were true. It is at best, arguable. "The
background knowledge to comprehend the concepts involved in this code
requires extensive education. "  No, I don't think so. It is based on a
handful of simple concepts. Even if 'education' were required (and a small
amount has to be, to acquire the fundamental concepts I mentioned) that
education is freely available to anyone who wants to avail themselves of it,
from the comfort of their living room.Some will simply 'do it' and maybe
never formally understand what they are doing. It doesn't matter.

> <snip>
> >Almost all of the variables above
> > are objects with built in methods and properties that I don't have to
even
> > think about; I can just use them.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or by specifications so detailed that they might as well be source.

Only in procedural languages. When it comes to objects, all that matters are
properties, methods, and events. Can this object do what I need? (NOT: How
will I make this object do what I want?)

> Payroll calculations come to mind.  Objects that you can use without
> thinking about are the ones that you fully understand.  That doesn't
> apply automatically.
>

Payrolls are notoriously complex for what, on the surface, appears to be a
straightforward application. I remember one many yeas ago that involved
paying people at a hospital and it meant everyone from cleaners to
consulting surgeons. (Around 4000 different classifications and awards).
There were allowances and bonuses of every conceivable description, not to
mention union requirements regarding shift length and so on. Yes, it was
diverse, yes, it was complex, and it had to run correctly every day. (We
wrote it in COBOL, and it did...) I agree that for this type of what is
essentially batch processing, COBOL is a good option. (I never debated that,
by the way...)
> > I believe Robert may have a point; perhaps the same factors that
contribute
> > to religious bigotry, are also found to a large extent in COBOL
programmers.
> > Or SOME COBOL programmers? Or SOME COBOL Programmers who frequent CLC?
(I
> > don't exclude myself from this group...)

I believe in the right tools for the job. COBOL is becoming less of the
right tool when it comes to web applications, and for me at least, that is
where I want to be. I don't want to be dragged kicking and screaming into
web programming, I want to be there and be comfortable with it by the time
the world wakes up and realises that it is fundamental to doing business.
(This process is happening as we speak...)
> >
>
> Certainly some of the posters exhibit symptoms of religiosity.  The most
> common examples are those who are sure that they do things the only
> possible correct way.

That would be everybody... :-) I don't think there is a single poster (or
reader) here who believes they do things the WRONG way or sets out to screw
it up... As for the ONLY way, well, the way we feel most comfortable with
tends to become the ONLY way...certainly for each of us.
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> closed-mindedness in the exposition.

Yes, I have been accused of closed-mindedness by some remarkably closed
minds... It is because my mind is open that I am prepared to examine all
solutions without deciding it HAS to be COBOL.

And I haven't read the posts for a
> couple of weeks.  So: apologies if I'm raising points that  have been
> thrashed to death.
>

It is a discussion; no apology is required.

Thanks for your post; I have tried to address your points.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Religion in CLC posts WAS: Re: MF Collection Class speed

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T14:03:55+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38pqfvF5r4cfjU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com> <cvhlg9$62l$1@panix5.panix.com> <llss11dmekhmkg0lr8ne2bdl802f1su53c@4ax.com> <388or3F5n9179U1@individual.net> <42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca> <38pjsmF5po42hU1@individual.net> <mu9f21pbbgc3oij08qissd0ar0v344usi8@4ax.com>`

```

"Jeff York" <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote in message
news:mu9f21pbbgc3oij08qissd0ar0v344usi8@4ax.com...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ouch! While it is certainly true that kids love games, they also love
technology in general.  The point I am trying (struggling?) to make here is
that they just use stuff and don't really care how it works. However, to
most of them it is familiar, so they have no problem when the time comes to
do more... Add to that the fact that OO has some very simple basic concepts
and these concepts are readily obtainable from the web, along with code
examples, and it is really not surprising that most teenagers can build web
sites, while their parents watch in bemused awe.. I would agree that not all
teenagers have affinity with or desire to use, computers at a more than
superficial level, but I claim poetic licence with that particular
generalization... :-).  The important thing here was my attempt to contrast
the openness and availability of information today, with the COBOL era where
the 'knowledge' was NOT freely available to all and sundry (hence my use of
'arcane'), and some people certainly decided it was in the interests of job
security to keep it as mystical as possible... Thus the practitioners of it
were more 'priest-like'.

Hey, I'm trying to entertain here as well as discuss... :-) It really isn't
that important...

> >> COBOL was an attempt to simplify programming to make it more
practicable
> >> for more people, and it has certainly succeeded.  IBM attempted to do
> >> the same thing with PL/1, but since it wasn't a non-proprietory,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Lol! The practioners of it would have you believe it embodied the BEST of
both... I only ever wrote one routine in it because somebody said it
couldn't be done (it involved AFP on mainframes and I wanted signatures on
letters; the encamped High Priest wasn't going to have  a manager demand
things from him that might involve effort or research, or challenge his
authority in any way. He had never used or heard of AFP and when I gave him
a manual open at the page he wasn't going to read it either... so he chose
the old: "Managers are idiots; tell him it can't be done" routine. Wrong
manager. I wrote it myself, in PL/1 and gave it to him to test the next day.
It worked. I then took him to lunch and told him of he ever pulled a stunt
like that again, he would re-employed pushing chad back into punch cards for
re-use... After that we got on famously, and he did some really good work.
PL/1 is certainly a programmer's language and it had facilities (like
dynamic allocation of memory and  dynamically sizing arrays) that COBOL
didn't have.

I don't think it was a serious contender for the COBOL crown, certainly not
in the commercial arena, but then, I'm biased... :-)

Pete.

> -- 
> Jeff.         Ironbridge,  Shrops,  U.K.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Religion in CLC posts WAS: Re: MF Collection Class speed

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T03:31:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8EQVd.569051$8l.223633@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<38pqfvF5r4cfjU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com> <cvhlg9$62l$1@panix5.panix.com> <llss11dmekhmkg0lr8ne2bdl802f1su53c@4ax.com> <388or3F5n9179U1@individual.net> <42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca> <38pjsmF5po42hU1@individual.net> <mu9f21pbbgc3oij08qissd0ar0v344usi8@4ax.com> <38pqfvF5r4cfjU1@individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "Jeff York" <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote in message
> news:mu9f21pbbgc3oij08qissd0ar0v344usi8@4ax.com...
> 
If bits of what you write depress me, it doesn't mean the "whole" 
depresses me :-)

>>>Tell that to someone under 25.
>>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> that important...

Based on Jeff's observation, I think you've modified the above slightly. 
  Previous messages - kids pick it up, use it, don't even want or need 
to know how it was put together.

So now we've got umpteen kids using zillions of components - based on 
what I've just defined. So in which corner does the decrepit old man 
exist who produced these components - and to whom does he pass his 
wisdom on to, before he croaks ? Just who do these kids ask for 
components embracing new computing or business concepts.

I'll throw in another one - partially they don't care because of a 
demise in education standards. Just a N. American observation, so don't 
know how it plays in Blighty, on the Continent or down in the Antipodes.
My observations tell me that education is not the same 'animal' that I 
had in my generation. Same goes for Chuck, Bill, PECD, Donald, Howard, 
Jeff, Doc, Richard and many more - you all are of roughly a decade and a 
bit, following me. I'll mention something like 'Charge of the Light 
Brigade" which you picked up on. Then Doc and Howard pick up on "The 
Highwayman". I had no idea that poem had significance in N. America.

I'm not plugging poetry here, but education-wise whatever the formula 
was, we were taught to memorize, think and sift. The 'in vogue' 
educators have rolled together some topics like 'social studies' now 
embraces perhaps history, geography and possibly a smidgen of political 
science - means none get covered in depth.

So our new generations, partially ill-equipped, wont be challenged to 
think up new components. That is a very large overstatement. Technology 
will certainly continue in leaps and bounds - but until the education 
system catches up, it will have a temporary crippling effect.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Religion in CLC posts WAS: Re: MF Collection Class speed

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T08:03:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d09mam$ook$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <mu9f21pbbgc3oij08qissd0ar0v344usi8@4ax.com> <38pqfvF5r4cfjU1@individual.net> <8EQVd.569051$8l.223633@pd7tw1no>`

```
In article <8EQVd.569051$8l.223633@pd7tw1no>,
James J. Gavan <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>I'll throw in another one - partially they don't care because of a 
>demise in education standards.

Ahhhh, for the Oldene Dayse... when an education standard could be 
adhered-to such as *ten* standards cannot, today!

>Just a N. American observation, so don't 
>know how it plays in Blighty, on the Continent or down in the Antipodes.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Highwayman". I had no idea that poem had significance in N. America.

Gah... N. America is a matter of geography, 'significance' is a matter of 
human beings, last I looked.  I will admit, though, when my jaw gets to 
wagging about some stuff... errrrr, most stuff... I've noticed, for the 
most part, reactions ranging from simple eye-glazing to something one step 
more courteous than 'Hold off! unhand me, grey-beard loon!'.

>
>I'm not plugging poetry here, but education-wise whatever the formula 
>was, we were taught to memorize, think and sift.

Plural majestatus est, Mr Gavan... 'we' might have been taught such things 
but it is one thing to have been taught, another to have learned... and 
still another to take love in that learning, take it to heart and make it 
part of one's life.  There were, in the Oldene Dayse, folk who went 
through the same schooling as you did and found themselves working as 
locksmiths, plumbers, waiters and the like... it might be interesting to 
learn how much such folks retain of the 'fine arts' training they had.

>The 'in vogue' 
>educators have rolled together some topics like 'social studies' now 
>embraces perhaps history, geography and possibly a smidgen of political 
>science - means none get covered in depth.

Try to remember that common curricula are often set by ''in vogue' 
educators'... but vogues change, I guess.  General education is supposed 
to be general... on the other hand, I think it was Aristotle who said 
something along the lines of 'an educated man should be able to hear a 
discourse on any subject and make a critical evaluation thereof'... but 
there weren't as many subjects about which to discourse, back then.

DD
```

```
>.. I would agree that not all
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Religion in CLC posts WAS: Re: MF Collection Class speed

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3932coF5r2t8qU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <381ustF5j6g87U1@individual.net> <sern11d72igqfdnm3lth34sjgsgqrv4dm9@4ax.com> <cvhlg9$62l$1@panix5.panix.com> <llss11dmekhmkg0lr8ne2bdl802f1su53c@4ax.com> <388or3F5n9179U1@individual.net> <42275161.10636831@mb.sympatico.ca> <38pjsmF5po42hU1@individual.net> <mu9f21pbbgc3oij08qissd0ar0v344usi8@4ax.com> <38pqfvF5r4cfjU1@individual.net> <tmsg21t7oo9ulaeq2f4dcq47be12q5oq6u@4ax.com>`

```
Hi Jeff,

this was such a good response I had to comment...

see below.

"Jeff York" <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote in message
news:tmsg21t7oo9ulaeq2f4dcq47be12q5oq6u@4ax.com...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> It was also a brand new concept, never before thought of in the
entire
> >> >> known history of the world:  not entirely COBOL, but programming
> >> >> generally.  Not surprising, then, that there was a small group of
> >> >> practioners who did by default take on some arcane attributes.  To
this
> >> >> day people ask me "I thought you just had to press a button and the
> >> >> financial statements (members' list, electron orbit calculations,
what
> >> >> have you) come out.  To this day most people do not understand how
> >> >> computers actually work.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >technology in general.  The point I am trying (struggling?) to make here
is
> >that they just use stuff and don't really care how it works.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, none of us really understand that. I was discussing same with a friend
who is a Lecturer in Maths, just this very evening. He summed it up by
saying: "When we observe these fundamental particles, it changes the way
they behave. The truth is that we really have no idea what matter and energy
packets are doing when we are not looking at them." I had come across this
idea before, but this is the first time I met someone who actually believes
it and cares about it. I had this vision of a Photon Club where photons go
when no one's looking at them... Seeing time is infinite for them and they
have no mass, I guess the floor show would go on for a very long time...
Talk about stretching a drink... :-)

> >.. However, to
> >most of them it is familiar, so they have no problem when the time comes
to
> >do more... Add to that the fact that OO has some very simple basic
concepts
> >and these concepts are readily obtainable from the web, along with code
> >examples, and it is really not surprising that most teenagers can build
web
> >sites, while their parents watch in bemused awe..
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, supposing you were right? Does it really matter? I can immediately
think of several people I know who are excellent and skillful drivers, yet
have no idea about the workings of a car...

> >.. I would agree that not all
> >teenagers have affinity with or desire to use, computers at a more than
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
ROFL! That is simply brilliant :-) I was chuckling for twenty minutes and
you made my day. Thanks.

> >..  The important thing here was my attempt to contrast
> >the openness and availability of information today, with the COBOL era
where
> >the 'knowledge' was NOT freely available to all and sundry (hence my use
of
> >'arcane'), and some people certainly decided it was in the interests of
job
> >security to keep it as mystical as possible... Thus the practitioners of
it
> >were more 'priest-like'.
>
> They probably still are, just at a slightly greater "distance" if you
> see what I'm getting at.

No Jeff, the whole nub of my argument is that the Internet has empowered
dissemination of knowledge. You CAN'T keep stuff secret anymore. Wanna learn
computer programming? Fine, get on the Net... That was never possible in my
day. I have learned ASP and JavaScript totally from the Net; have no books
on them in the house; all my references are online and bookmarked in my
Browser. Java I learned from (several) books, but, on reflection, I probably
could have got that from the Web as well. The open nature of the knowledge
availability places it in the hands of anyone who wants it. And lots of kids
do. (I agree, not all...). I think it is a fantastic thing. It is well time
that the caste of technocracy was abolished; let those who want to know,
know...

> OO gives you the ability to take a Lego
> approach to system building..  But somebody still has to make the
> bricks.
>

Yes, I think that was part of Jimmy's point too.

It isn't so. Somebody has to make the kernel of your Operating System too.
How long is it since you did that? Oh, never...? Just use it every day and
expect it to work, huh?

Just as there is not enough Operating System to fully employ all the
programmers in the world, so there won't be enough business components to do
so either. The number of people employed as full time computer programmers
will fall in the next ten years.  The world wants computer technology but it
wants it to be easy and not complicated. History has shown that what the
Market wants, the Market invariably gets. (One of the reasons that led to
the decline of COBOL is that 400,000,000 daily computer users are not about
to become programmers.. Yet they want their computers to do what they
expect. Packages and component based software, spreadsheets, word
processors, email, internet access, and databases, let them achieve that.
The free dissemination of knowledge supports it.)

Said it before, I say it again: "Computer programming was a phenomenon of
the latter half of the 20th century."   Just like cottage industry making
lace at the onset of the industrial revolution. (Ironically, the lace makers
believed their jobs were secure because the art of making it was so
intricate no machine could ever do it.) It took 30 years, but it was
achieved. Why? Because lace curtains were a status symbol and everybody
wanted them. The demand was such that a cottage industry could not keep up
with it. Next time you're in Nottingham, have a look at the lace museum;
they still have some of the original machines that men spent their working
lives devising...well worth a visit.

The component 'bricks' will be churned out by smart systems, not by people.
There are several ways this can be achieved (the best one is by heuristic
interaction, but there are many options...). Within 15 years, pending
breakthroughs in technology will provide computer power that can build and
discard a trillion components in a couple of minutes, until the required one
is found. By interacting with an end user, systems can be generated that
will do what people want. And if they change their mind about what they
want, (and we all know they do...)  the system can be regenerated instantly,
to the new requirement.

We have always built machines to do tedious (or profitable) work for us; why
should programming be any exception?

Or then again, maybe they'll just do it in COBOL... :-)


> >Hey, I'm trying to entertain here as well as discuss... :-) It really
isn't
> >that important...
>
> Fun though..  :-)
>

Yes, certainly fun. That's a good enough reason for being here... :-)

Pete.

>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Religion in CLC posts WAS: Re: MF Collection Class speed

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-07T08:47:43-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<d0hm1v$6iq$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<sc2l115bdtbh5tek9q8bjlkim6mi05pgnl@4ax.com> <38pqfvF5r4cfjU1@individual.net> <tmsg21t7oo9ulaeq2f4dcq47be12q5oq6u@4ax.com> <3932coF5r2t8qU1@individual.net>`

```
In article <3932coF5r2t8qU1@individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>I was discussing same with a friend
>who is a Lecturer in Maths, just this very evening. He summed it up by
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>it and cares about it.

Hmmmmm... reminds me of an incident that happened to me a few decades 
back; I saw a traffic-accident and I left my name and number with the 
injuted party as a witness.  Came the court-date and I had the following 
interchange with an attorney:

'What color was the traffic-light at the time of the accident?'

'I was first in my lane at the light, arriving at the intersection in time 
to see it turn red.  A matter of seconds later the truck pulled up next to 
me on my left; it, also, was first in its lane.  I was taking a flight out 
of Newark that afternoon and I glanced down at my paperwork on the 
passenger's seat for what I would say was no more than ten seconds; while 
doing that I heard the crash.  I looked up and saw that the truck which 
was next to me had pulled out into the intersection and that the 
traffic-light was red.  The light was red when I arrived, red when the 
truck pulled up and red within ten seconds of my looking away and looking 
back... but I *cannot* tell you what color the light was when I wasn't 
looking at it.'

DD
```

```
Jeff York wrote:
```

```
ROUTINE USES:  Tests are scored and files by SSN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T00:13:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ry7Vd.543502$8l.466823@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ef4c3$4224e5b5$45491f85$29286@KNOLOGY.NET>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <lvGUd.37193$by5.11131@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <ef4c3$4224e5b5$45491f85$29286@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
LX-i wrote:
> Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Catch-22 situation, Daniel.

The RAF is controlled by QR's and ACIs (Queen's Regulations and Air 
Council Instructions) - or at least that's what they were called some 
forty years ago. (R.N. and Army have something parallel). Those 
regulations are very specific, no ambiguity, about how 'Officers and 
Gentlemen' will conduct themselves and you may NOT <REPEAT> NOT - 
discuss women/sex, religion or politics in an officers' mess, or 
elsewhere in a military environment, for that matter.

Scenario # 1 - Young Pilot Officer Jones having finished his flying 
training, joins his first squadron. Within very short order the station 
commander says they will have an officers' dining-in-night. Young 
Jonesey is delighted to attend. Has a few more whiskies than he should 
and blurts out, "Oh. I voted for Harold Wilson's Labour Government". 
Deathly hush. Jonesey - his career rapidly disappears down between his 
legs and finishes up in his rectum.

Scenario # 2 - using same QRs and ACIs. While in Germany at a NATO unit 
with R.Neth.A.F and Luftwaffe, my immediate boss was a squadron leader 
(major). A miner's son with no pretensions of 'gentility, and like me 
was an ex-apprentice. (He was an airframe mechanic, I think). Along 
comes WW2 and he becomes a bomber pilot, got the DFC and lost hearing in 
one ear from shell explosion.

There was a General Election in UK in '60 or '61. Don't even recall who 
won. The next morning following the election he comes in steamin', just 
steamin'. The Hossifers and Gentlefolk had a tote board up in the 
Officers' Mess to list the results. (Absolutely VERBOTEN I would have 
thought). There were a group of civilian teachers living in the 
Officers' Mess - presumed logic - they had 'degrees', couldn't possibly 
house them with the serfs in the Sergeants' Mess. Trouble was this 
bunch, like a lot of teachers, had concluded there was more to socialism 
than conservatism. So each time a Labourite won - big CHEERS from this 
group.

My squadron leader - absolutely hopping mad, "They SHOULDN'T be in the 
Officers' Mess". Never occurred to him that the sergeant he was talking 
to, ONLY EVER ONCE voted for the Conservatives - more as hero worship, 
but it was Winnie Churchill.

Since that first vote I've always voted Liberal, regardless - and yeah I 
know Liberal is a sneaky way of saying 'Democrat' :-)

I assume Michael's reference above technically prohibits the military 
from storing your SSN in your computerized personnel record ? But the 
buggers still want it, regardless of federal legislation. Our Charter of 
Rights (replacing the BNA and English Common Law), prohibits what 
employers may ask. It's only a week ago on TV I saw somebody state  our 
Charter of Rights was based on yours - no bloody wonder we have
problems !

PS: How churlish of me before, I should have written "Congrats Mommy and 
Daddy".

Jimmy
```

```
From 
<http://groups.google.co.uk/groups?selm=acvl2d%24r0s%241%40panix1.panix.com&output=gplain> :

--begin quoted text:

... the words of Thomas Jefferson:

http://odur.let.rug.nl/~usa/P/tj3/writings/brf/jefl246.htm

--begin quoted text:

But I know also, that laws and institutions must go hand in hand with the 
progress of the human mind.  As that becomes more developed, more 
enlightened, as new discoveries are made, new truths disclosed, and 
manners and opinions changewith the change of circumstances, institutions 
must advance also, and keep pace with the times.  We might as require a 
man to wear still the coat which fitted him as a boy, as civilized society 
to remain ever under the regimen of their barbarous ancestors.

--end quoted text

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T16:43:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d07evm$7k4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <Ry7Vd.543502$8l.466823@pd7tw1no> <70e35$42252cac$45491f85$21395@KNOLOGY.NET> <d07bdv$5r4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d07d2n$8sl$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On  3-Mar-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >> Our Bill of Rights is fine - just when people start trying to say that
> >> the meaning of words "morphs" over time, and what the people wrote and
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> to remain ever under the regimen of their barbarous ancestors.

The trick here is to not pretend that the boy's coat is doing the job, it is to
replace that boy's coat with a man's coat.    If the Constitution needs
changing, change it.   But if you pretend it says something different from what
it really says - don't be surprised if others do the same thing.

I'm someone who doesn't trust the powerful to be uncorrupted by the power.  
They believe that whatever they do is the best, and abuse of power for the
common good as they see it is worth while.   In fact, their definition of the
common good is based upon how good it feels to have power to define it.    
Therefore, I like strict interpretations of the limits they have.

Thomas Jefferson foresaw the need to change those limits, but didn't make the
process too easy.   I commend his foresight.


I have to admit that I often wish it was easier to change programming shop
standards, so I have more power to do things the way I think they should be
done!!!     But I'm glad they aren't so easy to change so that if my shops hired
Mr. Wagner, he couldn't easily change them to the way he thinks they should be
done.
```

```
Best one of all, is that now 8 (I think) businesses in Miami Dade have

Leave people wearing a rather ill fitting boys coat, or have common people 
decide that it would be nice if we gave everyone the mandate to wear the 
latest mink fur boa with it.

have nor want so you ignore it.

Where'd all the sensible people go if they ain't in Florida?
```

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET...

> It doesn't work for *any* parts.  You may disagree with what the words
> say, but they say what they say.  :)  I don't mind folks wanting to
> change the substance of what it says (though I've seen few worthy of a
> change at that level), but changing the substance by redefining the
> original words is, IMO, wrong.

I don't think it's worthwhile to disagree with what the words are, but the
controversy is over what the words, in controversy, meant when they were
written, and whether what they meant then is meaningful or appropriate
*now*.   I've written at length about my *constitutional* right to own a
fully-armed nuclear attack submarine and a stealth bomber (regardless of
whether I actually want to do so or not)!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
In article <p6qdnUziCOilxLXfRVn-uw@rogers.com>,
rpl  <plinnane3REMOVE@NOSPAMyahoo.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>prior to the introduction of cast iron frames.

Hey, if it were easy then *everyone* would have been doing it!

>
>The further away you get from the original tempering sweet spot the more 
>discordant a piece sounds.

Ow... perhaps it is time to draw a line and add the label 'Here Be 
Dragons'... and refrain from crossing it.  How something 'sounds', as how 
something 'looks', might best be left to Trained And Certified 
Aestheticians; it might be readily argued that no matter how close to the 
original tempering sweet spot an instrument was it might have sounded 
discordant to some who were trained/accustomed/prone by... temperment to 
other systems of tuning.

DD
```

- **Message-ID:** `<d09quc$3f4$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
>meaning of the term. Try google, under "tempered scale".

Mr Tees, I was using the term 'well-tempered' to convey the sense of 'a 
system of tuning in which the distance between notes are equal in terms of 
frequency ratios' (as opposed, say, to Pythagorean tuning-by-fifths)... 
but I was sloppy in not quoting the phrase 'original tempering sweet 
spot', a use I have seen only in the posting to which I was responding.  
My error and apologies.

DD
```

```
>
>I live with a string player of world class, and got a two hour 
>conversation on the topic last night.  Prior to that my only explanation 
>was from a friendly piano tuner a few years back, so the time was not 
>wasted.

A... few decades back I made a presentation to my college music-class 
about the shift to equal temperment; I used the example of Pythagorean 
tuning to show that while it was perfect in terms of geometry and ratios 
the octave that resulted from it was (by modern standards) a about a 
half-tone wrong... that's why I remembered it.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T08:45:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uEZVd.53498$Vf6.1453282@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<p6qdnUziCOilxLXfRVn-uw@rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET> <d08f40$jh6$1@panix5.panix.com> <IWPVd.41174$kz6.775547@news20.bellglobal.com> <d09dc3$evh$1@panix5.panix.com> <p6qdnUziCOilxLXfRVn-uw@rogers.com>`

```
rpl wrote:
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> discordant a piece sounds.

"Well tempered" refers to adjusting the scale in a very specific way. 
The harps are not well tempered as is the piano, they are mainline 
tempered, while fret instruments are not tempered at all.

The term has nothing to do with iron frames. It is a mathmatically 
precise term refering to a specific adjustment of specific frequencies 
within the 88 notes of a piano.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T10:23:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w3%Vd.53793$Vf6.1478918@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d09sda$msm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <70e35$42252cac$45491f85$21395@KNOLOGY.NET> <d07bdv$5r4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET> <d08f40$jh6$1@panix5.panix.com> <IWPVd.41174$kz6.775547@news20.bellglobal.com> <d09sda$msm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  3-Mar-2005, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sure they do.

Not to technicians ... though you may be right when the ad types get 
into the act.

Regardless ... "well-tempered" refers to a specific method of tuning, 
always has, and still does. If used in the sense of "a good sound, or 
'nicely adjusted'" then it is not being used correctly.  It is not a 
matter of style, it is a matter of using a technical term incorrectly. 
Such things change much more slowly than simple phraseology.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T16:11:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0a1fc$qf1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <70e35$42252cac$45491f85$21395@KNOLOGY.NET> <d07bdv$5r4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET> <d08f40$jh6$1@panix5.panix.com> <IWPVd.41174$kz6.775547@news20.bellglobal.com> <d09sda$msm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <w3%Vd.53793$Vf6.1478918@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On  4-Mar-2005, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> >>Technical terms do not change.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not to technicians

Technology changes.   Not only in time, but in location.    Technical jargon
changes for useful and political reasons.   Terms such as "database", and
"network database" and "atomic power", and "font", and "destroyer", and
"astrology" and "law" (as in law of gravity) change.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T13:31:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JP1Wd.47605$kz6.859069@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d0a1fc$qf1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <70e35$42252cac$45491f85$21395@KNOLOGY.NET> <d07bdv$5r4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET> <d08f40$jh6$1@panix5.panix.com> <IWPVd.41174$kz6.775547@news20.bellglobal.com> <d09sda$msm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <w3%Vd.53793$Vf6.1478918@news20.bellglobal.com> <d0a1fc$qf1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  4-Mar-2005, Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> "astrology" and "law" (as in law of gravity) change.

Yes, but the names of specific theories tend not to.  The Pythagorian 
theorm, for example, has not changed an iota in a few thousand years. 
You can name it something else, but what it refers to stays the same. A 
well-tempered scaled fits into that class perfectly ... in fact there is 
a pythagorean tempered scale that pre-dates the well tempered scale, and 
it has not changed either.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <70e35$42252cac$45491f85$21395@KNOLOGY.NET> <d07bdv$5r4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET> <d08f40$jh6$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET>,
> LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> still refer to a piano's tuning as 'well-tempered'?

I might...  It still fits, IMO.

This is going to seem like it comes from nowhere, but it does have a 
point.  Growing up, we attended churches where the King James Version of 
the Bible was THE translation, and all others were a violation of 
Revelation 22:18-19 (adding to or taking away from the words of this Book).

I'm not sure I agree with all of their reasoning, but one of the ones 
they used was that this translation was done when English was at the 
height of its usage, and the average number of words in an 
English-speaker's vocabulary was quite high.  Now, a scant 100-150 years 
later, we have these founding documents.  Lots of words, lots of words 
that have meaning, and also lots of principles contained in those words. 
  There were very learned men who wrote these documents - doctors, 
scientists, etc.

For someone to take a new definition of their words and say "well, I 
know that's what they wrote, but what they *meant* was..." is dishonest. 
  They meant what they *wrote*, no more, no less.  If we as a nation are 
not happy with that, there is a process to clarify and/or change what 
was written - redefining the existing words should not be one of them.
```

```
LX-i wrote:
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<blahblahblah>

First there was just "just". But "just" just wasn't just enough; some 
people thought "just" did injustice so then there was "mean", but "mean" 
just sounded too mean if you know what I mean so then there was "equal". 
  There were all kinds of "equal" that weren't equal.  But eventually 
there was an "equal" that was equal.  Well, what we now call "well" used 
to be "equal" until an "equal" became really equal, then the "equal"s 
that weren't equal just became "well" and, well, the "equal" that really 
was equal remained "equal".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T12:25:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0a5ms$r3t$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET> <d08f40$jh6$1@panix5.panix.com> <2f0f4$422882eb$45491f85$22993@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
In article <2f0f4$422882eb$45491f85$22993@KNOLOGY.NET>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET>,
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Revelation 22:18-19 (adding to or taking away from the words of this Book).

Quite right... if King Jim's English was good enough for God to speak then 
it should be good enough for anyone!

One might not go so far as saying, as the Swedes used to, 'Traduttori, 
traditori' ('translators are traitors') but in my experience it is... 
difficult, to say the least, to translate from Hebrew and Ancient Greek to 
English and *not* add to or take away; my favorite example of this is the 
ambiguity in Luke II:14.

>
>I'm not sure I agree with all of their reasoning, but one of the ones 
>they used was that this translation was done when English was at the 
>height of its usage, and the average number of words in an 
>English-speaker's vocabulary was quite high.

Ahhhh, for the Oldene Dayse... when an English-Speaker had a vocabulary 
such as *ten* English-Speakers cannot, today!  I do not know how the 
'they' you cite came to this conclusion about a primarily illiterate 
society but quantity does not equal quality... it might be that a goodly 
chunk of this vocabulary was devoted to words describing the different 
kinds of saddles appropriate to various feast-days or the textures of 
various kinds of fertiliser.

>Now, a scant 100-150 years 
>later, we have these founding documents.  Lots of words, lots of words 
>that have meaning, and also lots of principles contained in those words. 

Here we must disagree.  The words do *not* 'have meaning', 'meaning' is 
the result of interpretation.  Consider the sentence:

'There he founded his wold.'

What might this mean, and to whom?  It might mean, to one, that in a 
certain place he established an unforested, rolling plain or it might 
mean, to another, that someone with a curious sense of verb conjugation 
was trying to say 'there he discovered (find/found/founded) his yellow 
dye'... or it might mean nothing at all to someone who speaks no English.

>  There were very learned men who wrote these documents - doctors, 
>scientists, etc.

Don't forget the lawyers... not as many then as now, true, but 
nevertheless skilled in the manipulation of language.

>
>For someone to take a new definition of their words and say "well, I 
>know that's what they wrote, but what they *meant* was..." is dishonest. 

I cannot speak of honesty here but, once again, given that meaning is the 
result of interpretation then they wrote what they wrote and its meaning 
comes from how it is interpreted.

>  They meant what they *wrote*, no more, no less.

The authors had their intentions, they did their best (one hopes) to 
convey these intentions by what they wrote... but the meaning comes from 
the one who reads.

DD
```

```
On  4-Mar-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >I'm not sure I agree with all of their reasoning, but one of the ones
> >they used was that this translation was done when English was at the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> various kinds of fertiliser.

At various times, the literate person was a conqueror who might have Norman
French as his primary language, and anglo-saxon as the language he used with the
serfs.   After a few generations of this, languages merge with twice as many
words.   The clergy who also spoke Latin contributed as well.   The serfs didn't
write much so we don't have a good count of their vocabulary.    Maybe if we
could find ancient reality TV shows.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T14:13:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0amiv$2jd2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <6c440$422796be$45491f85$1924@KNOLOGY.NET> <d08f40$jh6$1@panix5.panix.com> <2f0f4$422882eb$45491f85$22993@KNOLOGY.NET> <d0a5ms$r3t$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d0a5ms$r3t$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <2f0f4$422882eb$45491f85$22993@KNOLOGY.NET>,

> One might not go so far as saying, as the Swedes used to, 'Traduttori,
> traditori' ('translators are traitors') ...

Swedes???? Must be bilingual ones ...

> difficult, to say the least, to translate from Hebrew and Ancient Greek to
> English and *not* add to or take away; my favorite example of this is the
> ambiguity in Luke II:14.

Ambiguity is among the Greek manuscripts, too.  The appropriate translation
of this verse is  dependent on what one takes the original text in Greek to
be.

More specifically, it depends on whether the last word of the verse is
"eudokia" or "eudokias".   I don't have a recent Nestle-Aland handy (my
copy's at home), but the edition of the TR I have handy shows the former
according to Stephanus 1554 (with Elzevir 1624), but notes that Lachman
(1850), Tischendorf (1872), Tregelles (1872) and Alford (1870) all prefer
the latter.  I seem to recall that at least three of the Great Uncial
Codices (Alexandrinus, Sinaiticus and Vaticanus) from the fourth/fifth
century AD agreed with the Vulgate (of about the same time) and earlier
Latin recensions in reflecting the latter form, and that argues strongly for
it as more likely to be the original form.

Then, of course, one could argue that the TR *really was* the text that God
intended all along, and that it took fifteen centuries for Him to correct
all those errors from earlier texts ...

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
English.  One might want to know the quality of their Greek, Hebew and of 
course Latin (when they went to the Latin Vulgate).

> For someone to take a new definition of their words and say "well, I know 
> that's what they wrote, but what they *meant* was..." is dishonest. They 
> meant what they *wrote*, no more, no less.  If we as a nation are not 
> happy with that, there is a process to clarify and/or change what was 
> written - redefining the existing words should not be one of them.
Some words have no direct translation so you have to come close.  A bird in 
the hand is worth two in the bush....means one thing to someone equipped 
with the knowledge of proverbs and an innocent mind...but entirely another 
thing to a Londoner negotiating with a hooker.

JCE
```

```
Meself. Born In London but can't recall that ornithological quote :-)

But a real one and 'afters' = "pudding" or "dessert"

This particular youth is shortcutting from Shaftsbury Avenue which leads 
down to Piccadilly. So I short cut through the back streets to get me in 
a more direct route for Leicester Square.

 From out of the shadows comes a young lady of the night. God knows what 
she really looked like - can't tell with dimmed street lights.

Usual, "Hello deary. Want some fun ?"

"No", I reply, "I've already had my dinner/supper".

There's a rasping cackle from the shadows as her ugly 'supervisor' surfaces.

"Ha ! Ha !. Wot abaht yer afters ?".

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to validate SSN

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T07:56:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5fVUd.96674$pc5.35428@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<1109597126.424606.116000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <ZMKUd.3652224$f47.652088@news.easynews.com>`

```
http://www.ssa.gov/history/ssnmyth.html

Apparently due to the fact that the middle digits of the SSN are referred to 
as the "group number," some people have misconstrued this to mean that the 
"group number" refers to racial groupings. So a myth goes around from 
time-to-time that encoded in a person's SSN is a key to their race. This 
simply is not true.

As should be clear from the explanation of the SSN numbering scheme, the 
"group number" refers only to the numerical groups 01-99. For filing 
purposes, the "area numbers" are broken down into these numerical subgroups. 
So, for example, for area numbers starting with 527 there would be 98 
subgroups, one for every number starting with 527-01, and one for every 
number starting with 527-02, and so on. This was done back in 1936 because 
in that era there were no computers and all the records were stored in 
filing cabinets. The early program administrators needed some way to 
organize the filing cabinets into sub-groups, to make them more manageable, 
and this is the scheme they came up with.

So the "group number" has nothing whatever to do with race. [Allegedly].

JCE


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:ZMKUd.3652224$f47.652088@news.easynews.com...
> There are LOTS of "disclaimers" (and I have no PERSONAL knowledge of 
> whether any/all of this is true) but from:
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
news:up8821999g81q9lmp5ujdmr0npbiqdtrl8@4ax.com...

> It is if your compiler supports "function reverse" Not all do.

It's a requirement for compliance with ANSI X3.23-1985 as amended by ANSI
X3.23a-1989, the Intrinsic Function Amendment.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right justifying (arbitrary) alphanumeric input

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T17:37:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kL1Vd.3443288$B07.538680@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<gSLUd.3656529$f47.654009@news.easynews.com> <pd67211cb9qogpfg52ps32k3dkmanfoc2a@4ax.com> <762dne6b0dIqLr7fRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <up8821999g81q9lmp5ujdmr0npbiqdtrl8@4ax.com> <d024r5$2jkc$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Sorry Chuck - but you are in error.

The '89 Amendment introduced the Intrinsic Function module as OPTIONAL.  FIPS, 
on the other hand, made it enquired in their Standard. Therefore, a "fully 
conforming High level - amended third Standard COBOL" need not implement it.

The *really* odd thing about the '89 Amendment was that it didn't even make 
"function" a reserved word UNLESS you implemented the Intrinsic Function module. 
This was changed in '91 by the "Corrections Amendment" where it was made 
reserved whether or not you implemented that module.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right justifying (arbitrary) alphanumeric input

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T09:14:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d027td$2lkn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<gSLUd.3656529$f47.654009@news.easynews.com> <pd67211cb9qogpfg52ps32k3dkmanfoc2a@4ax.com> <762dne6b0dIqLr7fRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <9S1Am6GuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9S1Am6GuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...

>
> N> inspect function reverse (input-value)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> one would not need to create a (temporary) reversed string.

I don't think the INSPECT syntax needs anything more than is already
provided by FUNCTION REVERSE.  The idea of new syntax for INSPECT to include
a REVERSE option (or a TRAILING option) was discussed, and rejected, at WG4
(in Las Vegas in 2003, IIRC).

As to "one would not need to create a (temporary) reversed string", the
*user* doesn't need to do this.

Though an *implementor's* first stab at this statement might actually
produce the reversed string in a temporary, the implementor could also
recognize the context of the REVERSE function and optimize to scan the
original text backwards.    Given enough market pressure, the implementor
might even be inclined to provide direct hardware support for doing so.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right justifying (arbitrary) alphanumeric input

- **References:** `<gSLUd.3656529$f47.654009@news.easynews.com> <pd67211cb9qogpfg52ps32k3dkmanfoc2a@4ax.com> <762dne6b0dIqLr7fRVn_vQ@giganews.com>`

```

Ron wrote:
> >>01    TEST-VARIABLES.
> >>        04 INPUT-VALUE PIC X(22) VALUE 'MYTESTVALUE'.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> inspect function reverse (input-value) tallying tally-count for
leading spaces.
> move input-value (1 : length of input-value - tally-count) to
out-value.
>
> out-value will contain '         MY TEST VALUE'

Q: Are expressions allowed in reference modification?

[Slightly OT]

Here's an interesting way to abuse function reverse.

identification division.
program-id. rot1.
data division.
working-storage section.
01 chars.
05 x pic x(26) value 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'.
05 y pic x(26).
05 z redefines y.
10 z1.
15 filler pic x occurs 1 to 25 times depending on l1.
10 z2.
15 filler pic x occurs 1 to 25 times depending on l2.
01 counters.
05 i pic 99.
05 l pic 99.
05 l1 pic 99.
05 l2 pic 99.
procedure division.
main.
    compute l = function length(x).
    perform next-i varying i from 0 by 1
      until i is greater than 26.
    stop run.
next-i.
    move x to y.
    if i is > 0 and i < l perform
      compute l1 = i
      compute l2 = l - i
      move function reverse(z1) to z1
      move function reverse(z2) to z2
      move function reverse(y) to y
    end-perform.
    display i space y.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right justifying (arbitrary) alphanumeric input

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T11:16:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d053di$1hpo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<gSLUd.3656529$f47.654009@news.easynews.com> <pd67211cb9qogpfg52ps32k3dkmanfoc2a@4ax.com> <762dne6b0dIqLr7fRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <1109776059.651885.302910@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```

<epc8@juno.com> wrote in message
news:1109776059.651885.302910@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...

> Here's an interesting way to abuse function reverse. ...

I tried to compile your program and ran into some problems:

ANSI X3.23-1985 page VI-27, 5.8.3, the OCCURS clause, syntax rule 7:  "A
data description entry that contains format 2 of the OCCURS clause may only
e followed, within that record description, by data description entries
which are subordinate to it.  Ibid., page VI-38, 5.10.3, the REDEFINES
clause, syntax rule 5:  "Neither the original definition nor the
redefinition can include a variable occurrence data item."  Note also that
the definition of the COBOL character set on page III-3 includes the
quotation mark (") but not the apostrophe (').

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 307, 13.16.36.2, OCCURS clause, syntax rule 20:  "The
subject of the entry may be followed within that record description entry
only by data description entries that are subordinate to it.".  Ibid., page
336, 13.16.42.2, REDEFINES clause, syntax rule 6:  "Neither the original
definition nor the redefinition shall include a variable-occurrence data
item."   Note that the 2002 standard adds the apostrophe and the underscore
to the COBOL character repertoire.

I'd say this program abuses rather more than the REVERSE intrinsic function!
Could you maybe show a program that illustrates your point that doesn't
violate COBOL standards?       ;-)

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> <epc8@juno.com> wrote in message
> news:1109776059.651885.302910@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANSI X3.23-1985 page VI-27, 5.8.3, the OCCURS clause, syntax rule 7:
"A
> data description entry that contains format 2 of the OCCURS clause
may only
> e followed, within that record description, by data description
entries
> which are subordinate to it.  Ibid., page VI-38, 5.10.3, the
REDEFINES
> clause, syntax rule 5:  "Neither the original definition nor the
> redefinition can include a variable occurrence data item."  Note also
that
> the definition of the COBOL character set on page III-3 includes the
> quotation mark (") but not the apostrophe (').
>
> ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 307, 13.16.36.2, OCCURS clause, syntax rule
20:  "The
> subject of the entry may be followed within that record description
entry
> only by data description entries that are subordinate to it.".
Ibid., page
> 336, 13.16.42.2, REDEFINES clause, syntax rule 6:  "Neither the
original
> definition nor the redefinition shall include a variable-occurrence
data
> item."   Note that the 2002 standard adds the apostrophe and the
underscore
> to the COBOL character repertoire.
>
> I'd say this program abuses rather more than the REVERSE intrinsic
function!
> Could you maybe show a program that illustrates your point that
doesn't
> violate COBOL standards?       ;-)
>
>     -Chuck Stevens

Thanks. I have since learned that expressions are allowed in reference
modificaion, once I separate the operators from the operands. So the
program simplifies to this:

identification division.
program-id. test1.
data division.
working-storage section.
01 x pic x(26) value "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz".
01 y pic x(26).
01 i pic 99.
01 j pic 99.
procedure division.
main.
    compute j = function length(x).
    perform next-i varying i from 0 by 1
      until i is greater than j.
    stop run.
next-i.
    move x to y.
    if i is > 0 and i < j perform
      move function reverse(y(1:i)) to y(1:i)
      move function reverse(y(i + 1:j - i)) to y(i + 1:j - i)
      move function reverse(y) to y
    end-perform.
    display i space y.

-- Elliot
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Right justifying (arbitrary) alphanumeric input

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T23:32:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v2sVd.558267$8l.13592@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1109799364.993754.281280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<gSLUd.3656529$f47.654009@news.easynews.com> <pd67211cb9qogpfg52ps32k3dkmanfoc2a@4ax.com> <762dne6b0dIqLr7fRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <1109776059.651885.302910@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <d053di$1hpo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <1109799364.993754.281280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
epc8@juno.com wrote:
> 
> Thanks. I have since learned that expressions are allowed in reference
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

OK so it works - but what does it actually do ? (Lazy way - I compiled 
and ran it) - but it will hiccup if you do the following. Take your two 
fields pic x(26) and make them pic x(44, or 50, or....). Take the 
"a.....z" and put the relevant number of spaces in FRONT of the 
alphabet, similarly put the spaces after the alphabet - although you 
don't need to do that because the spaces are understood when you say pic 
x(44). Other permutations using pic x(44), some spaces in front and at 
the end, and split the alphabet so there are spaces in the middle -

  "abcdefghij  klmnopqrstuvwxyz".

To be worthwhile it has to handle the above options and possibly more I 
haven't thought of. I can do it quite simply using some OO methods (The 
code is already there without me thinking about it) - but that's not 
much help to you.

But I can give you some pointers, and this is NOT a 
recommendation/suggestion how you should do it using Procedural COBOL - 
the string and length are passed to a method which using an array 
technique, (a TABLE in COBOL parlance), i.e. each incoming character 
(not just alpha or numeric) is identified by a position/index in the 
array. Take the split alpha above - it ignores the spaces at the front 
and end and the spaces between "...hij" and "klm". Trying to keep this 
simple - it has a second array of 'words' the 'semi-words' you find 
without a space - those 'semi-words' get stored with differing lengths 
and then they are all STRUNG back together again.

You need to keep a count of the words it finds. So what's happening in 
OO - your y pic x(44) is initialized then I can do a

perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MyWordCount

    STRING the current "semi-word" into y.
    Second time around y contains the first 'semi-word' and now
       we string the second 'semi-word" into the existing y
    etc...

end-perform

Simplest way I would suggest, keeping a count of total characters found 
- do a Reference Modification where you want right justified, otherwise 
automatically left justified.

Now the original version I wrote, using the support methods just handled 
strings with the option of including one space between 'real words' in 
the resultant 01 y pic x(44) above, or string the whole thing together 
ignoring ALL spaces. Then because I want to take a text field and 
display it in a Dialog or as a label in a Treeview, then I added options 
to get back the text with/without x'00' at the end.

If the last bit sounds like first-class gibberish :-

01 x pic x(50) value "George Washington".

then you get back

01 y pic x(50)

which will have a value of 'George Washington" + x'00' - a 'real' total 
of 18 used characters.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> epc8@juno.com wrote:

[snip]  move x to y.

> >     if i is > 0 and i < j perform
> >       move function reverse(y(1:i)) to y(1:i)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >     display i space y.

[snip]

> OK so it works - but what does it actually do ? (Lazy way - I
compiled
> and ran it) - but it will hiccup if you do the following. Take your
two
> fields pic x(26) and make them pic x(44, or 50, or....). Take the
> "a.....z" and put the relevant number of spaces in FRONT of the
> alphabet,
[snip]

I was merely trying to illustrate doing something fun with function
reverse. Of course this would have been simpler:

if i is greater than 0 and i is less than j
  move x(1:i) to y(j - i + 1:i)
  move x(i + 1:j - i) to y(1:j - i)
else
  move x to y
end-if.

In its original context, rotating a string around end in place with
minimal temporary storage, this technique works well, but of course
this is in a language more primitive than COBOL. Also, if I have to do
things character by character, I like to reverse a string as follows
(pesudo-code):

p=start_loc
q=end_loc
while(p<q)
  swap s[p] and s[q]
  p=p+1
  q=q-1

This avoids having to figure out the length of the string and when to
stop. It also handles some special boundary cases, for example when the
string is empty.
```

```
Reflexive action can sometimes surprise the users who were expecting to
see
double spaces kept intact.

My clients routinely space out addresses and product descriptions to
get the effects they require within the free-form text fields.  They do
not want the computer to arbitralily change the way they input it.
```

<a id="t-9fd31bf4b4"></a>
## Thread: Micro Focus and "dialects" — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2005-02](2005-02.md#t-9fd31bf4b4)._

#### ↳ Re: Micro Focus and "dialects"

- **From:** "Brian Crane" <brian.crane@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T13:11:34
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d01olm$geo$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<C%3Ud.3190795$B07.504694@news.easynews.com>`

```
This following is my response speaking primarily from an IBM mainframe 
perspective. The latest products from Micro Focus make it very easy to 
develop code that is compatible with the target environment. The Micro Focus 
compiler itself is highly flexible, allowing conformance to a great range of 
COBOL dialects and standards through a number of compiler options and 
settings that can be configured as appropriate. In Mainframe Express 
Enterprise Edition (the latest product for development and testing of 
mainframe applications) we have made the configuration very easy. Instead of 
our customers requiring detailed knowledge of our compiler in order to 
fine-tune the settings to match the desired mainframe compiler 
version/level, we have a simple drop-down list that allows you to choose the 
desired mainframe compiler level. Within each choice (eg. Enterprise COBOL 
for z/OS, COBOL for OS/390, COBOL for MVS, VS COBOL II... etc) there are 
further options that allow you to match the compilation and runtime 
behaviour to the specific compiler options that you have set on the 
mainframe. When using mainframe dialects, the use of non-mainframe COBOL 
features in your source code produces a compile error on the PC, which 
therefore makes it very difficult to write code that will not run on z/OS. 
These settings can be easily adopted throughout the development organization 
through the use of  'templates' which define the shop-specific standards for 
the development and testing of your applications.

I hope this helps to clarify things.

Best regards,

Brian Crane
Product Director - Mainframe Application Development Products
http://www.microfocus.com/products/mfeee


 It is true that in older products such as COBOL Workbench, there was a 
range of setting.
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:C%3Ud.3190795$B07.504694@news.easynews.com...
> When I was a Micro Focus employee (about a decade ago now), it seems to me 
> that I spent VAST amounts of my time dealing with customers on the 
…[130 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
