## Thread: convert ascii file to ebcdid on mainframe — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### convert ascii file to ebcdid on mainframe

- **From:** mark.ludlow@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T07:50:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109692229.694658.261450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
My shop is having trouble converting a ascii file to ebcdic using
simple JCL.

We have explored using the OPTCD=Q option in a IEBGENER step but we
can't get anything but tape with standard labels on it.

Anyone have a trick up their sleeve?
```

#### ↳ Re: convert ascii file to ebcdid on mainframe

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T20:17:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-182462.20171801032005@ispnews.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1109692229.694658.261450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1109692229.694658.261450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>,
 mark.ludlow@gmail.com wrote:

> My shop is having trouble converting a ascii file to ebcdic using
> simple JCL.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone have a trick up their sleeve?


You could try the TCP/IP programs EZATCP04 and EXATCP05 that convert 
between ASCII and EBCDIC.  

I think IBM packages a few others as well in other products, but you are 
guaranteed to have TCP/IP at the current OS level.

Failing that, you might copy the ASCII dataset to a tape (or virtual 
tape) and the copy it back to DAST with OPTCD=Q.

Is that the same as TRTCH=Q?
```

#### ↳ Re: convert ascii file to ebcdid on mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T04:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<H%aVd.3789541$f47.674287@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1109692229.694658.261450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```
Does the source code ONLY include "text" data (i.e. numbers with "+" and "-" and 
"." as separate readable characters) or does it include any "COBOL-type" binary 
or packed-decimal data.  If it does include non-display numeric data conversion 
is NOT easy.  If all the stuff is text-like, then it should be medium easy. 
(Current Enterprise COBOL provides a facility for doing this via the NATIONAL-OF 
and DISPLAY-OF intrinsic functions.  Utilities probably exist as well)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: convert ascii file to ebcdid on mainframe

- **From:** Lawrence Greenwald <lgreenwa@cts.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T07:11:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lgreenwa-E2E130.23100401032005@chiapp18.algx.net>`
- **References:** `<1109692229.694658.261450@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <H%aVd.3789541$f47.674287@news.easynews.com>`

```
In article <H%aVd.3789541$f47.674287@news.easynews.com>,
 "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> Does the source code ONLY include "text" data (i.e. numbers with "+" and "-" 
> and 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> and DISPLAY-OF intrinsic functions.  Utilities probably exist as well)

OPTCD=Q only works on non-labelled tapes - "LABEL=(x,NL)" in JCL 
parlance where 'x' is the file number. It cannot be on a standard 
labelled tape because the label info will be written in EBCDIC (vs ASCII 
the data is in). You can't mix modes - EBCDIC or ASCII on a tape, not 
both and you can't write ASCII to a disk file.

I know, I'm still a mainframer and I deal with this on a regular basis.

--LG
```

---

<a id="t-23189034a3"></a>
## Thread: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: A very *PERSONAL* view of ISO 2002 COBOL for the IBM (mainframe) environment

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T09:02:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0277a$2l81$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
P. S. to my prior:

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:...
>
> "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
> news:DATUd.96666$pc5.23981@tornado.tampabay.rr.com...
>
> > But I still think that there is a large distinction from _discouraging_
to
> > not _providing_.

I certainly suggest the implementor *provide* the (standard) feature;
without it, in fact, their compiler is not compliant with the current
standard.  The implementor will provide the *extension* -- or not -- as his
marketplace dictates.

As to *discouragement*:  I once ran across a fully-conformant COBOL(68)
program that had clearly been written by someone who was fluent in ForTran,
and who equally-clearly thought the ALTER verb was the Bee's Knees.  Clearly
the least-maintainable program I have ever seen.

An INSPECT...REPLACING on an edited-numeric data item can switch the sign.
I would discourage users from taking that approach.

As a friend of mine was fond of saying "You may not get what you want.  You
may not even get what you need.  But one thing's for sure:  You get what you
get."

    -Chuck Stevens.
```

---

```
I wrote:

> It's a requirement for compliance with ANSI X3.23-1985 as amended by ANSI
> X3.23a-1989, the Intrinsic Function Amendment.

Bill Klein pointed out privately that the Intrinsic Function amendment is
*optional*.  I think I've made that mistake before.  My apologies (again).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
NEED QUICK EASY CASH???
Turn $6.00 into $60,000...read this to find out how!!!
READING THIS COULD CHANGE YOUR LIFE!!!
I found this on a bulletin board like this one and decided to try it. A
little while back, I was
browsing through a newsgroup, just like you are now, and came across an
article similar to this
that said you could make thousands of dollars within weeks with only an
initial investment of
$6.00! So I thought, "Yeah right, this must be a scam", but like most
of us, I was curious, so I
kept reading. Anyway, it said that you need to send $1.00 to each of
the 6 names and address
stated in the article. You then place your own name and address in the
bottom of the list at #6,
and post the article in at least 200 newsgroups. (There are thousands)
No catch, that was it. So
after thinking it over, and talking to a few people first, I thought
about trying it. I figured "what
have I got to lose except 6 stamps and $6.00, right?" Then I invested
the measly $6.00 (I use the
word "measly" because $6 really is measly compared to the money I have
made through the
initial investment). Well GUESS WHAT!!... within 7 days, I started
getting money in the mail! I
was shocked! I figured it would end soon, but the money just kept
coming in.
In my first week, I made about $25.00. By the end of the second week I
had made a total of over
$1,000.00! In the third week I had over $10,000.00 and it's still
growing. This is now my fourth
week and I have made just over $42,000.00 and it's still coming in
rapidly. It's certainly worth
$6.00, and 6 stamps, I have spent more than that on the lottery!! Let
me tell you how this works
and most importantly, why it works.... Also, make sure you print a copy
of this article NOW, so
you can get the information off of it as you need it. I promise you
that if you follow the directions
exactly, that you will start making more money than you thought
possible by doing something so
easy!
Suggestion: Read this entire message carefully! (print it out or
download it.)
Follow the simple directions and watch the money come in! It's easy.
It's legal. And, your
investment is only $6.00 (Plus postage)
IMPORTANT: This is not a rip-off; it is not indecent; it is not
illegal; and it is virtually no risk -
it really works!!!! If all of the following instructions are adhered
to, you will receive
extraordinary dividends.
PLEASE NOTE: Please follow these directions EXACTLY, and $50,000 or
more can be yours
in 20 to 60 days. This program remains successful because of the
honesty and integrity of the
participants. Please continue it's success by carefully adhering to
the instructions. You will now
become part of the Mail Order business. In this business your product
is not solid and tangible,
it's a service. You are in the business of developing Mailing Lists.
Many large corporations are
happy to pay big bucks for quality lists. However, the money made from
the mailing lists is
secondary to the income which is made from people like you and me
asking to be included in that
list.
Here are the 4 easy steps to success:
STEP 1: Get 6 separate pieces of paper and write the following on each
piece of paper
"PLEASE PUT ME ON YOUR MAILING LIST."
Now get 6 US $1.00 bills and place ONE inside EACH of the 6 pieces of
paper so the bill will
not be seen through the envelope (to prevent thievery).
Next, place one paper in each of the 6 envelopes and seal them. You
should now have 6 sealed
envelopes, each with a piece of paper stating the phrase "PLEASE PUT ME
ON YOUR
MAILING LIST.", your name and address, and a $1.00 bill. What you are
doing is creating a
service. THIS IS ABSOLUTELY LEGAL! You are requesting a legitimate
service and you are
paying for it!
Like most of us I was a little skeptical and a little worried about the
legal aspects of it all. So I
checked it out with the U.S. Post Office (1-800-725-2161) and they
confirmed that it is indeed
legal! Mail the 6 envelopes to the following addresses:


#1) Damon Bailey
1055 Brookfield Dr.
Medina, OH 44256

#2) Krysta Johnson
9821 Camino Villa #1024
San Antonio, TX 78254

#3) Daniel Torres
2727 W. Oak Ridge Rd.
Bld:8 Apt:3
Orlando Fl. 32809

#4) A.Strachan
P.O.Box CR-56992
Nassau, Bahamas

#5) Kim Pepin
1056 Centennial Cres.
Corbeil , Ontario, Canada
P0H 1K0

#6) Michael Sullivan
155 Parrott Rd.
West Nyack, NY, 10994

STEP 2: Now take the #1 name off the list that you see above, move the
other names up (6
becomes 5, 5 becomes 4, etc...) and add YOUR Name as number 6 on the
list.
STEP 3: Change anything you need to, but try to keep this article as
close to original as possible.
Now, post your amended article to at least 200 newsgroups. (I think
there are close to 32,000+)
All you need is 200, but remember, the more you post, the more money
you make!
This is perfectly legal! If you have any doubts, refer to Title 18 Sec.
1302 & 1341 of the Postal
lottery laws. Keep a copy of these steps for yourself and, whenever you
need money, you can use
it again, and again.
PLEASE REMEMBER that this program remains successful because of the
honesty and
integrity
of the participants and by their carefully adhering to the directions.
Look at it this way. If you are
of integrity, the program will continue and the money that so many
others have received will
come your way.
NOTE: You may want to retain every name and address sent to you, either
on a computer or hard
copy and keep the notes people send you. This VERIFIES that you are
truly providing a service.
(Also, it might be a good idea to wrap the $1 bill in dark paper to
reduce the risk of mail theft.)
So, as each post is downloaded and the directions carefully followed,
six members will be
reimbursed for their participation as a List Developer with one dollar
each. Your name will move
up the list geometrically so that when your name reaches the #1
position you will be receiving
thousands of dollars in CASH!!! What an opportunity for only $6.00
($1.00 for each of the first
six people listed above)
Send it now, add your own name to the list and you're in business!
---DIRECTIONS ----- FOR HOW TO POST TO NEWSGROUPS------------
Step 1) You do not need to re-type this entire letter to do your own
posting. Simply put your
cursor at the beginning of this letter and drag your cursor to the
bottom of this document, and
select 'copy' from the edit menu. This will copy the entire letter into
the computer's memory.
Step 2) Open a blank 'notepad' file and place your cursor at the top of
the blank page. From the
'edit' menu select 'paste'. This will paste a copy of the letter into
notepad so that you can add your
name to the list.
Step 3) Save your new notepad file as a .txt file. If you want to do
your postings in different
settings, you'll always have this file to go back to.
Step 4) Use Netscape or Internet explorer and try searching for various
newsgroups (on-line
forums, message boards, chat sites, discussions, etc.)
Step 5) Visit these message boards and post this article as a new
message by highlighting the text
of this letter and selecting paste from the edit menu. Fill in the
Subject, this will be the header
that everyone sees as they scroll through the list of postings in a
particular group, click the post
message button. You're done with your first one!
Congratulations...THAT'S IT!
All you have to do is jump to different newsgroups and post away, after
you get the hang of it, it
will take about 30 seconds for each newsgroup!
**REMEMBER, THE MORE NEWSGROUPS YOU POST IN, THE MORE
MONEY YOU
WILL MAKE!! BUT YOU HAVE TO POST A MINIMUM OF 200**
That's it! You will begin receiving money from around the world within
days! You may
eventually want to rent a P.O.Box due to the large amount of mail you
will receive. If you wish to
stay anonymous, you can invent a name to use, as long as the postman
will deliver it.
**JUST MAKE SURE ALL THE ADDRESSES ARE CORRECT.**

Now the WHY part: Out of 200 postings, say I receive only 5 replies (a
very low example). So
then I made $5.00 with my name at #6 on the letter. Now, each of the 5
persons who just sent me
$1.00 make the MINIMUM 200 postings, each with my name at #5 and only 5
persons respond
to each of the original 5, that is another $25.00 for me, now those 25
each make 200 MINIMUM
posts with my name at #4 and only 5 replies each, I will bring in an
additional $125.00! Now,
those 125 persons turn around and post the MINIMUM 200 with my name at
#3 and only receive
5 replies each, I will make an additional $626.00! OK, now here is the
fun part, each of those 625
persons post a MINIMUM 200 letters with my name at #2 and they each
only receive 5 replies,
that just made me $3,125.00!!! Those 3,125 persons will all deliver
this message to 200
newsgroups with my name at #1 and if still ONLY 5 persons per 200
newsgroups react I will
receive $15,625,00! With an original investment of only $6.00! AMAZING!
When your name is
no longer on the list, you just take the latest posting in the
newsgroups, and send out another
$6.00 to names on the list, putting your name at number 6 again. And
start posting again.
The thing to remember is do you realize that thousands of people all
over the world are joining
the internet and reading these articles everyday?, JUST LIKE YOU are
now!! So, can you afford
$6.00 and see if it really works?? I think so... People have said,
"what if the plan is played out
and no one sends you the money? So what! What are the chances of that
happening when there
are tons of new honest users and new honest people who are joining the
internet and newsgroups
everyday and are willing to give it a try? Estimates are at 20,000 to
50,000 new users, every day,
with thousands of those joining the actual internet. Remember, play
FAIRLY and HONESTLY
and this will really work!!!
Please keep this going!!! It's incredible... We can all help eachother
with some of our
financial problems!!! Thanks and have yourself a great day!
```

## Thread: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations — 46 msgs

### [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-01T18:58:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

Sorry, lost the original someplace... but here are the cites from my copy 
of The Compact Edition of the Oxford English Dictionary, Oxford University 
Press, c.1971, 20th Printing 1981, page 715, sub-page 312, column 1, first 
complete entry:

- under entry 1

1607 SHAKS. Timon II. ii. 52 Var. How dost Foole?  Ape. Dost dialogue with 
thy shadow?

(note - the text in shaks12.txt, downloaded from Project Gutenberg, 
renders this exchange as:

  VARRO'S SERVANT. How dost, fool?
  APEMANTUS. Dost dialogue with thy shadow?)

1817 COLERIDGE Biog Lit (1882) 286 Those puppet-heroines for whom the 
showman contrives to dialogue without any skill in ventriloquism.

1858 CARLYLE Fredk Gt I. IV. v. 426 Much semi-articulate questioning and 
dialoguing with Dame de Roucoulles.

DD
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38kp38F5p62e7U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
Excellent. Thanks Doc.

I don't believe I have ever read Timon of Athens.

I still don't like the sound of it, but at least I know it has credence.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>

```
DD

Do you have anything more moderner? [that is rhetorical]  The point of the 
original post was that dialogue "shouldn't" be  used as a verb - and 98% of 
the Usage Panel  agree (though, as you point out, they only reads literature 
produced in the last 100 years [or specifically avoid Shakespeare and 
Coleridge for reasons I simply _cannot_ fathom] and represents the millions 
of plebians who don't care enough to engage themselves as part of the 
panel - though it doesn't appear to be a democratically provided 
representation of people).

That doesn't make him [original poster] right, but it does mean that 
according to 98% of the people judging the validity of usage he is right. 
It doesn't make him right....but it does mean that according to 98% of the 
people judging the validity of usage he is right.

You have proved that it is defined in one reputable reference text and that 
it _has_ been used - but should it still be used?

I might start talking Shakespearean English from this point forth - 
unfortunately I don't think babelfish has English->Shakespear translation 
available right now and I'm not that literary.

But anyway, I'm done with this thread, and you've done far more to prove 
that you have the knowledge of a dictionary and its uses than I ever 
required ...

All I can say is that I hope I don't have to read any books with dialog as a 
verb, as from the quotes provided they all seem somewhat tedious.

And on the final note, I think we all ignored the fact that the original 
poster was just trying to make a disparaging remark to Kellie.....how cool 
is that - a thread that took something not positive and turned it into a 
debate on the relevance of Shakespeare in modern linguistics - and ALL in a 
COBOL group.

JCE
```

```
>> DD
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> although the Brit in me, would years ago, have wanted to throw up.
It wasn't.  What the hell is winterize? Never mind.....

> It truly is amazing how people can misinterpret something, even when 
> communicated in writing. Perhaps we should accompany our messages with a 
> jpg showing a picture of the author with either a quizzical look or a sly 
> smile on his/her face.
It is also amazing how quickly people jump and get excited about thinking 
they've been accused by a random person of coming across as disparaging in a 
newsgroup that is only about 400 groups away from women engaging in 
activities with farm animals :-)

A series of written words, even  with emoticons,  _is_ very different from 
spoken words.  That is why we are in clc and not off writing books that sit 
on the shelves next to Coleridge or Shakespeare.

> Jimmy

Having gone back to the original post. I determined that it was perhaps not 
"disparaging" even if it was just short, curt and to the point, so apologies 
to the original poster should he have read this far into the thread.

Sorry for unintentionally getting you riled!

JCE
```

```
LX-i wrote:
> jce wrote:

Had a look out of curiosity,. If I didn't actually speak English, be 
buggered if I could pronounce the word using their phonetic representation.

Still takes a while to 'Americanize'. I had to convert my 'windscreen' 
to a 'windshield' and my 'bonnet' to a 'hood'.

My wife followed later at an auto shop where I had been. The mechanic 
grinned and said to her, "I knew where he came from. He said his wing 
was damaged". Hell ! I WAS in the RAF for twelve years :-)

But the best is from a Brit. Bill will recall, he arranged a meeting for 
me with this young man back in 2000 at Newbury. Steve Biggs was replying 
in Answer Exchange to something I had written and he INVENTED the word 
'productized". "Good grief !", I replied, "We've got Brits at it now !".

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T15:42:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<lwvVd.560208$6l.483935@pd7tw2no>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <%cmVd.123973$qB6.75102@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <lBmVd.556058$Xk.241537@pd7tw3no> <BfnVd.97941$pc5.56797@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <a9bac$42266647$45491f85$14354@KNOLOGY.NET> <lwvVd.560208$6l.483935@pd7tw2no>`

```
> and my 'bonnet' to a 'hood'.

Interstingly (YMMV) some of the differences in the terms have sensible
origins.

When cars first started being used in England they were for short trips
to or through the countryside, the roads were unmade so a good pair of
wellies was required equipment. Not wanting to clutter up the insides
with dirty footwear the manufacturers added a 'boot locker'.

When cars first started being used in America they were for longer
trips, usually overnight, and so they required something to take the
clothes.  Often a cabin trunk was strapped to the back, then
manufacturers added one as a fixure.

Hence boot and trunk.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T16:11:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0893i$q2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <%cmVd.123973$qB6.75102@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <lBmVd.556058$Xk.241537@pd7tw3no> <BfnVd.97941$pc5.56797@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <a9bac$42266647$45491f85$14354@KNOLOGY.NET> <lwvVd.560208$6l.483935@pd7tw2no> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <HpNVd.567728$6l.307848@pd7tw2no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:HpNVd.567728$6l.307848@pd7tw2no...

> Interesting Richard. Help me out. Daniel just quizzed me on 'wing'. What
> the hell was I referring to in 'Americanese' - for the life of me can't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> when the 'front' wasn't a solid piece. Help me !


Think what the Yanks call a "fender" on just about anything British from
before WWII.  It's like a bird's wing on the side of the car.  Think MG
TC/TD/TF.  Think Model A Ford.

The one I could never get straight was "nearside" and "offside", since the
former seems to be the one *further* from the driver ("the side of a vehicle
nearest the kerb").

A friend had a very rare Morgan +4 (50 copies of this model) that was
seriously damaged in an accident.  Took many months of correspondence to get
all the terminology translated ("right-side headlight trim ring" => "offside
headlamp door" being one example -- note that it was "offside" even though
it was the side *nearest* the curb on this side of the pond!).  Repair took
over a year and a half all told.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T16:21:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109895718.512711.215220@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d0893i$q2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <%cmVd.123973$qB6.75102@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <lBmVd.556058$Xk.241537@pd7tw3no> <BfnVd.97941$pc5.56797@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <a9bac$42266647$45491f85$14354@KNOLOGY.NET> <lwvVd.560208$6l.483935@pd7tw2no> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <HpNVd.567728$6l.307848@pd7tw2no> <d0893i$q2s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
> the Yanks call a "fender"

Ah, yes, fender.  Does that say something about their driving habits ?

> The one I could never get straight was "nearside" and "offside",
since the
> former seems to be the one *further* from the driver ("the side of a
vehicle
> nearest the kerb").

That has nothing to do with 'driver', but with 'rider'. A horse is
always mounted on the nearside (left) and dismounted on the offside
(off side).  Think of it as port and starboard for horses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T11:46:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0a3d9$hmb$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  3-Mar-2005, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>trunk", which are foreign to me.

One of the differences between American and BritSpeak is that the former 
will tend to use brand-names for generic items while the latter tends 
towards using the name of the inventor or a well-known user.

In the USA a 'kleenex' (a brand-named owned by Kimberly-Clark) is a 
tissue, a 'band-aid' (owned by Johnson & Johnson) is an adhesive bandage, 
a 'frigidaire' (owned by the Frigidaire Corp.) is a refrigerator - 
althought the latter is falling out of use - and in The Empire 
'wellingtons' (affectionate diminutive 'wellies') are galoshes (after the 
Duke of Wellington), 'macadam' is tar-covered pavement and 'plimsols' are 
canvas shoes (sneakers) (after the resemblance of their mudguards to the 
marking system developed to indicate a ship's load-lines by Samuel 
Plimsoll).

A little-known similarity might be found in another name used for the 
aforementioned Macadam; a unsucessful tar-based road-surfacing material 
was developed by Frederick Ah ('Ah' pronounced as the 'a' in 'mat').  This 
material was notorious for becoming loose and gooey during the heat of 
summer; upon coming to the city the country-folk would be amazed by the 
filth that would cling to their shoes and would find themselves involved 
in an interchange along the lines of:

'What is all this muck? Who's responsible for all that mess in the 
streets?'

'Oh, that?  That's...

... Ah's fault.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T17:15:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0a58q$sjb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0a3d9$hmb$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On  4-Mar-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> 'macadam' is tar-covered pavement and 'plimsols' are
> canvas shoes (sneakers) (after the resemblance of their mudguards to the
> marking system developed to indicate a ship's load-lines by Samuel
> Plimsoll).

I can trace down to the exact day that USAmericans started calling airport
aprons (which are usually made out of concrete), tarmacs.    This came from the
raid on Entebbe 1976.   The US Networks weren't there, so we listened to BBC
announcers who used this unfamiliar word "tarmac" instead of asphalt.  
USAmericans looked at the pictures and saw that they were referring to that
airport apron and inferred this new meaning.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T18:13:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7z1Wd.570700$Xk.433592@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d0a58q$sjb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0a3d9$hmb$1@panix5.panix.com> <d0a58q$sjb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  4-Mar-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> airport apron and inferred this new meaning.

I can relate to aprons from RAF days. A bit vague, but rather than the 
runway, it referred to the area surrounding the runway, or near hangars, 
where aircraft were parked. Unless it's changed since I left, I think 
the word runway is used rather than tarmac. (The BBC commentators of 
course were not in the RAF :-) ).

Jimmy
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0a9b8$2cp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0a3d9$hmb$1@panix5.panix.com> <d0a58q$sjb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <7z1Wd.570700$Xk.433592@pd7tw3no>`

```
On  4-Mar-2005, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

> > I can trace down to the exact day that USAmericans started calling airport
> > aprons (which are usually made out of concrete), tarmacs.    This came from
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> course were not in the RAF :-) ).

They weren't referring to the them being on the runway or apron.   They were
referring them to being on the asphalt, which can be known as MacAdam road
(after the inventor), or tar-macadam or tarmac.    The material and the
structure got confused by USAmericans.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T17:27:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9U0Wd.572087$6l.445554@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  3-Mar-2005, "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> trunk", which are foreign to me.

Richard can do it without any help, but :-

"wellies' - slang if you like but refers to the boots the Duke of 
Wellington wore (Battle of Waterloo and subsequently Prime Minister). A 
reflection of Britain's honouring the man. But 'wellies' is now taken to 
mean rubber boots, garden boots. Drawings of Christopher Robin show him 
wearing 'wellies', sometimes, when accompanied by Winnie the Pooh.

"cabin trunk" - the trunk (large suitcase) you took as a passenger when 
going to sea and was put in the cabin you had booked. Recall Richard's 
previous reference to port and starboard - relates to this going to sea 
as well. 'Posh' people, (affluent people) - "Port Out, Starboard Home" - 
booking your cabin on the side that gave you a view of the coast.

Jimmy
```

```
fewer letters than 'starboard'... but my teachers had their senses of 
humor, I was also taught 'drag these ropes to the front end of the boat'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T11:28:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109964531.057213.68750@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d0a9r3$s2u$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U0Wd.572087$6l.445554@pd7tw2no> <d0a68t$531$1@panix5.panix.com> <d0a94h$28r$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d0a9r3$s2u$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
> I was taught that 'left' has fewer letters than 'right' and 'port'
has
> fewer letters than 'starboard'

The colours used also fit that: red is shorter than green and those
align with the other words.  The way to rember all is thinking of
having run out of an after dinner drink:

There is no RED PORT LEFT.

Starboard comes from the time before rudders were central on the stern
of the boat (a Chinese invention) and an oar, or a modified one, was
used to steer over the stern quarter.  For right handed sailors this
was on the right hand side which was then called the 'Steering Board
Side'.

Because the steering board was delicate and needed room to operate this
was never put alongside a dock and so the other side was always
alongside the port's dock and was the 'Port Side'.

When navigating at night the colours are important for avoiding
collisions, so simple ditties should be memorized:

   Red to Red
   or Green to Green
   she'll be right
   go straight between.

no, wait, that should be:

   Green to Green
   or Red to Red
   all is clear
   go straight ahead.
```

```
Civil War encampment under the command of General Hooker.

were "Hooker's".

And that is what he printed...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] 'Dialogue' As a Verb - OED Citations

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-04T10:27:49-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1109960869.049771.258240@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<d02vje$cqi$1@panix5.panix.com> <1109893349.131243.20580@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d09tpe$o6m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
> Especially your use of words such as "wellies" and "cabin
> trunk", which are foreign to me.

Wellies = Wellington boots, named after the Duke of Wellington.

On passenger ships of years ago people put their belongings into trunks
which were then put in the 'trunk room' for storage, stacked to the
roof.  By making the lid of a trunk curved it became impossible for
anything to be stacked on top, so itself was the top layer and
accessible, or was not put in the trunk room and had to be put in the
cabin.
```

<a id="t-1dfb4a3e9c"></a>
## Thread: [OT] Changing the CPU Regression Test — 8 msgs

### [OT] Changing the CPU Regression Test

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T17:11:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tmVd.123989$qB6.32044@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
All.

Just a dumb question.

I work in an environement where they do somewhat silly things.  As an 
example : the code freeze.  This a period in the year around financial 
reporting where they "freeze" any code changes without special exemptions 
even code fixes that may prevent some problem from occurring. Ironically, 
this is a _good_ time to upgrade O/S, DBMS and CICS.

I have rationalized and accepted this.

But now I have a new one:
We are undergoing a CPU upgrade. The business group wants to run a pre and 
post test using the prior months data to validate that the same amounts and 
financial figures are calculated to ensure that a CPU upgrade didn't affect 
the output.
Now, I know that Intel had some floating point math errors - but what are 
the chance? Anyone have any idea why a CPU upgrade what affect COBOL math ?

This is Z/OS batch JCL environment....they also want to prove that the 
number of datasets produced before and after is the same......this one I'm 
_really_ struggling with.   I can only imagine that somewhere along the line 
that a SOX audit had left a comment on a dataset creation somewhere.  I'm 
beginning to really wonder whether the right people drive SOX into technical 
environments - I'm not sure how a finance graduate working for KPMG or the 
like understands an operating environment.

JCE
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Changing the CPU Regression Test

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T19:13:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JfoVd.3862239$f47.692287@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<7tmVd.123989$qB6.32044@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
If in the change of CPU's they *also* change release levels of z/OS (or even 
POSSIBLY "maintenance" levels of z/OS) this can impact LE and LE run-time 
options.  Therefore, some of what they are talking about MIGHT change - if the 
upgrade was not done "correctly".

That's the best that I can think of.

P.S.  even the number of datasets could change if they don't keep the same 
CBLQDA run-time setting.
```

```
And, the chances of "correctly" are slim - at one point the "plan" was to

They didn't consider any of the implications of _just_ switching it off.

This may have answered my question: maybe the level of trust they have in

JCE
```

```
I don't think this is 'silly'...

Whether there is minimal or no technical risk involved or not, it will
simply make everybody feel better. How can that be bad?

Of course, if it involves setting up hundreds of datasets and a lot of
effort, then I can see how you might baulk a bit.

Why not take an image backup of the current datasets (you probably have
anyway), restore last month's environment, re-run on the new processor, get
the outputs, then restore the current environment?

It's a lot of work, but the system (not you... :-)) has to do it (mainly as
batch), and some of it has probably already been done as part of SOP.

Good chance to test disaster recover procedures while you're at it :-)

Pete.
```

```
One thing we found when we place our system on different hardware is

things that the new hardware, being fast and all, didn't.  It's not a
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Changing the CPU Regression Test

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-02T22:31:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<112d4oderk0gk8e@news.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<7tmVd.123989$qB6.32044@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <2e665$42266386$45491f85$9532@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
LX-i wrote:
>
> I've never worked in that environment, but I have a feeling that if I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> bad idea - unless you're not paid by the hour.  ;)

I once heard about an engineer who had a piddly Fortran program he needed to 
run about once a week. It only took two minutes, but without its output he 
was dead in the water.

Thing was, since his job was so insignificant, he kept getting automatically 
pushed to the back of the queue. Sometimes it was DAYS before his little job 
was done.

He created a subprogram to compute the inverse of a giant matrix. Burned up 
an immense amount of CPU cycles. This had the amazing effect of bumping his 
job to the head of the queue! Other engineers wondered how he was able to 
get such high priority. He began to share his subprogram. Soon all the 
engineers were happy, but management was going nuts, what with all the 
increased computer demands.

Management ordered a newer, faster computer.

Shortly it arrived and there were CPU cycles for everyone. The engineers, no 
longer having a need for a cycle-eater, removed the subprogram from all 
their submission. CPU time and wall-clock time plummeted.

The computer manufacturer re-wrote their sales brochures for the new machine 
(and raised the price).

Everyone was happy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Changing the CPU Regression Test

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T07:02:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:2e665$42266386$45491f85$9532@KNOLOGY.NET...

> One thing we found when we place our system on different hardware is that 
> the slowness of our hardware actually allowed us to get away with things 
> that the new hardware, being fast and all, didn't.  It's not a bad idea - 
> unless you're not paid by the hour.  ;)

This happened our last upgrade - turns out that somewhere we had code 
generating unique keys from the full timestamp.  The new cpu let us get two 
to three of the same generated key per transaction.  I hope that the 
solution that someone added was not to add an extra PERFORM 
DO-STUFF-TO-WASTE, but in this world, who knows?  :-)

But nice idea, and may have been a consideration

JCE
```

```
An interesting position. Between a rock and a hard place.
Internal audits prevent failures by external auditors. They
seem costly, and unnecessary.]

I remember one time when an employee did an audit on the incentive
calculation to verify a paycheck.

Short story, the payroll program had been making this mistake for
years. It was not a simple task to go back, and re-calculate the
correct amount for everyone under that plan, and clean up the
books by paying everyone what they should have.  All because,---
who do you point to here? The IE's created the rate tables,
the systems turned it into a program, and many people were paid
wrong for a long time. The  internal auditors had not caught it.
Extreme caution seems silly, but......  calculate the cost of
not doing the audit in the first place.

Warren
```

```
> Er kann die Tinte nicht halten, und wenn es ihm ankommt, jemand zu besudeln, so besudelt er sich gemeiniglich am meisten. -G.C.Lichtenberg

I think it's called the 'Cardboard Programmer'. At least, it is in the 
case where you ask a colleague to be a cardboard programmer so that you 
can explain an inexplicable problem to them. They don't neccessarily 
have to listen to a word, provided you listen to your own explanation, 
and don't even have to understand the programming language involved :)
```

- **References:** `<xUuUd.4232$kz6.123606@news20.bellglobal.com> <9RhyRBd9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <IQqVd.48711$Z14.36413@news.indigo.ie>`

```

"Mic" <mic0@eircom.net> wrote in message
news:IQqVd.48711$Z14.36413@news.indigo.ie...
> Lueko Willms wrote:
> > .    On  27.02.05
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Er kann die Tinte nicht halten, und wenn es ihm ankommt, jemand zu
besudeln, so besudelt er sich gemeiniglich am meisten. -G.C.Lichtenberg
>
> I think it's called the 'Cardboard Programmer'. At least, it is in the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, I've worked on sites where they employed cardboard programmers. They
are ideal employees, never demand a raise, never bitch about workloads,
never argue with management, and best of all, they are incapable of
independent thought.

The trick is to have ONE human being on the site (who has to be paid), let
him think he is part of a team (but in fact the rest of the team are
cardboard programmers) and he'll actually solve his own problems by
interacting with his cardboard colleagues.

Leaving the above whimsy aside for a moment, there was a time in the 1980s
when IBM used development teams comprised of 'brains' and 'bunnies'. There
would be one 'brain' and a number of 'bunnies'.  It is a similar concept...
'brains' working in isolation were not as prodctive as those working with
'bunnies'.

(Of course the talent employed at IBM was such that some of the brains HAD
to work in isolation; they simply weren't aware that there were other people
on the planet...)

Pete.

PS. I actually support the technique of explaining something to someone as a
means of analysing it for yourself. I've had two marriages... A cat or dog
make much better cardboard programmers...
```

<a id="t-508200e157"></a>
## Thread: Pro*COBOL and (possible) NULL host variabe in WHERE condition — 2 msgs

### Pro*COBOL and (possible) NULL host variabe in WHERE condition

- **From:** "Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T07:56:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.databases.oracle
- **Message-ID:** `<1109865419.462518.13810@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
I have tableA, defined as:

field1 varchar2(10),
field2 varchar2(10),
field3 varchar2(10)

I have host variables defined as:

v1 pic x(10) varying
v2 pic x(10) varying
v3 pic x(10) varying

If insert the following record:

v1-len=5 v1-arr=Hello
v2-len=0 v2-arr=<spaces>
v3-len=5 v3-arr=World

I end up with this in the database:

field1=Hello
field2=<null>
field3=World

However, if I set the host variables exactly the same and try this:

select
  'I found it'
from
  tableA
where
  field1 = :v1
and
  field2 = :v2
and
  field3 = :v3

I am getting a 1403 SQLCODE returned.

I know that I cannot use indicator variables in WHERE conditions to
search for NULLs, so what is the fix? I can't create a separate WHERE
condition for each "NULL possibility" - in this small example alone I
woul dneed 8 different possible SELECT statements.

I know it must be something fairly obvious, but its not jumping out at
me right now and my eyes hurt from trying to wade through the Oracle
docs.

As always, any thoughts/suggestion/solutions are most appreciated.


Thanks,
Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: Pro*COBOL and (possible) NULL host variabe in WHERE condition

- **From:** "Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-03T08:22:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.databases.oracle
- **Message-ID:** `<1109866926.730294.310650@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1109865419.462518.13810@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1109865419.462518.13810@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Chris wrote:
> I have tableA, defined as:
>
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> I know it must be something fairly obvious, but its not jumping out
at
> me right now and my eyes hurt from trying to wade through the Oracle
> docs.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris


I knew that I would find something as soon as I posted this message.

One solution (as documented in Oracle's 9i documentation for Pro*COBOL)
is a combination of host indicator variable and NULL condition
checking. For example:

isnull pic s9(4) comp  value -1.


select
  'I found it'
from
  tableA
where
  ( field1 = :v1 or ( field1 is null and :v1:isnull is null ) )
and
  ( field2 = :v2 or ( field2 is null and :v2:isnull is null ) )
and
  ( field3 = :v3 or ( field3 is null and :v3:isnull is null ) )

This solution does in fact work, but it seems rather clumsy to me, and
honestly I'm concerned with optimizing the performance on this query.
Especially if my only existing index is: field1, field2, field3 (and
there are a few million records in the table).

Does anyone know of a better/cleaner/more efficient way to do this? I
imagine there has to be one - and I'd be extremely grateful to see one.
```

- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <2Q3Wd.572846$6l.290675@pd7tw2no> <1110196318.7b0d85a7b1b6328970916feac935e5cb@teranews>`

```
In article <1110196318.7b0d85a7b1b6328970916feac935e5cb@teranews>,
Peter E.C Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>Free advice costs nothing and is probably worth
>the price...

Gotta love this web-thingie.  From:

http://www.searchlyrics.org/allan_sherman/good_advice.html :

--begin quoted text:

I know a man named Otis who invented a room,
And his heart was filled with pride.
I said to Mr. Otis, "What does your room do?"
He said, "It goes from side to side."
So I said, "Mr. Otis, if you take my advice,
You'll be the richest man in town.
You gotta take that room that goes from side to side,
And make it go up and down."
And that was good advice, good advice.
Good advice costs nothing, and it's worth the price.
I sincerely doubt
That the world could do without
My good advice.

--end quoted text

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T11:43:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ierj21d0u76pjob9u1tsv9m68b3jnu0mjt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <232i21tikijjtumct542lb10ailsurl5i0@4ax.com> <d0cdtu$2bj$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 5 Mar 2005 08:58:22 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>*Do* try to keep up, Mr Wagner... just recently, in a discussion of terms 
>used in American and Britspeak, I mentioned how trade-names in the USA 
>became generic terms and that Johnson & Johnson's product...

Trade names that have become generic:

Allen wrench (capitalized because named after company), aspirin,
cellophane, celluloid, dry ice, escalator, gramophone, granola, gunk,
heroin, jungle gym (from Junglegym), kerosene (never was trademarked),
launderette, leatherette, linoleum, magnum (gun, cartridge),
mah-jongg, milk of magnesia, mimeograph, nylon (never was
trademarked), phone bank, photostat, pogo stick, saran, simonize (not
Simoniz), spandex, tabloid, tarmac, thermos, Tollhouse cookie,
touch-tone, windows (in dispute), yellow pages (trademarked in UK),
yo-yo, zipper

Still trademarked, should not be used as generic:

Adrenalin (the generic words are adrenaline and epinephrine),
AstroTurf, Autoharp, BVDs, Baggies, Bake Off, Bakelite, Beer Nuts,
Benzedrine, Boogie Board, Breathalyzer, Brillo Pads, Caplet,
Carborundum, Chap Stick, Chiclets, Cinerama, Day-Glo, Deepfreeze,
Demerol, Dianetics, Dictaphone, Dictograph, Ditto machine, Dixie cups,
Dolby, Dow Jones Average, Dumpster, Dvorak Keyboard, Erector Set,
Eskimo Pie, Ethernet, Exercycle, Fiberglass, Fig Newtons, Formica,
Freon, Frigidaire, GI Joe, Green Stamp, Hide-a-Bed, Hi-Liter,
Hula-Hoop, Jacuzzi, Jarlsberg, Jell-O, Jockey Shorts, Kewpie (doll),
Kitty Litter, Ko-Rec-Type, Jaws of Life, Laundromat, Levi's,
Liederkranz, Life Savers (candy), Linux (unregistered), Liquid Paper,
Lucite, Mace, Mack (truck), Magic Marker, Mailgram, Mary Janes
(sprinkles, shoes), Masonite, Metroliner, Mickey Mouse, Minicam,
Muzak, Novocain, NutraSweet, Orlon, Ouija, Pan-Cake (makeup),
Parcheesi (the generic word is pachisi), Peg-Board, Photostat,
Picturephone, Ping-Pong, Playbill, Play-Doh, Plexiglas, Polaroid, Pop
Tarts, Popsicle, Post-it Note, Pyrex, Q-Tip, Realtor, Rollerblade,
Roller Derby, Roquefort (salad dressing), SAT, Sanforized, Sanka,
Scientology, Scotch Tape, Scrabble, Seeing Eye (dog), Sheetrock,
Skivvies, Slim Jim, Styrofoam, Superglue, Technicolor, Teflon,
TelePrompTer, Teletype, Thermos, TV Dinner, UNIX (by The Open Group),
Valium, Vaseline, Velcro, Victrola, Walkman, Wedgwood, Welcome Wagon,
Wiffle Ball, X-Acto, Xerox, Zamboni
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T00:31:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rbsWd.580870$6l.168288@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<q92k21hkni0dta416shtss5ats5ooc1kuo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <232i21tikijjtumct542lb10ailsurl5i0@4ax.com> <d0cdtu$2bj$1@panix5.panix.com> <ierj21d0u76pjob9u1tsv9m68b3jnu0mjt@4ax.com> <4229FF90.18EBE70A@mb.sympatico.ca> <q92k21hkni0dta416shtss5ats5ooc1kuo@4ax.com>`

```
Jeff York wrote:
> Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Yes, considering my known prowess when it comes to things auto, I too 
recall "Gunk". I had a can in my hands once. Perhaps I used it on the 
moped I had or again - remember 'em Jeff - Corgis. Those mini fold-up 
motor bicycles that were originally dropped with paratroopers in WW2 ? 
When using latter somebody commented I looked like an elephant sitting 
on a pea ! I aint completely dumb; I never had thought to trying to 
drink the Gunk.

While Gunk was effective, yours truly found a much more effective 
solution. Can't recall, but from the following one of you 'auto techies' 
will latch on. Hydro..., hydra... something or other comes in powder or 
crystal form. Excellent for getting rid of grease.

My then girl-friend/fiancee, we are in the kitchen giving it a try. No 
sweat the future Father-in-law is glued to the idiot box, his Daily 
Mirror by his side, his packet of Woodbines and the occasional suck on a 
Polo (mints). Future Mum-in-Law crippled with arthritis - so she is no 
danger either.

Step # 1 - remove exhaust/baffle unit from said machine.
Step # 2 - Now put a pail of water on gas stove and bring to a boil
Step # 3 - Pour crystals into baffle
Step # 4 - now insert baffle into hot water
Step # 5 - Propulsion ! Hey kiddo, NASA aint got nuthin' on me

The friggin thing spewed everywhere - we were lucky it didn't hit us, 
but it covered the gloss painted walls of the kitchen with tell-tale marks.

Oh my gawd. We feverishly went into clean-up mode washing down the walls 
- and it was very effective, I must say.

Curious, not having seen us for a while, future Mom comes into the 
kitchen with her canes. Eileen glibly explains, "We got a few marks on 
the wall, so we thought we would clean them down completely".

Mom's reaction. "Oooh yes. Doesn't the kitchen look nice and clean".

JJG
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T03:19:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<attk2111vvvhopa8afehs6o30sdorq7fi0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <232i21tikijjtumct542lb10ailsurl5i0@4ax.com> <d0cdtu$2bj$1@panix5.panix.com> <ierj21d0u76pjob9u1tsv9m68b3jnu0mjt@4ax.com> <4229FF90.18EBE70A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
On Sat, 05 Mar 2005 12:50:56 -0600, Peter Lacey
<lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>What was "gunk" to begin with?

Gunk was trademarked in 1932 by A.F. Curran Co. of Malden, MA. It was
a degreasing agent used by auto mechanics, primarily to clean their
hands. Today, the word is applied to many automotive chemicals made by
Radiator Specialty, of Charlotte, NC, without trademark protection.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Steve.T" <steve_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T17:50:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110073816.690792.169510@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ierj21d0u76pjob9u1tsv9m68b3jnu0mjt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <232i21tikijjtumct542lb10ailsurl5i0@4ax.com> <d0cdtu$2bj$1@panix5.panix.com> <ierj21d0u76pjob9u1tsv9m68b3jnu0mjt@4ax.com>`

```
windows (in dispute)

Uh, only from Micro$**t's view point. However, the judge in the Lindows
case had let Billy Boy know that you can't perfect a generic name into
a trademark.

Case settled out of court, with Lindows Corp changing name to Linspire
and getting a chunk of change (full terms undisclosed).

So since the case was settled out of court, there has been no ruling
and the PTO, as I understand it, still says that this is a generic
name, eventhough Micro$**T still claims it is trade marked.

I guess that about does it.

Later,
Steve.T
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-07T12:13:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0ickh$1ml9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <232i21tikijjtumct542lb10ailsurl5i0@4ax.com> <d0cdtu$2bj$1@panix5.panix.com> <ierj21d0u76pjob9u1tsv9m68b3jnu0mjt@4ax.com> <d0i1mg$6rn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:d0i1mg$6rn$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  5-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's interesting.   I've never heard anybody call chocolate chip cookies
that
> - except for the company selling chocolate chips.   So I figured they
called it
> that in order to keep "chocolate chips" from becoming generic.

A quick internet search revealed this, from
http://www.ideafinder.com/history/inventions/story047.htm :

Back in 1930, Kenneth and Ruth Wakefield purchased a Cape Cod-style toll
house located halfway between Boston and New Bedford, on the outskirts of
Whitman, Massachusetts. Originally constructed in 1709, the house served as
a haven for road-weary travelers..

Here, passengers paid toll, changed horses, and ate much-welcomed
home-cooked meals. It was also here, over 200 years later, that the
Wakefield decided to open a lodge, calling it the Toll House Inn. In keeping
with the tradition of creating delicious homemade meals, Ruth baked for
guests who stayed at the Toll House Inn.

As she improved upon traditional Colonial recipes, Ruth's incredible
desserts began attracting people from all over New England. One day, while
preparing a batch of Butter Drop Do cookies, a favorite recipe dating back
to Colonial days, Ruth cut a bar of our Nestl� Semi-Sweet Chocolate into
tiny bits and added them to her dough, expecting them to melt. Instead, the
chocolate held its shape and softened to a delicately creamy texture. The
resulting creation became very popular at the Inn. Soon, Ruth's recipe was
published in a Boston newspaper, as well as other papers in the New England
area. Regional sales of Nestl� Semi-Sweet Chocolate Bar skyrocketed.

Ruth eventually approached Nestl� and together, they reached an agreement
that allowed Nestle to print what would become the Toll House Cookie recipe
on the wrapper of the Semi-Sweet Chocolate Bar. Part of this agreement
included supplying Ruth with all of the chocolate she could use to make her
delicious cookies for the rest of her life.

As the popularity of the Toll House cookie continued to grow Nestle looked
for ways to make it easier for people to bake. Soon, they began scoring the
Semi-Sweet Chocolate Bar, and packaged it with a special chopper for easily
cutting it into small morsels. Shortly after, in 1939, they began offering
tiny pieces of chocolate in convenient, ready-to-use packages and that is
how the first Nestl� Toll House Real Semi-Sweet Chocolate Morsels were
introduced.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> I just HAD to ask, ...
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


I am looking for some COBOL related quotes. Anything that suggests that 
non-programmers could use and/or read COBOL programs. I think there was 
a lot of this in the early days.

You see these claims all the time for new tools. Sometimes they are 
true, sometimes not - because there is more to programming than 
understanding syntax, it is a whole engineering discpline.

Tim Josling
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T05:56:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CSbWd.574310$8l.217822@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d0b9h9$o7d$1@possum.melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <d0b9h9$o7d$1@possum.melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote:
> I am looking for some COBOL related quotes. Anything that suggests that 
> non-programmers could use and/or read COBOL programs. I think there was 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Hello Tim,

Obviously you've been lurking for sometime - but nice to hear from you 
again.

I might apply the term 'engineering discipline' to what you are doing - 
producing a compiler - because you definitely need to do bit twiddling etc.

Engineering discipline s it applies to real engineers. My experience 
both in the oil patch and what I hear from time to time, is that they 
aren't disciplined, although by the very nature of their jobs, branches 
of engineering do have discipline built in. I hear time and again where 
they conceptualize something, but not in the finite detail required.

Example 1 - engineer draws up something in broad outline with noted
concepts and shoves it over to a draughtsman. The damn thing shuffles 
backwards and forwards between the two, the engineer complaining 'That's 
not what I said I wanted", and the draughstman cursing internally, "Well 
it would have been if you had friggin well spelled out exactly what it 
was you wanted".

Example 2 - Same as above. An MSc programmer has an intermediary systems 
analyst, who tos and fros between him and the engineer. Similar sort of 
thing - it go backwards and forwards for same reasons. Not dreamed up - 
the Chinese programmer spent six months on this abortive effort, got 
absolutely sick of it, chucked it in and got a new job.

While yes - you can probably get the quotes you want from a 
mainframe/procedural environment - but when you move to the Web, OO-ing 
or GUI-ing, although the results are effective and work it would be 
tough trying to illustrate for non-programmers.

See if you understand what I'm talking about - Having produced a 
Treeview I want to set an icon Red = Unactioned, Green = actioned. No 
big deal, use logic to swap the icons. Here's the problem within my 
TreeviewControl and TreeViewItem classes I can initially set an array of 
the icons I'm using and for each node in the Treeview attach the 
appropriate coloured icon - well almost. There are no methods to check 
what the current icon actually is attached to a specific Treeview node. 
The way around that -  have to sub-class both TreeviewControl and 
TreeviewItem with my own sub classes MyTreeviewControl inherits from 
TreeView and MyTreeviewItem inherits from TreeViewItem.

You happy with that ? Now go and explain it to a rookie. Not even going 
to help if I provide the code - it's absolutely meaningless without the 
background. That sort of thing applies to Webbing too - regardless of 
tools - although somebody from one of those smaller islands below you 
will probably disagree.

Having said that - How's your COBOL going down in Oz. Seemed to me you 
were the one closest to producing an Open Source  compiler. Are you 
still at it, have you given up, or do you have a target date. Out of 
curiosity - fill us in with a brief background. I'm assuming you 
probably haven't even tackled OO as yet. (Did you solve your BIG MONSTER 
- all those RESERVED WORDS ?)

Nice to hear from you again.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej_at_melbpc.org.au_rubbish@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T12:51:04+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0doa5$rvp$1@possum.melbpc.org.au>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<CSbWd.574310$8l.217822@pd7tw1no>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <d0b9h9$o7d$1@possum.melbpc.org.au> <CSbWd.574310$8l.217822@pd7tw1no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> ... See if you understand what I'm talking about - Having produced a 
> Treeview I want to set an icon Red = Unactioned, Green = actioned. No 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Thanks James,

What you have above is exactly what I was talking about! And here is a 
quote about Grace Hopper: "Admiral Hopper believed that the major 
obstacle to computers in non-scientific and business applications was 
the dearth of programmers for these far from user-friendly new machines. 
The key to opening up new worlds to computing, she knew, was the 
development and refinement of programming languages - languages that 
could be understood and used by people who were neither mathematicians 
nor computer experts. It took several years for her to demonstrate that 
this idea was feasible."

I have an answer for the reserved words, I can turn them on and off 
based on the options selected and where they are context dependent there 
are ways to deal with that - mainly intrinsics which don't create any 
big problem.

I am going to be a lot more discplined when I start up again about being 
focussed on the specific deliverable which is COBOL 85. COBOL 2002 is 
later, including OO.

Unfortunately I have inherited a set of problems from my father who died 
last year. I am hoping to have most of it sorted out within a couple of 
months and another $10,000 of legal costs. Nothing much else has been 
happening in the meantime unfortunately, so I have marked the 
sourceforge project "in limbo" for now. I am hoping this year will be a 
better year with some more time to devote to the cause.

Regards,
Tim Josling
```

```
I suppose that if compared to assembler, COBOL gave some semblence of
being "plain english", but in reality expecting a non-programmer to
use a programming language is as unreasonable as expecting a
non-mechanic to fix your car.

>You see these claims all the time for new tools. Sometimes they are 
>true, sometimes not - because there is more to programming than 
>understanding syntax, it is a whole engineering discpline.

I think that it's one of the major things that non-programmers fail to
understand..  Syntax is a piece of cake..  It's understanding what to
do with it that takes the skill.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T09:45:08-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0cglk$576$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <d0b9h9$o7d$1@possum.melbpc.org.au> <t83j21h1siencbciob8k5kkhbokmdpe1ae@4ax.com>`

```
In article <t83j21h1siencbciob8k5kkhbokmdpe1ae@4ax.com>,
Jeff York  <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I suppose that if compared to assembler, COBOL gave some semblence of
>being "plain english", but in reality expecting a non-programmer to
>use a programming language is as unreasonable as expecting a
>non-mechanic to fix your car.

I'm not sure about what constitutes 'reality', 'use a programming 
language', 'non-mechanic' and 'fix(ing) your car' in the above... but just 
the other day I was in a meeting - can't dodge 'em all, I guess - and 
folks appeared to be having a bit of difficulty expressing the conditions 
for a particular set of actions... and what actions should result from 
these conditions.  My job is to write code - well, part of my job, anyhow 
- and there was one other person in that meeting (my tech supervisor) 
whose job it is to write code; everyone else might be labelled 'a 
non-programmer'.  I stood up, Frowned A Mighty Frown, said to the woman 
who called the discussion 'I'm confused... might I use the whiteboard for 
a moment?' and, after receiving permission, scrawled the following:

IF SPECIAL-CASE-EMPLOYEE
    IF CREDIT-HRS-EARNED-OR-USED
        IF COMPRESSED-WORK-SCHEDULE
            IF NOT (COURT-LEAVE AND SICKNESS-LEAVE)
                PERFORM THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS.

Yes, I wrote it in all caps *and* without scope-delimiters *and* with a 
big, honking period/full stop right there... but I'll plead the necessity 
of limited resources, there was a lot of other stuff scrawled and I didn't 
have much space.  I then looked at the group and said 'Is this what we're 
trying to do?'... and brows furrowed for a moment and then there was 
overall agreement.  I said 'Right... now I'm still unsure... what 
constitutes a SPECIAL-CASE?  OK, that's anyone who works for Division X 
*or* is a left-handed Ethiopian smoke-shifter, job code 73421... OK, and 
we know that CREDIT-HRS are EARNED-OR-USED because we've received, 
somewhere in the batch, a record with a TYPE-CODE of 681 or 599... '

I think you get the idea.  Granted, the group's efforts were facilitated 
by my stumbling actions and limited talents but I would say that this 
constituted an example of 'a non-programmer using a programming 
language'... and, quite frankly, a manifestation of what COBOL was 
designed for back when Mr Simmons was still thin, attractive, 
clear-thinking and...

... oh... hi, Mr Simmons, didn't see you there.  Still thinking lewd 
thoughts at Sunday School?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-07T04:39:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eWQWd.130924$qB6.70615@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <d0b9h9$o7d$1@possum.melbpc.org.au> <t83j21h1siencbciob8k5kkhbokmdpe1ae@4ax.com> <d0cglk$576$1@panix5.panix.com> <26sj21hnsku6uacmt7vg8o043p408c3scg@4ax.com> <lvrWd.147997$JF2.89600@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <qs6l21lppfnhdpf42jue5h7kc4fuqg0ol5@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:qs6l21lppfnhdpf42jue5h7kc4fuqg0ol5@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 05 Mar 2005 23:44:17 GMT, "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> IF SPECIAL-CASE-EMPLOYEE

That is the first condition in a series of conditions.  As you don't 
actually "know" what that means, you cannnot apply any latter decisions 
based on that.  Anything like that is merely speculative on your part.  It 
may be a condition based on whether a DEPARTMENT has a special case 
employee.  Though, you're probably right.

>>B) Where do you see that they are are concurrent ?
>
> AND

Not even close.   AND is a simple operation on two variables.  It is 
checking if two flags are set concurrently at a code or syntactic level - 
however, what those flags represent have no concurrent time implications at 
all.  If COURT-LEAVE  (had court leave this pay cycle) or SICKNESS-LEAVE 
(had sickness leave this pay cycle) - the AND doesn't imply real concurrency 
at all.

>>C) Where do you see that they are working on a singular item?
> Singular 'employee' means the logic is dealing with one employee at a
> time.

As you don't actually "know" what SPECIAL-CASE-EMPLOYEE means, you cannnot 
apply any latter decisions based on that.  Anything like that is merely 
speculative on your part.

Point is, this was a logic fragment (not even code) used on a whiteboard to 
explain a contextualized problem in a room full of people that (I'm 
assuming) knew something about that context.   I don't understand where you 
have garnered your complete understanding from.

Same as mentioned in the other thread - just because something is written in 
English like structure, doesn't mean it automatically makes sense for all 
english speakers.

JCE

JCE
```

```
>have garnered your complete understanding from.

When people do post complete code and details, it usually draws little
response. Readers don't want to spend hours understanding a posting;
they want the essence expressed in a few sentences. My few sentences
were about logic in general, not specific to a payroll system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-05T21:05:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0doh7$hq1$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <t83j21h1siencbciob8k5kkhbokmdpe1ae@4ax.com> <d0cglk$576$1@panix5.panix.com> <26sj21hnsku6uacmt7vg8o043p408c3scg@4ax.com>`

```
In article <26sj21hnsku6uacmt7vg8o043p408c3scg@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 5 Mar 2005 09:45:08 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>logic error. No employee will be on court leave AND sickness leave.

Mr Wagner, are we seeing the same thing here?  Given the code:

01  A-FIELD PIC X VALUE SPACES.
    88  BIRTHDAY-LEAVE  VALUE  'B'.
    88  COURT-LEAVE     VALUE  'C'.
    88  SICKNESS-LEAVE  VALUE  'S'.
...

MOVE 'B' TO A-FIELD.
IF NOT (COURT-LEAVE AND SICKNESS-LEAVE)
    PERFORM THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS.

... would THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS be performed or not?

(Perhaps Mr Wagner is just pointing out how managers have been right when 
they admonish coders for using negative conditionals 'because they confuse 
people'.)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T00:14:07-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n47l21p12s8fn5n8be3plfp0be8gktri20@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <t83j21h1siencbciob8k5kkhbokmdpe1ae@4ax.com> <d0cglk$576$1@panix5.panix.com> <26sj21hnsku6uacmt7vg8o043p408c3scg@4ax.com> <d0doh7$hq1$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 5 Mar 2005 21:05:27 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <26sj21hnsku6uacmt7vg8o043p408c3scg@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>... would THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS be performed or not?

They would. But
MOVE 'C' TO A-FIELD
IF NOT (COURT-LEAVE AND SICKNESS-LEAVE)
    PERFORM THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS.
would also perform the actions. In fact, ANY value in A-FIELD would
perform the actions.

>(Perhaps Mr Wagner is just pointing out how managers have been right when 
>they admonish coders for using negative conditionals 'because they confuse 
>people'.)

It isn't just negatives. People trying to use conversational English
skills to understand logic (rather than Boolean algebra) are confused
by all combinations of AND and OR.

The solution is not to simplify conditions. The same logic will be
evaluated ONE WAY OR ANOTHER. The solution is to educate
'professionals' to deal with logic with mathematical rather than
conversational skills.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T08:32:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0f0q3$7ou$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <26sj21hnsku6uacmt7vg8o043p408c3scg@4ax.com> <d0doh7$hq1$1@panix5.panix.com> <n47l21p12s8fn5n8be3plfp0be8gktri20@4ax.com>`

```
In article <n47l21p12s8fn5n8be3plfp0be8gktri20@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On 5 Mar 2005 21:05:27 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>perform the actions.

*Very* good, Mr Wagner... now that the basics have been demonstrated it is 
on to the next!  Consider:

01 A-FIELD PIC X VALUE SPACES.
    88  BIRTHDAY-LEAVE  VALUE  'B'.
    88  COURT-LEAVE     VALUE  'C'.

01 B-FIELD VALUE SPACES.
    88 HOLIDAY-LEAVE   VALUE  'H'.
    88 SICKNESS-LEAVE  VALUE  'S'.

...

PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
 UNTIL SUB1 > 15
    EVALUATE LABOR-CODE (SUB1)
        WHEN 002
            SET BIRTHDAY-LEAVE  TO  TRUE
        WHEN 003
            SET COURT-LEAVE     TO  TRUE
        WHEN 005
            SET HOLIDAY-LEAVE   TO  TRUE
        WHEN 007
            SET SICKNESS-LEAVE  TO  TRUE

    END-EVALUATE
END-PERFORM.

IF NOT (COURT-LEAVE AND SICKNESS-LEAVE)
    PERFORM THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS.

Still with me here?  Given two week's worth of data, one LABOR-CODE per 
day - and in my experience, Mr Wagner, one labor code per day is an 
oversimplification of what pay systems evaluate - what would be necessary 
for THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS to be executed?

>
>>(Perhaps Mr Wagner is just pointing out how managers have been right when 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>by all combinations of AND and OR.

'People'?  Which 'people' are those, Mr Wagner... the two-year 
programmers?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T15:49:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<co7m215gsvguvkf8l5vk22dnc3s4qps34i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <26sj21hnsku6uacmt7vg8o043p408c3scg@4ax.com> <d0doh7$hq1$1@panix5.panix.com> <n47l21p12s8fn5n8be3plfp0be8gktri20@4ax.com> <d0f0q3$7ou$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 6 Mar 2005 08:32:51 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <n47l21p12s8fn5n8be3plfp0be8gktri20@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
>for THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS to be executed?

The intent is to perform THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS when either COURT or SICK
is absent. The logic is now broken even worse. THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS
will sometimes be performed when both are present, sometimes not. For
example, it will be performed when labor codes are in this order:

(1) 003  Court
(2) 002  Birthday
(3) 007  Sickness
(4) 005  Holiday

but will not be performed when the order is reversed. 

Do keep trying. For extra credit, write it in SQL with a single SELECT
on labor_code rather than two.
```

```
  does gives us A => NOT B, and B => NOT A, which, as he indicated,
```

```
In article <QCIWd.48970$Z14.37228@news.indigo.ie>,
Mic  <mic0@eircom.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <co7m215gsvguvkf8l5vk22dnc3s4qps34i@4ax.com>,
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
>conclude that they both belong to the same variable.

... or it seems reasonable that each is a different kind of leave, to be 
evaluated separately and together according to a set of arcane rules 
originating from a combination of the Personnel Department, the Union 
Representatives, the State Labor Department and the Federal Labor 
Department and that coding for each possibility separately allows for the 
greatest amount of manipulation across the various kinds of leave and the 
greatest amount of flexibility for the future changes which are *bound* to 
come down.

'Reasonable' might not allow for conclusions which show up so... 
reasonably, at times.

>Mr Wagner sure has an aggressive way of putting it, I can understand 
>somebody getting riled when he chat's about IQ, but the above assumption 
>  does gives us A => NOT B, and B => NOT A, which, as he indicated, 
>would mean the condition NOT (A AND B) will *always* be true!

Look at how it wound up above, with each condition independent of the 
other... if processing is to be different depending on whether an employee 
had one kind of leave, another kind or *both*... or not... during the 
pay-period then coding for the possibilities just got easier.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL anyone?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-06T14:16:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110147390.461003.151000@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d0ffb8$r7l$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<V13Wd.257539$Zm5.33514@news.easynews.com> <n47l21p12s8fn5n8be3plfp0be8gktri20@4ax.com> <d0f0q3$7ou$1@panix5.panix.com> <co7m215gsvguvkf8l5vk22dnc3s4qps34i@4ax.com> <d0ffb8$r7l$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <co7m215gsvguvkf8l5vk22dnc3s4qps34i@4ax.com>,
> Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>*Very* good, Mr Wagner... now that the basics have been
demonstrated
> it is
> >>on to the next!  Consider:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>Still with me here?  Given two week's worth of data, one LABOR-CODE
per
> >>day - and in my experience, Mr Wagner, one labor code per day is an

> >>oversimplification of what pay systems evaluate - what would be
necessary
> >>for THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS to be executed?
> >
> >The intent is to perform THIS-SET-OF-ACTIONS when either COURT or
SICK
> >is absent.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> How perceptive, Mr Wagner... are you sure you're not getting help on
the
> side for this?  To clean things up, then... what happens when - after

> weeks of errors showing up that nobody can seem to predict - the
> WORKING-STORAGE gets changed to:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

My preference is to use condition-names that 'obviously' 'belong' to
their parent data-names, e.g.

01  leave-fields.
    05  leave-birthday-fld  pic x value space.
         88  leave-birthday value 'B'.
    05  leave-court-fld    pic x value space.
         88  leave-court         value 'C'.
    05  leave-holiday-fld  pic x value space.
         88  leave-holiday       value 'H'.
    05  leave-sick-fld     pic x value space.
         88  leave-sick          values 'S'.

Then it is easier to see what is being done when the values of the
fields are being set.  Having said that, there is a new way in COBOL
2002 to set condition-names to false using the "WHEN SET TO FALSE"
phrase of the VALUE clause and the format 4 SET statement.

Robert
```
