- **Message-ID:** `<d1chpl$egn$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d1annd$al0$1@panix5.panix.com> <d1c64u$r2m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <41j_d.153818$qB6.45239@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
In article <41j_d.153818$qB6.45239@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>,
jce <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I missed the note re: Professional S series suffering through 
>legistlation.....I always wondered where they went when I wanted a new one. 

As I understand it the EU regs now demand that knives used in professional 
kitchens are not allowed to have handles constructed in a manner which 
could possibly or imagineably allow food particles to hide between the 
handle and the blade; this resulted in the Professional S series being 
'supplanted' by the 5-Star series.  The Professional S series is still 
available, just not recommended for... Professional use.

[snip]

>My favourite knife has nothing to do with brand - though I have the 
>Henckels - .any sharp cleaver  (not meat) rules :-)

Furi sells one of those 
(http://www.furitechnics.com/Furipro/Knives/ProChopperLarge.htm) 
(damnation... I ought to contact them about referral fees) and I've found 
it to be - like their other products - a delight to use.  For daily use I 
prefer the East-West knife (can't remember the length)... funny thing was 
I received it as a gift, looked at it and thought 'durned newfangled 
gizmos'...

... and tossed it into the Miscellaneous Kitchen Stuff drawer for nigh a 
year before using it.  After a few weeks in my hand I began scouring the 
web for good deals and got a three-piece set (a 'cook's knife', a paring 
knife and a utility knife) in a box for a reasonable amount... can't 
remember the numbers, no... and the sharpening-stuff which will, with a 
bit of diligence, put a decent edge on a car's bumper.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: cooking pots

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-18T03:48:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fujk319kcefe1q2gc6mcp77546qfuvjqpd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <iv8f319lao1f9ltufgcph9aujblral45dn@4ax.com> <d19gde$mnh$1@panix5.panix.com> <budh31hc268d8gs4dou011tek6at46j2v6@4ax.com> <d1annd$al0$1@panix5.panix.com> <d1c64u$r2m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <41j_d.153818$qB6.45239@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
On Thu, 17 Mar 2005 17:22:08 GMT, "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
wrote:

>I'm a big fan of going out.  That said, I'm a big fan of my rather cheap and 
>large nonstick saute pan......I tried to duplicate this with a cheap target 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>if you didn't scrub it, the eggs would work.

The best I had cost $5 on the clearance table at a Revere outlet store
in St. Augustine. It may have came from the same factory. Those
Chinese waifs are cunning with nonstick. It put a beautiful crust on
hash browns, like the Swiss Rosti, and let me execute perfect
one-handed flips dozens of times. Sadly, it expired due to scratches.
I got more than $5 use out of it.

>I like the feel of cast iron also....I would like to have a kitchen stuffed 
>with Le Creuset but my cupboards cannot support the weight...

And its porcelain cannot withstand the heat of frying. It cracks and
turns brown. The cleaning solution sold by Le Creuset works great for
reversing the damage.  Enameled cast iron is no good frying.

>Of course, the thing that you all forget to mention is the type of stove you 
>all have.....seems to me that some of your choices would work fantastico on 
>Gas but not on your standard electric hob. 

I moved into a house having gas for heat, but the kitchen was wired
for an electric stove. Rather than cook on electric, I went six months
with NO stove. After doing major surgery on the sheetrock to supply
gas to the kitchen, I happily started cooking on a gas stove. 

>To bring the thread back to COBOL by using the analogy common to CLC, it's 
>the availability of the correct tools at the appropriate time.  You don't 
>always need a browning pan.

Nor do you need a stove to cook. Countertop electric appliances, with
their thermostatic control, can do the job better than a stove. Rice
cookers are a good example of their superiority.

>I missed the note re: Professional S series suffering through 
>legistlation.....I always wondered where they went when I wanted a new one. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>em overnight.

After my ex dishwashered my Wusthofs, I called their US sales rep for
replacement of the handles or knives.  His best offer was a new set
'at cost' i.e. 50% of list. What about the lifetime warranty? The
warranty doesn't cover abuse.

I dumped the abuser, got a ceramic knife and lived happily ever after.
```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:fujk319kcefe1q2gc6mcp77546qfuvjqpd@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 17 Mar 2005 17:22:08 GMT, "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> warranty doesn't cover abuse.

Actually it is covered by whatever the person you are talking to covers it 
for.  I've found that a modicum of friendliness goes a long way.  I do know 
for a fact that one Chef took his knife back because it bent when he dropped 
it.   In the interest of customer satisfaction they let it go.  You just 
need to convince them that 'other than this incident' you love their 
products and would want to buy again, but that this disappoints you and 
you've read online it's quite common not like 'xxxx'.  As I've said before, 
I tried this with my car and VW gave me an $800 rebate on work done......and 
yes, I got rid of the one VW immediately afterwards.....but I kept my other 
one and I love it to pieces...This $800 dollars means that I continue to 
recommend people towards my car even today - I've probably made them their 
money back by now......

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cooking pots

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-19T01:36:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j6nm31huejg49vlc76s82ra7q3203c7e5p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <iv8f319lao1f9ltufgcph9aujblral45dn@4ax.com> <d19gde$mnh$1@panix5.panix.com> <budh31hc268d8gs4dou011tek6at46j2v6@4ax.com> <d1annd$al0$1@panix5.panix.com> <d1c64u$r2m$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <41j_d.153818$qB6.45239@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <fujk319kcefe1q2gc6mcp77546qfuvjqpd@4ax.com> <8OB_d.198718$JF2.8747@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
On Fri, 18 Mar 2005 14:43:16 GMT, "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
wrote:

>Actually it is covered by whatever the person you are talking to covers it 
>for.  I've found that a modicum of friendliness goes a long way.  I do know 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>money back by now......

Automobile companies are infamous for discontinuing models and whole
product lines that people love. I've loved: MG 1100, BMW 4-cylinder,
Corvair v2, Oldsmobile Toronado (owned four), Ford Escort GT, Honda
CRX, Mazda MX6 and small Saturn. They're all gone now, not from lack
of demand but lack of supply. BMW's retro Mini (Cooper) has been very
successful. So has the retro VW beetle, now a disguised Golf. 

Car makers are more interested in some executive's vision than in
customer opinion. Saturn began as an exception, giving people what
they really wanted. After five years, they devolved to being like the
others.

My next car will probably be Korean .. because they seem to care the
most about value and customers.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> On Tue, 15 Mar 2005 13:07:23 -0600, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
[snip]
> 
> I hate to say it but el cheapo serrated knives are pretty darn good.
> You can't sharpen them. When they get dull, buy another set at WalMart
> for $10.

That is what my ever-savvy wife told me, and it makes sense.  I think a 
set of steak knives was $240, if memory serves - most folks won't go 
through 24 sets of steak knives in their lifetime.  (Well, folks like 
me, that don't get steak very often...  ;>  )

> Vector is a marketing scheme that exploits both reps and consumers.
> Its products are good, not the best, but way overpriced. 

I do wonder, though, how you get to the top in a company like that. 
There are people there, so they had to get there somehow.  My wife is 
not as sales-challenged as I - she sold Kirby vacuums when she was in 
college, and once sold 3 in one day!

>>I didn't do much better with Amway - although, buying stuff I needed 
>>anyway helped it not be a complete wash. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> unexceptional products? They should Get A Life. 

My mother-in-law is a Director with Mark Kay - as we speak, she's in 
Cadillac qualification.  I think the draw for that is not overpriced 
makeup, but the fact that they get them from a "consultant" who can tell 
them what's best for their skin.  If the consultant's upline is worth a 
grain of salt, they've trained them not just on how to sell, but what 
products/colors work with skin types and tones.  (My mother-in-law holds 
weekly classes with her consultants - not all of them are there every 
week, but the newer ones gobble the information up.)

I don't think it's just a companionship thing - but if it is, well, the 
free market is making that possible.  :)
```

On 14-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> What if you learn the true rate, work for a year, then sue for the
> difference .. retroactively? The pimp would be out thousands. If
> you're W2, as you must be for max per diem, the pimp would be in
> trouble with the Wage & Hour people. Pimps are averse to such
> unpleasantries.

What kind of contract do you have with your pimp?    I've had hourly rates and
salaries, but never any that were defined as a percentage of what the pimp gets.
   Same thing happened when I was a regular employee.   I never looked up how
much money my employer made and sued if it made more than I thought.
```

wrote:

>
>On 14-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>salaries, but never any that were defined as a percentage of what the pimp gets.

My contracts are the same. But contracts are invalid when based on
factual error, especially when the error is due to fraud. If the pimp
sent a forged invoice, as Doc described, he'd lose all credibility
with the court. The valid contract would then be the verbal one.

> factual error, especially when the error is due to fraud. If the pimp
> sent a forged invoice, as Doc described, he'd lose all credibility
> with the court. The valid contract would then be the verbal one.

Both contracts are verbal.
```

On 13-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> FWIW, the word 'decimated' doesn't mean totally destroyed, it means
> reduced by 10%. In Roman days, when a legion screwed up it was
> punished by going down the line and killing every tenth soldier.
> That's where 'decimated' comes from.

I've often wondered how the meaning of that word changed.   I'm surprised so
many dictionaries list the original meaning 1st, as it doesn't seem to me to be
its main meaning anymore.
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<le5c31598gemc9p8t45vclfta5gntjtqkl@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <ei4731hfc2rd0ihm5ogfck9dvsl468d8v6@4ax.com> <d10a7f$nbh$1@panix5.panix.com> <re38311i2cfggicc5qoa90r6opkbtt6gk0@4ax.com> <d11agl$cc6$1@panix5.panix.com> <Ng7Zd.20225$hU7.15467@newssvr33.news.prodigy.com> <ip1a31ta24ghj8oqfokmc9rkpctnu3tbur@4ax.com> <d14cqi$ptd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

On Mon, 14 Mar 2005 16:03:59 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On 13-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>line is between doing their job for their stockholders and being piggish?

Market forces -- supply and demand -- determine the balance between
fear and greed. Y2K destabilized the process by introducing
under-equipped suppliers from third-world countries, notably India and
Russia. 

Free Market advocates defend capitalism 'to the death' -- I used to be
an options and futures trader -- but sensibly back off when opponents
are unevenly matched. It's just no fun when one side is incompetent or
being exploited. 

The correct income for an Indian woman working out of her New Jersey
home  is 30%; for a Big Five contacting company such as Accenture, EDS
or Deloitte Touche, it's 50%. Big Five are the only ones that have
public stockholders.
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-16T01:18:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<70te3113h0lhh8netbucotj2r3e8l86e2p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <ei4731hfc2rd0ihm5ogfck9dvsl468d8v6@4ax.com> <d10a7f$nbh$1@panix5.panix.com> <re38311i2cfggicc5qoa90r6opkbtt6gk0@4ax.com> <d11agl$cc6$1@panix5.panix.com> <Ng7Zd.20225$hU7.15467@newssvr33.news.prodigy.com> <ip1a31ta24ghj8oqfokmc9rkpctnu3tbur@4ax.com> <d14cqi$ptd$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <le5c31598gemc9p8t45vclfta5gntjtqkl@4ax.com> <d16r67$cir$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Tue, 15 Mar 2005 14:21:24 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 14-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Market forces say whatever you and your employer agree to is the correct amount.

Only when there's sufficient liquidity i.e. enough buyers and sellers.
Free markets don't work in the absence of liquidity, and are wisely
shut down. For example, when there's a panic or crash.

Futures markets are shut down automatically when prices move "by the
limit"

>> Free Market advocates defend capitalism 'to the death' -- I used to be
>> an options and futures trader -- but sensibly back off when opponents
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>And you were incompetent, exploited, or exploiter?

Contracting companies have unsuccessfully attempted to exploit me.
I've worked with contractors who were being exploited, which was
obvious to me but not to them. I've worked with Indians who KNEW they
were being exploited and resented it. They eventually moved back to
India, with a low opinion of the US.

Free marketers such as floor traders laugh off 'morality'. They'll
fleece a neophyte trader without a second thought. But they have a
natural sense of fairness, or as they might put it 'inefficient
markets'. It's just not right to exploit people who don't understand
the language or culture. Computer pimps who do so are not champions of
free markets, they're predators. 

What's the difference? In pure terms, a free market is a zero-sum
game. For every winner, there's an equal and opposite loser. The
predator game is not zero-sum. The losses of a large population are
greater than the benefits to the predators. In the overall inequality,
society loses value.

>> The correct income for an Indian woman working out of her New Jersey
>> home  is 30%; for a Big Five contacting company such as Accenture, EDS
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>have meant that she was being piggish?

The woman WAS the pimp .. the one who piggishly wanted 71% gross
profit. The figures were derived by marketplace norms for markup and
operating expenses.
```

On 12-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> On the other hand, if you hate commuting traffic as much as I do, a
> contractor can usually live within walking distance of the office. An
> employee will typically spend one to two hours in traffic, each way.
> I've always gone home for a gourmet lunch. As I said, it's a lifestyle
> choice.

I've found the opposite.   A contractor has less choice about the relationship
between his home and his work than an employee.   I've been more willing to
contract to jobs on the other side of the city than I have been willing to hire
on permanently.   Although I did take on a contract that turned into my last job
- and then moved closer.
```

On Mon, 14 Mar 2005 15:54:55 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On 12-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>- and then moved closer.

I go to Yahoo Yellow Pages, Change Location to the office address, and
ask for apartments. It shows them sorted by distance. After being on
the job a week or two, I visit them and pick one. 

Caution:  'Apartment Finder' Web sites are a poor way to find an
apartment. They list the most exploitive and expensive ones. I have
nothing against expensive, the ones I pick usually are, but they're
not exploitive. 

In urban areas, the closest isn't always the best. Walking half a mile
through a classy downtown area can be better than across the street.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Architect position-Indianapolis

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-15T14:26:12+00:00

On 14-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> >I've found the opposite.   A contractor has less choice about the
> >relationship
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the job a week or two, I visit them and pick one.


I've been a contractor for years (I'm not now), and have worked with lots of
contractors.   But we all live at home and commuted more.   Commuting an extra
hour per day is not good for family life, but certainly beats renting an
apartment close to work, paying for two homes, and only seeing the family on
weekends.   The occasional remote weeks were bad enough.
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 14-Mar-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> weekends.   The occasional remote weeks were bad enough.

About 4-5 years back, I dropped my rate to 1/3, and decided to never 
take another job where I had to leave home.  If they outsource to India, 
then they can outsource to my high speed line as well.  I now get 
dressed and have breakfast during my morning coffee break, and spend 
zero hours/dollars on commuting/traveling to and from work. I wish the 
technology had been there 20 years ago.  I'd never go back, and end up 
with more money in my pocket.

Donald
```

On Tue, 15 Mar 2005 10:25:06 -0500, Donald Tees
<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>About 4-5 years back, I dropped my rate to 1/3, and decided to never 
>take another job where I had to leave home.  If they outsource to India, 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>with more money in my pocket.

When I worked at home, for two years, I became compulsive. I was
either working or sleeping. I ate while working. Would sometimes work
30 hours before crashing. When the clock said six o'clock, I had to
look out the window to see whether it was AM or PM. 

I was working on software (an operating system) that would have been
on magazine covers. I wouldn't have been so turned on by applications
programming.
```

- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <fsidnSO_n9hsqazfRVn-jg@rogers.com> <d0rqsc$d6p$1@panix5.panix.com> <JaSdnapvGrHoTazfRVn-uw@rogers.com> <d0slas$j7s$1@panix5.panix.com> <ei4731hfc2rd0ihm5ogfck9dvsl468d8v6@4ax.com> <d14c9h$pj3$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <gd8c31t717q8q92rcnca2uogbbquihr5ek@4ax.com> <d16rf7$cj3$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <p7DZd.24177$Jd2.589744@news20.bellglobal.com> <mi1f315basv6v3cqd6qmsjut5g24796pm6@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> On Tue, 15 Mar 2005 10:25:06 -0500, Donald Tees
> <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming.

I have a hunch that is more age related than home/office oriented.  I 
have owned a few companies where I was either very close or had a 
parallel home system, and did the same thing.  But I was twenty years 
younger.

I still spend an hour playing with computers for every hour of bill out, 
but now I play with stuff I like, and do it quite separately from work. 
  Bill out, I am a clock watcher ... but then I am charging junior 
rates, and getting all of it, $100%. I email a bill each Friday, and a 
cheque arrives in the mail one week from the following Tuesday.  Both my 
customer and I get a bargain ... he gets a solid programmer that can 
work alone, and I get to work 30 hours a week for enough to live 
comfortably on. I am not getting paid to worry about it after hours, and 
I do not.

With no travel time, and no wasted time, I can do that between 9 and 5, 
taking one day a week off at my leisure. Often, if the weather is nice, 
I'll take a couple of afternoons off and go to the park. I have not 
worked an evening or a weekend in two years, though in an emergency, I 
would.

I think it a simpler and more satisfying life, though I'll grant it's 
not for everyone.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Architect position-Indianapolis

- **From:** "Joe" <NoSpam@SpamSucks.Org>
- **Date:** 2005-03-10T22:36:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uKdnXC2b_X5vazfRVn_vQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
>A well-respected financial institution in Indianapolis has created a
> new opportunity for a Mainframe Architect to analyze their existing
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> technical architect who can .... etc. etc.

Rapid growth in volume and you want to move mission critical systems
to client server? Why? Because it's newer technology? Boy are you in
for problems. NO client server beats the I-O power of the mainframe.
If you want fast, efficient, reliable, stable, secure processing, you want
a z/OS server not some click and giggle unix or windows p.o.s..

I'm working on a large mainframe based DB2 system that interfaces
with an ORACLE unix system. We can completely restore the entire
DB2 system to a test environment in a few hours. It takes 3 full days
of intense effort by programmers and DBA's to restore the ORACLE
system with a quarter of the volume. But ooooo it's ORACLE, ooooo
it's client server, oooooo it's better, it's cool, management loves the
pretty pictures. It's slow as a turtle and takes forever to get anything done.

You're going to need more personnel, more equipment, more time,
more work and more money than if you had increased and upgraded
mainframe capacity. Good Luck. Every single "take it off the mainframe"
project I've ever observed had been a very expensive fiasco.

And BTW... JAVA, C++, webshere and all the "new" stuff runs
on the mainframe too you know. You can also serve web pages from CICS
you know. Don't build mission critical systems on toy computers using hot new
whiz bang technology that could turn out like 8-track tapes or the Betamax.
You'll be sorry if you do. Tell that to your well-respected financial institution
and I hope they don't shoot themselves in the head.
```

Number one should be eliminated as anomalous. It's tracking, what,

on 'Billy Boy's click and giggle operating system.'

Isn't REALITY wonderful?

>You're going to need more personnel, more equipment, more time,
>more work and more money than if you had increased and upgraded

winner with companies that want an expensive fiasco.

>on the mainframe too you know. You can also serve web pages from CICS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

I'm disappointed by the absence of the well-respected financial
institutions I worked for on the above lists. Every time someone goes
to Yahoo Financial and asks for details, he's hitting my database. The
server isn't a mainframe; it's a toy computer running Sybase or SQL
Server against trillions of rows on "some click and giggle unix or
windows p.o.s."

One would think financial services companies who are in the business
of selling data could afford computer experts like you to set them
straight. I'm referring to Reuters, Bloomberg, Thomson and
Morningstar. All four of them got it wrong. They're hosting their
databases on Unix and Windows platforms rather than throwing money at
IBM. You should write to their CIOs warning them of the risk and
proposing a mainframe solution. A good laugh is always welcome.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Architect position-Indianapolis

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T13:05:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f5n531d53pdv5bb4cfvju8v3052t5bbktp@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7uKdnXC2b_X5vazfRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <9ac4311k4khvcsb5jtebka9oqm16jv259b@4ax.com> <MvwYd.1033929$Zm5.155502@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sat, 12 Mar 2005 07:04:12 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Robetrt, (why am I suirprised),
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>relevant)

DSS stands for Decision Support System, commonly known as Data
Warehouse. For completeness, OLTP stands for On-Line Transaction
Processing. They share the same underlying database technology. The
only difference is access tools. DSS uses Data Mining tools such as Ab
Initio designed for high volume; OLTP uses application programs
issuing traditional SQL. 

I chose DSS over OLTP because the numbers are bigger. The point here
was reporting the biggest and most active.

>and of course, see:
>
>  http://www.wintercorp.com/VLDB/2005_TopTen_Survey/TopTenProgram.html
>
>for how databases get INTO this survey.

They're self-nominated. That would give the advantage to mainframes,
whose proponents like to say 'mine is bigger'. I picture them slipping
to the men's room with six-inch rulers. :)

>Would you care to change your opinion now????

Not until a mainframer reports bigger to Winter Corp. 

There are many big databases that aren't included in the Winter Corp.
report. For instance, Social Security has hundreds of millions of
account holders, each with a few hundred quarterly earnings reports.
Big deal. Ten billion rows wouldn't make the top ten on Winter.

The Winter reports reflect inefficient design more than productive use
of a database.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Architect position-Indianapolis

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T09:01:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0usnh$1ul$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <9ac4311k4khvcsb5jtebka9oqm16jv259b@4ax.com> <MvwYd.1033929$Zm5.155502@news.easynews.com> <f5n531d53pdv5bb4cfvju8v3052t5bbktp@4ax.com>`

```
In article <f5n531d53pdv5bb4cfvju8v3052t5bbktp@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>On Sat, 12 Mar 2005 07:04:12 GMT, "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>issuing traditional SQL. 

Not *quite*, Mr Wagner.  As I was taught rather recently (not 
e'er-so-long ago) the difference between DSS and OLTP designs have to do 
with the kind of queries that are run agains them and the kind of indices 
designed to support them.

DSS systems are to be designed for data which do not change much, 
typically daily/monthly batch loads.  They are to be designed with 
multiple keys for easy access and a decrease in the number of full table 
scans needed; multiple keys are easy to deal with because the overhead of 
key-update is not encountered during queries... which are, of course, 
read-only.

OLTP, on the other hand, deals more in individual records/rows, order 
entry being the classic example.  The overhead encountered during 
key/index insertion/update is *very* important because such updates take 
time and folks waiting for an online transaction do not like to wait.

>
>I chose DSS over OLTP because the numbers are bigger. The point here
>was reporting the biggest and most active.

You seem to have choses what supported your argument, Mr Wagner, and 
ignored the fact that a read-only database (DSS) will have - or 'should 
have' - an entirely different design and function than an OLTP one.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Architect position-Indianapolis

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T12:19:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5hb631lkqv7esgsctl843k0dc6dlm8kls5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <9ac4311k4khvcsb5jtebka9oqm16jv259b@4ax.com> <MvwYd.1033929$Zm5.155502@news.easynews.com> <f5n531d53pdv5bb4cfvju8v3052t5bbktp@4ax.com> <d0usnh$1ul$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 12 Mar 2005 09:01:21 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <f5n531d53pdv5bb4cfvju8v3052t5bbktp@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>time and folks waiting for an online transaction do not like to wait.

All true, and well said. But it doesn't change the fact that DSS
databases have the largest size and activity.

>>I chose DSS over OLTP because the numbers are bigger. The point here
>>was reporting the biggest and most active.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>have' - an entirely different design and function than an OLTP one.

One can alter access strategy without changing design. On Oracle, for
instance, tweaking the parameters optimizer_index_caching and
optimizer_index_cost_adj can convince it to favor or disfavor full
table scans. FWIW, out-of-the-box default values are far from optimal,
especially for OLTP,  yet many shops run with them.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CU2Zd.5036438$f47.913021@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7uKdnXC2b_X5vazfRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <9ac4311k4khvcsb5jtebka9oqm16jv259b@4ax.com> <MvwYd.1033929$Zm5.155502@news.easynews.com> <f5n531d53pdv5bb4cfvju8v3052t5bbktp@4ax.com>`

```
So now the question is ...

Let's go back to the original post in this thread,

"A well-respected financial institution in Indianapolis has created a
new opportunity for a Mainframe Architect to analyze their existing
Mainframe environment and make recommendations as to how to migrate to
newer technology (i.e: Client Server). Their mission critical systems
are experiencing rapid growth in processing volume, and need a highly
technical architect who can ensure that their system infrastructure
will continue to support increasing business demands. This position
requires hands-on experience designing and configuring large, complex
computer systems with high-performance, high-availability, and load
balancing properties."

Now the question is WHICH type of "system" DSS or OLTP does one think provides 
the best "model" for information related to this environment?

I don't think it is "obvious" - and if I were actually interested in the job (or 
making a recommendation to that site), then I would certainly want to find out 
HOW they are using their database today.

However, given the fact that they are a CICS/DB2 shop today, I would GUESS (and 
could easily be wrong) that the statistics from the quoted paper on OLTP 
environments would PROBABLY be a better guide than those for DSS.

Given this, then a decision to STAY on the (IBM) mainframe would likely keep 
them with the "largest" systems currently handling similar systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Architect position-Indianapolis

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T12:23:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110659002.202624.298730@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<MvwYd.1033929$Zm5.155502@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7uKdnXC2b_X5vazfRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <9ac4311k4khvcsb5jtebka9oqm16jv259b@4ax.com> <MvwYd.1033929$Zm5.155502@news.easynews.com>`

```
What the survey doesn't indicate is the number of machines and CPUs
involved.  You can get high numbers from any system if you throw enough
hardware at it.  Those SQL Server sites may be running a farm of dozens
or hundreds of machines, each doing low numbers of transactions per
second. The efficiency of the system then depnds on how well the load
is distributed.

This is certainly not proof that 'micros' are better than mainframes,
or are even cost effective, let alone have a better TCO.
```

> On 12-Mar-2005, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> one has blue trim and the other has Red trim.

Reminds me of the joke of Paddy and Murphy who inherited racehorses from 
their grandmother.  In order to prevent confusion, they put a red tag on one 
and a blue tag on the other.  For the next few months each dilligently took 
care of their horse, brushing, feeding and training without problem.  One 
day the horses in their frollicking managed to lose their tags.....after the 
initial shock wore off they came to a mutual agreement and Paddy said to 
Murphy, "Oil take de white one, you can 'av de black one".

I didn't claim it to be funny.  I guess at age 8 you haven't become 
politically correct yet......just wondering if you swapped the trim the Sun 
would be an IBM?

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframe Architect position-Indianapolis

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner)

- **Message-ID:** `<mQ9OCpHpvO8Q092yn@visi.com>`
- **References:** `<1110469607.661635.23860@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <7uKdnXC2b_X5vazfRVn_vQ@giganews.com> <9ac4311k4khvcsb5jtebka9oqm16jv259b@4ax.com> <MvwYd.1033929$Zm5.155502@news.easynews.com> <d14d56$q71$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <wWB_d.159374$qB6.118460@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <d1ervc$mmp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Here in comp.lang.cobol,
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> spake unto us, saying:

>What makes the IBM a mainframe, but not the Sun?

One still has the power to handle applications like high-end airline
reservation systems, and one does not.

Guess which is which?  :-)

>Probably just people not realizing that things have changed.

Sun boxes can now be logically partitioned and are getting better at
recovery and such, but I don't see them serious replacing either IBM
or Unisys boxes yet in the airline industry.

Part of the reason is tradition and part pure inertia, but those are
not the only reasons.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:9ac4311k4khvcsb5jtebka9oqm16jv259b@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 10 Mar 2005 22:36:49 -0600, "Joe" <NoSpam@SpamSucks.Org>
> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't see any mainframe there.

I also don't see any comments from owners, or employees of said companies 
with opinions of said products or relative performance.

I also don't know where your source is from (again) given that the list 
changes when you consider whether you are looking at DSS, OLTP...or 
Normalized Data Volume

Here _is_ a source for you with emphasis added

http://www.eweek.com/article2/0,1759,1410944,00.asp

"For OLTP systems database size spanning all OS environments, Land Registry 
came in first with 18.3TB. EMC dominated the board by having its storage 
products used by 7 of the 10 vendors on the list. ***IBM and the mainframe 
also dominated the category***, taking the first 5 spots and 8 of a possible 
ten. For OLTP Unix-based systems, in the second consecutive survey Oracle 
ran the board as the DBMS vendor used by each respondent. Lastly, in the 
Windows OLTP category HP servers were used by 7 of 10 organizations, and 
Microsoft SQL Server was the DBMS choice for seven respondents. That's a 
huge leap compared to the 2001 survey findings, when not enough respondents 
met the minimum requirements to meet the size category"

Also one should consider the infrastructure and role of said systems. I 
think that we're confusing mainframe and client-server with the realities of 
what is in place.  Last time I checked, Nielsen recently had job postings 
for _mainframe_ positions.  Only a portion of their business is Sybase on 
Solaris........as it's a data warehouse and oltp type application _OF 
COURSE_ it is client server......it just happens to be a freaking big 
server.

Client/Server versus mainframe is predominantely a network architecture 
statement.  An unix cluster as found in an SP may be at a "nuts and bolts" 
level client server-ish (MPP), but deep down its just another large logical 
machine.  Newer MPPs are a _share nothing_ architecture and are actually 
clusters of SMPs communicating over a shared bus - still one logical 
machine.

Maybe people are too near and dear to what actually is termed a "mainframe". 
Today, IBM sells mainframes as "large servers".

I don't care one way or the other what people think - but I wish people 
would get out of this mode where Mainframe = Big and Bad and Client Server = 
Small and Crap....and vice versa.

A small unix box will never match a large "mainframe" - *duh*
A large unix cluster will surpass a small "mainframe" - *duh*
A mainframe can be a client or a server - *duh*

I think this is in essence disagreeing fundamentally with RW and the top 
level posting dimwit.

This whole argument is again like the "COBOL sucks, Java is better" - 
doesn't mean _either_ of them is actually close to being right.  Often, Rexx 
is better :-)

>>If you want fast, efficient, reliable, stable, secure processing, you want
>>a z/OS server not some click and giggle unix or windows p.o.s..
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> on 'Billy Boy's click and giggle operating system.'
Why eliminate 1?  If it's tracking money wasting government projects, it's 
probably forever going to be number one...

> Isn't REALITY wonderful?
Debatable considering the least effective war the last 20 years has been the 
war on drugs.

>>You're going to need more personnel, more equipment, more time,
>>more work and more money than if you had increased and upgraded
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> winner with companies that want an expensive fiasco.
If it were my business to gambe, I'd have wager on the accuracy of your 
statement.

Given the following source - it's old 4 years, but I think you've probably 
undertaken at least as many of these jobs before this date as after:
http://www.accenture.com/xd/xd.asp?it=enweb&xd=ideas%5Coutlook%5C1.2000%5Cover_big.xml
 {begin
a.. Only 8 percent of large-scale applications projects (those that cost 
between $6 million and $10 million) succeed.
a.. Among all IT development projects, only 16 percent occur within 
acceptable constraints of cost, time and quality.
a.. Cost overruns of anywhere from 100 percent to 200 percent are common in 
software projects.
a.. The price tag for annual cost overruns for IT projects has been 
estimated at $59 billion in the United States alone. Another study puts the 
figure at $100 billion.
a.. IT workers spend more than 34 percent of their time just fixing software 
bugs.
end}

Assuming that "take it off the mainframe" projects are large scale (hence on 
a mainframe to begin with), you have successfully managed to avoid 92 out of 
every 100 projects. I then will suggest that it would not seem rational or 
feasible to suggest that your role on each of these projects was _so_ great 
that you could have _saved_ the other 92 % with your $55/hr expertise.

Perhaps your views - which are imho, not always absurd as others make out - 
would be better served with a little more humility and a little less 
exaggeration.

>>And BTW... JAVA, C++, webshere and all the "new" stuff runs
>>on the mainframe too you know. You can also serve web pages from CICS
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> to Yahoo Financial and asks for details, he's hitting my database. The
So you're the owner of Yahoo now....?

> server isn't a mainframe; it's a toy computer running Sybase or SQL
> Server against trillions of rows on "some click and giggle unix or
> windows p.o.s."
I've yet to see any toy computer with enough disk space for trillions of 
rows of anything.  I went to the toy store and the only toy computers I 
found were made by leapfrog and were used to educate children...maybe I went 
to the wrong section.

> One would think financial services companies who are in the business
> of selling data could afford computer experts like you to set them
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> proposing a mainframe solution. A good laugh is always welcome.
Errr....IBM was on your lists provided as evidence...lets see....25% of the 
time by my count....and that's _without_ offering a DBMS design purely for 
DSS which is your typical large DBMS and accounts for a large portion of 
your _evidence_.

JCE
```

## Thread: display an english text string in another language — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### display an english text string in another language

- **From:** "kathie" <kktbva@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-10T08:53:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110473582.509852.55280@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi all,

I'm very new with COBOL and working on displaying an english text
string in another language such as korean, japanese, farsi, etc.

This is what I have in my program for displaying in japanese:

.....
01  WS-TEST             PIC X(20)    VALUE 'MY NAME IS JOHN DOE'.

01  ws-rec.
    05  WS-TITLE        PIC N(20)    USAGE NATIONAL.
.....
MOVE FUNCTION NATIONAL-OF(WS-TEST, 1399) TO WS-TITLE.
....
WRITE PRINT-REC FROM WS-REC.

The program compiled OK but it abends when I run it.  This is the
error:

CEE3250C The system or user abend S019  R=00000000 was issued.

Am I doing something incorrect?  Please help!!!!

Thanks,
Kathie
```

#### ↳ Re: display an english text string in another language

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-10T23:26:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hI4Yd.873094$Zm5.124925@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<1110473582.509852.55280@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
My guess is that you have a problem with the Unicode Conversion services at your 
site.  If you aren't the systems programmer at your site, check with them and 
have them review the information at:

   http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3c120/4.1

See:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/iea2h750/2.18

for information on S019 Abend
```

## Thread: Should cobol be behind bars?... — 9 msgs

### Should cobol be behind bars?...

- **From:** "Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM>
- **Date:** 2005-03-11T08:35:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110558946.217050.53610@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello Everyone,

I am trying to program some business graphics and need your kind
help with some key points. I am plotting a comparison Bar graphs
with six quantitative values to represent on the axis scale, these
variable data that determines the range of values on the scale are
dynamically obtained, therefore, the span of these numbers could be
low, medium or jumbo high. So, I would like to know if there is
some golden rules, when it comes to charting graphics with regards
to the following criteria:

Visual Clarity & Perception:

1). Should the scale have a starting-point of zero?
2). Should the scale have an ending-point higher than the maximum
    value represented?
3). Should the scale interval equals the minimum value represented?

thanks a lot for your help. Kellie.
```

#### ↳ Re: Should cobol be behind bars?...

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-03-11T12:07:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d0sj8i$iqc$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<1110558946.217050.53610@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1110558946.217050.53610@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>,
Kellie Fitton <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote:
>Hello Everyone,
>
>I am trying to program some business graphics and need your kind
>help with some key points.

[snip]

>1). Should the scale have a starting-point of zero?
>2). Should the scale have an ending-point higher than the maximum
>    value represented?
>3). Should the scale interval equals the minimum value represented?

Ow... what you're asking here is a matter of data presentation and 
representation; people get advanced degrees in stuff like this.  The 
answer(s) to your questions are simple, though, and can be applied to all 
three questions you pose:

It depends on what kind of data you are presenting and the kind of 
audience to which you are presenting them.

Take your first question about a starting point of zero.  If you are 
graphing the distortions found in the roadbed of a large suspension-bridge 
under varying weights then a good answer might be 'no'... a large 
suspension-bridge's roadbed will show the more-or-less the same distortion 
for zero, ten or twenty passenger-cars.  Five hundred or eight hundred 
cars start to might start to induce stress but under a certain point 
charting 'minimal distortion' would be a waste of space.

If you are graphing changes in thread-alignment of a piece of string on 
which successively greater weights are hung... then yes, zero is the place 
to start; the difference a tenth of a gram makes here is significant.

Likewise, who will be reading the data?  A general-practise physician 
might be interested in variations across individual patients, differences 
between two or three folks might be considered significant.  An 
epidemiologist might consider any population increment less than five 
thousand to be insignificant.  One size does not fit all.

http://www.gis-objectland.com/support/doc/usermanual/MLOT_Diagrams.html 
seems to have some good examples of different ways to chart data.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Should cobol be behind bars?...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-11T17:55:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IYkYd.644599$8l.457412@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1110558946.217050.53610@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<1110558946.217050.53610@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Kellie Fitton wrote:
> Hello Everyone,
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
You go all directions at once don't you :-)

You wont like it - OO :-)

I'm sure there are specific M/F examples covering this feature, but your 
best bet, initially, is to look at a White Paper (on the M/F site) 
written by Wayne Rippin. He does a neat job of showing how to link COBOL 
to Microsoft Word or Excel.

Using OO COBOL method passing features you can make Excel do exactly 
what you want. As a backup feature, from the IDE look at the Type 
Library Assistant. You can extract a list of methods both passing and 
returning features for a large number of products. If you do an extract 
for both Word and Excel you will get two HUGE files/listings - from 
memory something like 23,000 lines each, covering all the methods 
applicable. I've never checked but it's a fair bet that a large 
proportion of the methods required for Word and Excel are identical, 
(e.g. Open, Close, Save etc.).

The point is, this is one where you don't re-invent the wheel. OK no 
doubt, you can probably do it using your beloved APIs - but I'm thinking 
the OO approach will be simpler.

It is not high priority to me - but I need some simple graphs - and 
that's the route I propose to take, some time in the future - using 
Excel. (Well of course - if you haven't got Excel on your machine 
......? Still that Type Library Assistant droplist *might* contain a 
graphing tool that you want or are familiar with).

PS: Looking at your subject title - perhaps you should be :-)

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Should cobol be behind bars?...

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2005-03-11T22:11:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-238D7D.22113311032005@ispnews.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<1110558946.217050.53610@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <1110558946.217050.53610@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>,
 "Kellie Fitton" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote:

> Hello Everyone,
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks a lot for your help. Kellie.

I will toss my humble $0.02 in.

   Only if it makes visual sense.  If you have six bars that are between 
700 and 900 then your scale should be something like 650 to 950.  
Otherwise it becomes hard to tell the difference between bars.

   Picking a scale slightly higher than the highest value and slightly 
lower than the lowest value gives maximum difference between the bars, 
As making the differences between things visually distinct is the entire 
purpose of graphs, this is a good idea.
```

### Parameters passed into Wed input form

- **From:** Phillip.Small@gmail.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1110577561.467475.290670@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```
I have been having trouble for quite a bit now.  The OS is Sun and
Microfocus is the product.  I am new to Cobol so bare with me as I try
to describe the issue.  I am working on a web report program(s).  My
file oits.cbl creates a oits.exe file in which I run.  When ran, it
calls, oidriver.pco, i39l.pco, and a couple of web (html) files.  The
parameters are the start and end dates, and it returns the input form
indicating that the start and end dates are not being passed into the
form to create the file or go to the next step in bring back the
report.  I am running this in Batch mode within Unix for testing.  The
HTML code for the input form returns when the program is executed
instead of the final product.  I have been through all programs and do
not see why the parameters are not being passed.  I went through all
Start date variables and columns.  The parameters inserted into the
database  directly, then called from i39l.pco program.  Has anyone
worked with web programs and multiple files interacting with each other
or has such a problem?  Any help would be greatly appreciated as I have
been working on this and am about to break something!!!  No, not
really, but please help if you can.  Thx
```

It doesn't bother me that the President fooled around with an intern 
*nearly* as much as his lying about it (even under oath).  The truth is, 
he *did* have sexual relations with her, and lied to the American people 
- not out of a lack of knowledge, but out of a sheer selfish motive. 
People mess up, people make mistakes - but, character is admitting the 
mistake, making amends, and learning from it.

> I commented to the lady, "What a disgrace etc.....". Her response, "Who

This is the man with his finger on the nuclear button!

> Then of course there's FDR, Ike, JFK - bugger me ! With the exception of

I missed the implication - are you talking about my rankings?

> Can you say with honesty, holding your Good Book, (jes like WJC would 
> show up at the Washington Cathedral holding his copy), "I've never been 
> curious or ever been to a strip joint ?"

No, I could not.  I spent many a dollar at establishment like that many 
many years ago.  You know what I learned?  The religious people were 
right - there's nothing in that scene but disappointment, frustration, 
and a huge financial drain.

I don't go proclaiming this far and wide, because it's not really that 
important - really and truly, it's none of anyone's business.  But, if I 
sit here and take the 5th *, my point will not be made.  All you or 
other observers would say is "ha - won't answer the question - what a 
hypocrite..."

* translation - refuse to self-incriminate - refers to the 5th Amendment 
to the Constitution

> When it comes to LIES - how do you figure this one, during the 
> Bush/Kerry campaign. Firstly the Bush 'minders' stir the proverbial over 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

Heck, it's been weeks since I watch Fox.  I guess you were watching when 
all the #42 handlers were *indicted* and *incarcerated* - even with 
Iran-Contra, I can't remember a single administration in recent history 
that has been fraught with such corruption.  From real estate 
irregularities, to suspicious deaths, to records that miraculously 
"appear", to convicted fund-raising law violators, to stolen FBI files 
and classified documents - #42's administration had no respect for the 
office, our laws, or the people they claimed to serve.  *That* is, to 
me, the biggest thing that was wrong with his administration.

You know what the comment was when #43 took over?  Someone was overheard 
saying "The adults are back."

#### ↳ Re: OT: US Presidents and Foreign Policy

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>

> James J. Gavan wrote:

What does 'his finger on the button' have in common with his having his

above was right and Donald was right, Bill should have given the BIG 
FINGER. (You enjoyed that one pissing yourself with laughing while your 
wife was trying to take in some TV program with some poor sod dying of 
cancer).

I'm not denying his culpability - but of independent mind I am much more 
upset at the Republican 'Hounds of the Baskervilles' salivating and 
doggedly (no pun intended), pursuing him over such a minor issue. Is 
there something in your Constitution that forbids a president having a 
'quickie' ? BIG MISTAKE - he should have neither said "Yes' or 'No'. He 
got poor advice - certainly none from Hilary I would suggest.

Much more important to me is the 'real' corruption of your democracy.

Joe McCarthy - way before your time and I had to google to find out his 
political affiliation. Started out as a Democrat during FDR days but 
became a Republican. First on google list, "He was a homosexual".  Many 
sites also highlight that he was a catholic, which I already knew, plus, 
which I also knew, he used to have a catholic priest in his entourage as 
a backup. Probably the most despicable event in US History - the way, 
through Senate hearings he tore into supposed 'commies' or some who 
definitely were sympathetic 'lefties'. That cruel beast destroyed so 
many lives in your arts/culture community, before he got his own cum 
uppance.

Tricky Dicky - difficult one for me to rate on your 'popularity' list. 
I'd give him very high marks for establishing communication with the 
Chinese using Kissinger. But Watergate - and I don't give a hoot about 
profanities or whatever picked up on tape recordings in the Oval Office.
Whether he signed-off or just acquiesced, the terrible intent of the 
subjugation of democracy in the US to suit one party's objectives.

Ollie North - an altar boy like me, but we obviously went off the altar 
in different directions. Be-ribboned at the Contra hearings spouting 
'Flag and Country' - side-stepped that his actions were totally illegal, 
according to your Constitution For his sins he became a US Senator.

Have I missed anybody in the 'corruption of democracy game' ? Funny that 
they are all Republicans.

Back to the 'finger on the trigger'. Ever given it any thought, I've 
never read up on him. The 'folksy' little Harry Truman, the former WW1 
major. Ever pondered the agonizing that he must have gone through before 
he authorized the bombings of Hiroshima or Nagasaki. Sure we all know 
the arguments about saving many millions of Allied lives - but it still 
leaves us very uncomfortable. I'm thinking Harry probably did agonize 
even though we won.

(On that one, (personal culpability) there was a Canadian General 
Daillaire - head of UN troops in Rwanda. Personally held himself 
responsible for the dreadful atrocities/slaughters. Suffered severe 
nervous depression etc. Still thinks he was the culprit. Nobody else 
holds him responsible. Thank goodness. Canada has just told him so by 
giving him an honorary award).

Back to Harry - Round # 2 - the new Emperor of Japan (Macarthur) got 
uppity - Harry fired him.

Harry the little guy had the moral fibre to follow through on unpleasant 
decisions. His decisions would be difficult for anybody operating from 
the Oval Office. Who can judge which of the occupants in our list would 
falter. Bill might have made exactly the same decisions as Harry.

I can just see the current guy, 'Nuke 'em ? OK, on to the next topic.

no, Lebber. OK guys help me".

>

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

James J. Gavan wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> FINGER.

Again, you're missing the point.  You'll note that I gave JFK high 
ratings in my list.  If he wants to run around on his wife, that's one 
thing.  But perjury is quite another matter.

> (You enjoyed that one pissing yourself with laughing while your 
> wife was trying to take in some TV program with some poor sod dying of 
> cancer).

??

> I'm not denying his culpability - but of independent mind I am much more 
> upset at the Republican 'Hounds of the Baskervilles' salivating and 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> got poor advice - certainly none from Hilary I would suggest.

The point was perjury, not having a quickie.  And yes, some of us think 
honesty is an important trait in the chief law enforcement officer of 
the land.

> Much more important to me is the 'real' corruption of your democracy.
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> uppance.

Joe McCarthy is one of the most unfairly maligned people in American 
history.  His job was to vet people for a particular type of clearance 
for White House positions.  He merely didn't recommend several people 
(who, with the fall of the USSR and documents obtained later, were 
*actually* Soviet spies).  He did not use character assassination, he 
simply detailed the record of the people he was considering.  Sometimes 
the truth is ugly.  Had more people in the administrations listened to 
Senator McCarthy, the Cold War would have been much, much shorter.

And, remember, *Senator* Joe McCarthy had nothing to do with the *House* 
Un-American Activities Council - who, among other things, was 
responsible for "outing" several more proved spies.  Not that anyone was 
listening.

> Tricky Dicky - difficult one for me to rate on your 'popularity' list. 
> I'd give him very high marks for establishing communication with the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> subjugation of democracy in the US to suit one party's objectives.

Sabotage and theft, like perjury, are also actions unbecoming the leader 
of our country.  He did the right thing in admitting his wrong and 
stepping down, unlike #42.

> Ollie North - an altar boy like me, but we obviously went off the altar 
> in different directions. Be-ribboned at the Contra hearings spouting 
> 'Flag and Country' - side-stepped that his actions were totally illegal, 
> according to your Constitution For his sins he became a US Senator.

I don't remember Ollie North ever being a Senator. ?  Iran-Contra was a 
blight on the end of the Reagan administration, but it basically stems 
from a foreign policy decision that ultimately worked out in this 
country's favor.

> Have I missed anybody in the 'corruption of democracy game' ? Funny that 
> they are all Republicans.

Oh sure, you've missed a lot of 'em.  You've picked three, and the first 
one is a hero, not an example of corruption.  What people today still 
refuse to admit is that Joseph McCarthy was *right*!  History has 
vindicated this man, and people still act like he was some kind of 
monster.  Have you ever read about the level of success that Soviet 
espionage had in our country around WWII?  How do you think they figured 
out how to make nuclear weapons?

> Back to the 'finger on the trigger'. Ever given it any thought, I've 
> never read up on him. The 'folksy' little Harry Truman, the former WW1 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> even though we won.

Anyone would agonize over that decision.  And, the proof is in the 
pudding - only once has that decision ever been made in the affirmative.

> Harry the little guy had the moral fibre to follow through on unpleasant 
> decisions. His decisions would be difficult for anybody operating from 
> the Oval Office. Who can judge which of the occupants in our list would 
> falter. Bill might have made exactly the same decisions as Harry.

Depends on how the polls were doing.  I can't think of a single thing he 
did that wouldn't have gotten at least 50% on an opinion poll.  That's 
not leadership - anyone can do that.

> I can just see the current guy, 'Nuke 'em ? OK, on to the next topic.

That's ludicrous.  Those of us who believe that death is a reasonable 
punishment for a government to mete out (either against its own 
criminals, or hostile nations) realizes that there is also an inherent 
responsibility with that power.  That's what makes our country different 
from a dictatorship.  And, given the fallout (both literal and 
political) from Hiroshima and Nagasaki, I doubt this country will ever 
use a large-scale nuclear weapon on another nation again.  One of the 
reasons we bombed Baghdad at night was to destroy the infrastructure 
while the people were not in the building.

> What's the best way to remember how to pronounce Leber, err, Leber-ron, 
> no, Lebber. OK guys help me".

Keep on - he's a dunce, he's an idiot, he's stupid - he's also 
responsible for many, many lives being saved in Iraq and Afghanistan, as 
well as bringing a stabilizing force to the Middle East.  He's said that 
he likes being misunderestimated.  Rather than realize that's what 
they're doing, liberals just make fun of him using the word.

>>> Then of course there's FDR, Ike, JFK - bugger me ! With the exception 
>>> of Ike they are friggin Democrats !
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Biblical meaning of the word.

Well, heck, add me to that list!  I just keep my knowledge limited to my 
wife.  :)

>> I don't go proclaiming this far and wide, because it's not really that 
>> important - really and truly, it's none of anyone's business.  But, if 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fifth or given them the BIG FINGER !

I could have - but would you feel the same way about me as you do since 
I answered it truthfully?

>> I'm not familiar with this.
> 
> I'm not pulling yur chain Daniel. That's exactly what I saw on TV

I don't doubt it - I just said I wasn't familiar with it.

>> You know what the comment was when #43 took over?  Someone was 
>> overheard saying "The adults are back."
> 
> Made by just over HALF the population ?

No, made by the people who work in Washington, and around the White House.

> Meanwhile only to-day my wife 
> phoned Syracuse NY, her pen-friend from school days that I haven't yet 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> We were surprised. Mention #43 to them and they just G-R-O-A-N.

That's their loss, really.  Being a man of principle means acting in 
accordance with those principles.  Voters like that - no surprises. 
Even if they may not agree with the decisions he makes, at least they 
know where he stands.

Even if the "We've had this coming for a long time" crowd had gained 
traction, we still would have gone after Afghanistan - and, given Iraq's 
repeated "big finger" to the UN, probably would have still gone after 
them too.

> It's OK this stuff - but time consuming isn't it ?

>>
>> Joe McCarthy - way before your time and I had to google to find out 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Senator McCarthy, the Cold War would have been much, much shorter.

Reaction ? Well at first, thought, "I'm speechless". Well not quite. 
Didn't google but 'web ferreted' giving me a condensed list and looked 
for McCarthy, hearings etc. Your boy sure gets lots of coverage.

I guess I must have inadvertently hit on a 'Red' or Democratic site - 
who knows. Check this one out, I'm sure there are LOTS more. Is this one 
factually out of sync with your beliefs about McCarthyism ?

http://webferret.search.com/click?wf,joseph+mccarthy,,www.spartacus.schoolnet.co.uk%2FUSAacheson.htm,,yahoodir

Have to chop it down 'cos you don't need that Web Ferret stuff up front. 
  Democratic site - perhaps not - look at that last bit 
'schoolnet.co.uk'  - guess this has to be some hangover 'lefty' or 
'commie' site based in Blighty.

Still the above does support your contention - starts way back about 
1938 and follows through 40's and 50's and half way into article that's 
when Dirty Joe shows up. Ike sys "Enough, is enough" and puts his hound 
dogs on McCarthy to get rid of him.

Bit of background because of my 'eternal youth' :-). Way back when the 
Nazis attacked Russia, Hollywood made a 'kindly' movie about Uncle Vanya 
- God knows what it was called. The kindly Comrade Doktor was played by 
Walter Houston. Similarly film director Erich von Stronheim (he also 
played Rommel once) was the villainous Nazi with a bald head. The enemy 
of mine enemy is my friend.

Postwar - when we woke up. Now another black and white movie, "House on 
92nd Street". Dum dee dah dah. The FBI springs into action to round up 
this nest of communist vipers.

Sure while you were searching them out, UK was also shocked to find they 
had the Cambridge Four, (Blount, Philby, Burgess and Maclean - Blount 
became the Queen's art treasurer, for want of a name), commies in senior 
positions in security. Check out BBC if you want to follow topic. We 
were all a bit slow.

Seems like there was a need for an outfit called 'Homeland Security' 
more than 60 years ago. By George you've got it - you did something right !

I did glance your list of Presidents as well as those from Skippy. I 
didn't let them register, so that if I do it, it will be my list. I did 
notice you had #43 with a 9 though. I wont mark him. What zero ? Nope - 
to try and be impartial, in all seriousness, I would have to wait until 
February 2008 when he leaves the White House. Then dependent upon 
answers to the following, then I think one can more fairly rate him :-

- What was the situation with the following when GWB left Office - 
Afghanistan, Iraq, Iran, Syria, N. Korea, Russia and specifically China.

I might be half-baked about Ollie becoming a senator - tried to google 
but didn't see anything to confirm or deny it, just that he is now a 
guest speaker, broadcaster etc.

I mentioned Harry T. and the BOMB. You are probably already aware, but I 
had no idea. March 9/10 was the 60th anniversary of when the USAAF 
bombed Tokyo. Short article I read said that raid caused more 
destruction and death, 100,000 (?), than Hiroshima and Nagasaki put 
together.

Really must get back to COBOL - uhh ? I'm so close on finishing what I 
want in my Treeview, but requires concentration - currently coding reads 
as three sets, in successive levels - Continent, Country and Cheese 
names. That approach allowed me initially to think the thing through as 
three entities, although they have a lot in common. Bit more thought and 
identifying things by object reference, should be able to turn those 
three separate sets into one class - get it to operate for three Levels 
then obviously it can do the same for 'n' Levels. Pass an object through 
linkage as lnk-Object and the compiler is smart enough to know which 
Level label I'm trying to add/change/delete.

Jimmy

```
LX-i wrote:
>
>> Sure while you were searching them out, UK was also shocked to find 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Was there anyone there who suspected, but wasn't listened to?

Only threw the paperback away about ten days ago. "Spy Catcher", fairly 
old now and don't remember author except he worked for MI5 or MI6 - I 
always get those two mixed up - somewhat like two branches of CIA - one 
domestic the other foreign/military.

That bloody English 'caste' system played a part. Not sure, but would 
guess they were all public school graduates who went on to university at 
Cambridge. Your average nice, conservative, Englishman. Not so. They had 
an affinity with the idealism of communism. That was their common 
meeting point as young men. (Very early 'Thirties I think).

Not sure of Blount who eventually finished up as an adviser to the Queen 
on art, but the other three, from memory, made a career in MI5/MI6. 
Meanwhile, as they moved up the ranks, there was somebody in the States, 
Appleyard, I think, one of Bill Stevenson's former OSS people 
(forerunner to CIA), who started to get whispers and passed them on to 
his UK contacts.

Get real vague here. The author of the book was a junior in intelligence 
- I'll call him 'A'. At some stage his supreme boss (forgotten name - so 
we will call him 'B'), put him on to ferreting out suspects. Just 
remembered "A's" dad was a well-known engineer or something with 
Marconi, giving "A" easy entrance into the spy game as a career, plus 
like his father, he had an electrical/electronic bent which he used for 
subsequent spy-catching - see below. He certainly established friendly 
contacts with FBI, CIA or whoever, but mainly he was on his own 
reporting to "B". As part of the exercise they started the then 
available wire-tappings, listening devices, looking out for commies. I 
think at one stage they had the Soviet embassy wired for sound.

I think his research, deliberations, (he was basically a one man team), 
led him in the direction of the Cambridge Four. Meanwhile in summary 
form he's reporting this to "B". Somebody tipped off Philby and he flew 
the coop. Subsequently Burgess and Mclean did too. All three I think 
finishing up in USSR. So our young detective is thinking to himself, 
"Who blue the whistle ?'.

By a series of deductions he came to the conclusion it had to be "B" - 
i.e. the Head of either MI5 or MI6. He paints a picture of the man, 
elegant and as smooth as silk, almost cobra like in his charm.  Whether 
there was a preliminary hearing to fathom "B" I don't know, but he 
subsequently retired honourably becoming Sir "B".

Our boy "A" still pursues it - getting interviews with "B" - he just 
deftly shrugs off the accusations.

"A" eventually mentally exhausted with the whole exercise retires and I 
think went sheep farming in Australia. Other than the reference to the 
Cambridge Four - nothing whatever conclusive about Sir "B". As you can 
well imagine, in the States, both FBI and CIA were less than thrilled 
with the British performance in this long-running bit of espionage.

The story above of course is how "A" writes it - is it true or slanted - 
who knows ?

It severely shook the USA's belief in MI5/MI6 - and I don't know whether 
the latter have made headway with their American friends.

Perhaps they have. Don't forget both CIA and MI5/MI6 thought they saw 
the same 'swallow' flying over Iraq :-)

Jimmy
```

```
LX-i wrote:
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yep. Sounds like the outlines of a move script doesn't it. Except this 
one happened. One, (Philby) or all three, (i.e. excluding Blunt) - did 
time overseas - and in Washington - OUCH ! Can't recollect numbers but 
it was Philby, (again I think), who was responsible for the death of say 
50, perhaps a 100 UK agents, and possibly some US agents, in Eastern Europe.

There was a young girl called Cora ...		No I'm kidding.

Trivia. There was an English actress, (here we go again - I think), 
called Cora Brown.  Not big time but she was known on the London stage 
and I recognised her. Philby was somewhat the ladies man, great charm, 
and although a homosexual, had an appeal to women, (from a local lady, 
they feel 'safe' with homosexuals). Cora Brown knew and associated with 
Philby. When he fled to Moscow she subsequently visited him there, 
possibly while she might have been visiting, acting in some Will 
Shakespeare touring thing for the Russkies.

The events of the visit were turned into a BBC/ITV drama shown on PBS. 
Alan Bates played Philby ...AND..... Cora Brown played the part of Cora 
Brown !

If you want a quick read - the following is not too long. Probably 
refutes everything I've written above :-)

http://www.crimelibrary.com/terrorists_spies/spies/cambridge/2.html?sect=23

Hell, googling on 'Spy Catcher" I even got the author - Mr. "A" = Peter 
Wright and Mr. "B" = Mr. Hollis :-

http://www.allreaders.com/Topics/info_16858.asp?BSID=14884376

One fact I recall about Peter Wright from the book, was he had an 
analytical mind. Early days of ADP = EDP = IT, but we had punched cards 
in UK as well as you folks in N. America. But a pretty crude way of 
doing analysis with card sorts. As I recall, he developed a manual DB of 
cards, cross-referring names to events. Not in five minutes, but as 
things became retentive in his mind, he started to match up his cards - 
that led eventually to the Cambridge Four - but as he writes, he was 
blocked from success, presumably by Hollis.

OK kid. Don't count your swallows before they have hatched :-)

Jimmy
```

## Thread: OT: Pleading the Fifth — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Pleading the Fifth

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T05:16:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bXuYd.649461$6l.339536@pd7tw2no>`

```
Daniel,

Thanks for the translation BTW - but didn't need it this time. It's been 
used so often since my adult days that even us 'foreigners' almost know 
it off by heart. Yep, we also know about Miranda too.

Still, come to losing VPs like Miller - now why would I remember some 
obscure loser. I can visualize Barry Goldwater - but would have to stop 
and give it some thought as to his exact time era.

Not verbatim, but let me have a go at the Fifth :-

Joseph (The Mower), Grappinelli :-

"Yers honor. Oi plead da Fifth on da grounds I moight incriminate 
moiself. Besoides which I could finish up swimmn' with da fishes".

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<bXuYd.649461$6l.339536@pd7tw2no>`

```
Unfortunately these days, people are pleading for the sixth.

JCE

James J. Gavan <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
>Daniel,
>
>Thanks for the translation BTW - but didn't need it this time. It's been 
>used so often since my adult days that even us 'foreigners' almost know 
>it off by heart. Yep, we also know about Miranda too.

Chalk it up to American Cultural Imperialism... I remember reading an 
article about social difficulties in French banlieus - impoverished 
suburbs as opposed to impoverished inner cities - which reported a scene 
of a lad being arrested.  Some of the onlookers yelled out 'His rights!  
You have to read him his rights!'... and one of the police said to the 
author 'These kids, they learn from American films... this is France, we 
do not have to read such 'rights'.'

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Pleading the Fifth

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T18:21:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<bXuYd.649461$6l.339536@pd7tw2no>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Daniel,
> 
> Thanks for the translation BTW - but didn't need it this time. It's been 
> used so often since my adult days that even us 'foreigners' almost know 
> it off by heart. Yep, we also know about Miranda too.

I never know.  DD has got me recognizing more of my colloquialisms...

> Joseph (The Mower), Grappinelli :-
> 
> "Yers honor. Oi plead da Fifth on da grounds I moight incriminate 
> moiself. Besoides which I could finish up swimmn' with da fishes".

heh...  I don't know if you all get Chappelle's Show up there - it's on 
Comedy Central here.  Anyway, he had this one skit where his character 
was supposed to go up before Congress and testify on something.  Not an 
accusatory testimony, just information gathering.  He started pleading 
the 5th for every question.

"I plead the 5th"
"I plead the fizz-if"
"one two three four FIFTH!" (screeched in a high voice)

I almost fell off the couch...  :)
```

## Thread: EXEC CMD - END-EXEC statement in IF Statement or EVALUATE STATEMENT — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### EXEC CMD - END-EXEC statement in IF Statement or EVALUATE STATEMENT

- **From:** apknight@gmail.com (apknight)
- **Date:** 2005-03-12T00:36:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95895e6d.0503120036.64bf1cdf@posting.google.com>`

```
follow text is correct?

1.
     IF  SQLCODE = ZERO                                           
         MOVE  VARA       TO  VARC    
         EXEC SQL INCLUDE 'SAMPLE.INC' END-EXEC.
         MOVE  VARA       TO  NBM
     END-IF.                                                      

2.
     IF  SQLCODE = ZERO                                           
         MOVE  VARA       TO  VARC    
         EXEC SQL INCLUDE 'SAMPLE.INC' END-EXEC
         MOVE  VARA       TO  SAMPLE
     END-IF.                                                      


3.
     IF  SQLCODE = ZERO                                           
         MOVE  VARA       TO  VARC    
         EXEC SQL SELECT A FROM B  END-EXEC
         MOVE  VARA       TO  VARC
     END-IF.                                                      

4.
     IF  SQLCODE = ZERO                                           
         MOVE  VARA       TO  VARC    
         EXEC SQL SELECT A FROM B END-EXEC.
         MOVE  VARA       TO  VARC
     END-IF.   


My question's point is "EXEC CMD  -- END-EXEC" statement must end wiht "."..
( all cobol compiler..case.)
```

- **References:** `<95895e6d.0503120036.64bf1cdf@posting.google.com>`

```

>follow text is correct?

…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>( all cobol compiler..case.)
